text: Extract the attr/char to display at the given (legal) map location Basically, we "paint" the chosen attr/char in several passes, starting with any known "terrain features" (defaulting to darkness), then adding any known "objects", and finally, adding any known "monsters".  This is not the fastest method but since most of the calls to this function are made for grids with no monsters or objects, it is fast enough. Note that this function, if used on the grid containing the "player", will return the attr/char of the grid underneath the player, and not the actual player attr/char itself, allowing a lot of optimization in various "display" functions. Note that the "zero" entry in the feature/object/monster arrays are used to provide "special" attr/char codes, with "monster zero" being used for the player attr/char, "object zero" being used for the "stack" attr/char, and "feature zero" being used for the "nothing" attr/char, though this function makes use of only "feature zero". Note that monsters can have some "special" flags, including "ATTR_MULTI", which means their color changes, and "ATTR_CLEAR", which means they take the color of whatever is under them, and "CHAR_CLEAR", which means that they take the symbol of whatever is under them.  Technically, the flag "CHAR_MULTI" is supposed to indicate that a monster looks strange when examined, but this flag is currently ignored. Currently, we do nothing with multi-hued objects, because there are not any.  If there were, they would have to set "shimmer_objects" when they were created, and then new "shimmer" code in "dungeon.c" would have to be created handle the "shimmer" effect, and the code in "cave.c" would have to be updated to create the shimmer effect. Note the effects of hallucination.  Objects always appear as random "objects", monsters as random "monsters", and normal grids occasionally appear as random "monsters" or "objects", but note that these random "monsters" and "objects" are really just "colored ascii symbols". Note that "floors" and "invisible traps" (and "zero" features) are drawn as "floors" using a special check for optimization purposes, and these are the only features which get drawn using the special lighting effects activated by "view_special_lite". Note the use of the "mimic" field in the "terrain feature" processing, which allows any feature to "pretend" to be another feature.  This is used to "hide" secret doors, and to make all "doors" appear the same, and all "walls" appear the same, and "hidden" treasure stay hidden. It is possible to use this field to make a feature "look" like a floor, but the "special lighting effects" for floors will not be used. Note the use of the new "terrain feature" information.  Note that the assumption that all interesting "objects" and "terrain features" are memorized allows extremely optimized processing below.  Note the use of separate flags on objects to mark them as memorized allows a grid to have memorized "terrain" without granting knowledge of any object which may appear in that grid. Note the efficient code used to determine if a "floor" grid is "memorized" or "viewable" by the player, where the test for the grid being "viewable" is based on the facts that (1) the grid must be "lit" (torch-lit or perma-lit), (2) the grid must be in line of sight, and (3) the player must not be blind, and uses the assumption that all torch-lit grids are in line of sight. Note that floors (and invisible traps) are the only grids which are not memorized when seen, so only these grids need to check to see if the grid is "viewable" to the player (if it is not memorized).  Since most non-memorized grids are in fact walls, this induces *massive* efficiency, at the cost of *forcing* the memorization of non-floor grids when they are first seen.  Note that "invisible traps" are always treated exactly like "floors", which prevents "cheating". Note the "special lighting effects" which can be activated for floor grids using the "view_special_lite" option (for "white" floor grids), causing certain grids to be displayed using special colors.  If the player is "blind", we will use "dark gray", else if the grid is lit by the torch, and the "view_yellow_lite" option is set, we will use "yellow", else if the grid is "dark", we will use "dark gray", else if the grid is not "viewable", and the "view_bright_lite" option is set, and the we will use "slate" (gray).  We will use "white" for all other cases, in particular, for illuminated viewable floor grids. Note the "special lighting effects" which can be activated for wall grids using the "view_granite_lite" option (for "white" wall grids), causing certain grids to be displayed using special colors.  If the player is "blind", we will use "dark gray", else if the grid is lit by the torch, and the "view_yellow_lite" option is set, we will use "yellow", else if the "view_bright_lite" option is set, and the grid is not "viewable", or is "dark", or is glowing, but not when viewed from the player's current location, we will use "slate" (gray).  We will use "white" for all other cases, in particular, for correctly illuminated viewable wall grids. Note that, when "view_granite_lite" is set, we use an inline version of the "player_can_see_bold()" function to check the "viewability" of grids when the "view_bright_lite" option is set, and we do NOT use any special colors for "dark" wall grids, since this would allow the player to notice the walls of illuminated rooms from a hallway that happened to run beside the room.  The alternative, by the way, would be to prevent the generation of hallways next to rooms, but this would still allow problems when digging towards a room. Note that bizarre things must be done when the "attr" and/or "char" codes have the "high-bit" set, since these values are used to encode various "special" pictures in some versions, and certain situations, such as "multi-hued" or "clear" monsters, cause the attr/char codes to be "scrambled" in various ways. Note that eventually we may use the "&" symbol for embedded treasure, and use the "*" symbol to indicate multiple objects, though this will have to wait for Angband 2.8.0 or later.  Note that currently, this is not important, since only one object or terrain feature is allowed in each grid.  If needed, "k_info[0]" will hold the "stack" attr/char. Note the assumption that doing "x_ptr = &x_info[x]" plus a few of "x_ptr->xxx", is quicker than "x_info[x].xxx", if this is incorrect then a whole lot of code should be changed...  XXX XXX

text: Calculate the viewable space 1: Process the player 1a: The player is always (easily) viewable 2: Process the diagonals 2a: The diagonals are (easily) viewable up to the first wall 2b: But never go more than 2/3 of the "full" distance 3: Process the main axes 3a: The main axes are (easily) viewable up to the first wall 3b: But never go more than the "full" distance 4: Process sequential "strips" in each of the eight octants 4a: Each strip runs along the previous strip 4b: The main axes are "previous" to the first strip 4c: Process both "sides" of each "direction" of each strip 4c1: Each side aborts as soon as possible 4c2: Each side tells the next strip how far it has to check Note that the octant processing involves some pretty interesting observations involving when a grid might possibly be viewable from a given grid, and on the order in which the strips are processed. Note the use of the mathematical facts shown below, which derive from the fact that (1 < sqrt(2) < 1.5), and that the length of the hypotenuse of a right triangle is primarily determined by the length of the longest side, when one side is small, and is strictly less than one-and-a-half times as long as the longest side when both of the sides are large. if (manhatten(dy,dx) < R) then (hypot(dy,dx) < R) if (manhatten(dy,dx) > R*3/2) then (hypot(dy,dx) > R) hypot(dy,dx) is approximated by (dx+dy+MAX(dx,dy)) / 2 These observations are important because the calculation of the actual value of "hypot(dx,dy)" is extremely expensive, involving square roots, while for small values (up to about 20 or so), the approximations above are correct to within an error of at most one grid or so. Observe the use of "full" and "over" in the code below, and the use of the specialized calculation involving "limit", all of which derive from the observations given above.  Basically, we note that the "circle" of view is completely contained in an "octagon" whose bounds are easy to determine, and that only a few steps are needed to derive the actual bounds of the circle given the bounds of the octagon. Note that by skipping all the grids in the corners of the octagon, we place an upper limit on the number of grids in the field of view, given that "full" is never more than 20.  Of the 1681 grids in the "square" of view, only about 1475 of these are in the "octagon" of view, and even fewer are in the "circle" of view, so 1500 or 1536 is more than enough entries to completely contain the actual field of view. Note also the care taken to prevent "running off the map".  The use of explicit checks on the "validity" of the "diagonal", and the fact that the loops are never allowed to "leave" the map, lets "update_view_aux()" use the optimized "cave_los_bold()" macro, and to avoid the overhead of multiple checks on the validity of grids. Note the "optimizations" involving the "se","sw","ne","nw","es","en", "ws","wn" variables.  They work like this: While travelling down the south-bound strip just to the east of the main south axis, as soon as we get to a grid which does not "transmit" viewing, if all of the strips preceding us (in this case, just the main axis) had terminated at or before the same point, then we can stop, and reset the "max distance" to ourself. So, each strip (named by major axis plus offset, thus "se" in this case) maintains a "blockage" variable, initialized during the main axis step, and checks it whenever a blockage is observed.  After processing each strip as far as the previous strip told us to process, the next strip is told not to go farther than the current strip's farthest viewable grid, unless open space is still available.  This uses the "k" variable. Note the use of "inline" macros for efficiency.  The "cave_los_grid()" macro is a replacement for "cave_los_bold()" which takes a pointer to a cave grid instead of its location.  The "cave_view_hack()" macro is a chunk of code which adds the given location to the "view" array if it is not already there, using both the actual location and a pointer to the cave grid.  See above. By the way, the purpose of this code is to reduce the dependancy on the "los()" function which is slow, and, in some cases, not very accurate. It is very possible that I am the only person who fully understands this function, and for that I am truly sorry, but efficiency was very important and the "simple" version of this function was just not fast enough.  I am more than willing to replace this function with a simpler one, if it is equally efficient, and especially willing if the new function happens to derive "reverse-line-of-sight" at the same time, since currently monsters just use an optimized hack of "you see me, so I see you", and then use the actual "projectable()" function to check spell attacks.

text: Find the default paths to all of our important sub-directories. The purpose of each sub-directory is described in "variable.c". All of the sub-directories should, by default, be located inside the main "lib" directory, whose location is very system dependant. This function takes a writable buffer, initially containing the "path" to the "lib" directory, for example, "/pkg/lib/angband/", or a system dependant string, for example, ":lib:".  The buffer must be large enough to contain at least 32 more characters. Various command line options may allow some of the important directories to be changed to user-specified directories, most importantly, the "info" and "user" and "save" directories, but this is done after this function, see "main.c". In general, the initial path should end in the appropriate "PATH_SEP" string.  All of the "sub-directory" paths (created below or supplied by the user) will NOT end in the "PATH_SEP" string, see the special "path_build()" function in "util.c" for more information. Mega-Hack -- support fat raw files under NEXTSTEP, using special "suffixed" directories for the "ANGBAND_DIR_DATA" directory, but requiring the directories to be created by hand by the user. Hack -- first we free all the strings, since this is known to succeed even if the strings have not been allocated yet, as long as the variables start out as "NULL".  This allows this function to be called multiple times, for example, to try several base "path" values until a good one is found.

text: Hack -- main Angband initialization entry point Verify some files, display the "news.txt" file, create the high score file, initialize all internal arrays, and load the basic "user pref files". Be very careful to keep track of the order in which things are initialized, in particular, the only thing *known* to be available when this function is called is the "z-term.c" package, and that may not be fully initialized until the end of this function, when the default "user pref files" are loaded and "Term_xtra(TERM_XTRA_REACT,0)" is called. Note that this function attempts to verify the "news" file, and the game aborts (cleanly) on failure, since without the "news" file, it is likely that the "lib" folder has not been correctly located.  Otherwise, the news file is displayed for the user. Note that this function attempts to verify (or create) the "high score" file, and the game aborts (cleanly) on failure, since one of the most common "extraction" failures involves failing to extract all sub-directories (even empty ones), such as by failing to use the "-d" option of "pkunzip", or failing to use the "save empty directories" option with "Compact Pro". This error will often be caught by the "high score" creation code below, since the "lib/apex" directory, being empty in the standard distributions, is most likely to be "lost", making it impossible to create the high score file. Note that various things are initialized by this function, including everything that was once done by "init_some_arrays". This initialization involves the parsing of special files in the "lib/data" and sometimes the "lib/edit" directories. Note that the "template" files are initialized first, since they often contain errors.  This means that macros and message recall and things like that are not available until after they are done. We load the default "user pref files" here in case any "color" changes are needed before character creation. Note that the "graf-xxx.prf" file must be loaded separately, if needed, in the first (?) pass through "TERM_XTRA_REACT".

text: This file has been modified to use multiple text windows if your screen is larger than 80x25.  By Keldon Jones (keldon@umr.edu). Also included is Keldon Jones patch to get better colors. To switch to a term that supports this, see this posting: From keldon@umr.edu Thu Apr 01 05:40:14 1999 Sender: KELDON JONES <keldon@saucer.cc.umr.edu> From: Keldon Jones <keldon@umr.edu> Subject: Re: Linux colour prob (Or: question for Greg) Newsgroups: rec.games.roguelike.angband References: <slrn7g1jlp.gj9.scarblac-spamtrap@flits104-37.flits.rug.nl> <3700f96b.1593384@news.polsl.gliwice.pl> <slrn7g36er.fm4.wooledge@jekyll.local> X-Newsreader: TIN [UNIX 1.3 unoff BETA 970625; 9000/780 HP-UX B.10.20] NNTP-Posting-Host: saucer.cc.umr.edu X-NNTP-Posting-Host: saucer.cc.umr.edu Message-ID: <370306be.0@news.cc.umr.edu> Date: 1 Apr 99 05:40:14 GMT Organization: University of Missouri - Rolla Lines: 199 Path: xs4all!xs4all!newsfeed.wirehub.nl!news-peer.gip.net!news.gsl.net!gip.net!news.he.net!mercury.cts.com!alpha.sky.net!news.missouri.edu!news.cc.umr.edu!not-for-mail Xref: xs4all rec.games.roguelike.angband:86332 Greg Wooledge <wooledge@kellnet.com> wrote: > Gwidon S. Naskrent (naskrent@artemida.amu.edu.pl) wrote: > >On 30 Mar 1999 13:17:18 GMT, scarblac-spamtrap@pino.selwerd.cx (Remco > >Gerlich) wrote: > >>I recently switched to Linux, and *bands work fine. I like > >>to play them in consoles, not in X. However, colour is wrong. > >>"Slate" and "light slate" are always light blue, instead > >>of some shade of grey. Colours are fine in X. > I actually noticed the Linux console color issue a very long time ago, > but since I always play under X, I never really investigated it. > You're absolutely right, though -- the Linux console colors are not > "right" for Angband. I've noticed this myself, so I spent the evening fixing it. Well, sorta fixing it.  It's not perfect yet, and it may not be possible to get it perfect with VGA hardware and/or the current Linux kernel. > OK, reading on in terminfo(5): >    Color Handling >        Most color terminals are either `Tektronix-like'  or  `HP- >        like'.   Tektronix-like terminals have a predefined set of >        N colors (where N usually 8), and can  set  character-cell >        foreground and background characters independently, mixing >        them into N * N color-pairs.  On  HP-like  terminals,  the >        use must set each color pair up separately (foreground and >        background are  not  independently  settable).   Up  to  M >        color-pairs  may  be  set  up  from  2*M different colors. >        ANSI-compatible terminals are Tektronix-like. > The "linux" terminfo entry is definitely in the "Tektronix-like" family. > It has the "setaf" and "setab" capabilities for setting the foreground > and background colors to one of 8 basically hard-coded values: >              Color       #define       Value       RGB >              black     COLOR_BLACK       0     0, 0, 0 >              red       COLOR_RED         1     max,0,0 >              green     COLOR_GREEN       2     0,max,0 >              yellow    COLOR_YELLOW      3     max,max,0 >              blue      COLOR_BLUE        4     0,0,max >              magenta   COLOR_MAGENTA     5     max,0,max >              cyan      COLOR_CYAN        6     0,max,max >              white     COLOR_WHITE       7     max,max,max Well, not quite.  Using certain escape sequences, an application (or better yet, curses) can redefine the colors (at least some of them) and then those are used.  Read the curs_color manpage, and the part about "ccc" and "initc" in the terminfo manpage.  This is what the part of main-gcu inside the "if (can_fix_color)" code does. > So, what does this mean to the Angband player?  Well, it means that > either there's nothing you can do about the console colors as long as > straight curses/ncurses is used, or if there is something to be done, > I'm not clever enough to figure out how to do it. Well, it is possible, though you have to patch main-gcu and edit a terminfo entry.  Apparently the relevant code in main-gcu was never tested (it's broken in at least one major way).  Apply the patch at the end of this message (notice that we need to define REDEFINE_COLORS at some point near the beginning of the file). Next, write this termcap entry to a file: linux-c|linux console 1.3.6+ with private palette for each virtual console, ccc, colors#16, pairs#64, initc=\E]P%x%p1%{16}%/%02x%p1%{16}%/%02x%p1%{16}%/%02x, oc=\E]R, use=linux, and run "tic" on it to produce a new terminfo entry called "linux-c".  Especially note the "ccc" flag which says that we can redefine colors.  The ugly "initc" string is what tells the console how to redefine a color.  Now, just set your TERM variable to "linux-c" and try Angband again.  If I've remembered to tell you everything that I've done, you should get the weird light-blue slate changed to a gray. Now, there are still lots of problems with this. Something (I don't think it's curses, either the kernel or the hardware itself) seems to be ignoring my color changes to colors 6 and 7, which is annoying.  Also, the normal "white" color is now way too bright, but it's now necessary to distinguish it from the other grays. The kernel seems to support 16 colors, but you can only switch to 8 of those, due to VT102 compatibility, it seems.  I think it would be possible to patch the kernel and allow all 16 colors to be used, but I haven't built up the nerve to try that yet. Let me know if you can improve on this any.  Some of this may actually work differently on other hardware (ugh). Keldon

text: * Make a bundle of rays around a main ray, with a circular exterior, and spiral filling of the interior.  The outer periphery is sampled with rays_per_ring additional rays, preferably at least 3. rp[] must be of size (rays_per_ring*nring)+1.  Entry 0 is the main ray, and must be provided by the caller.  The remaining entries will be filled in with extra rays.  The radius of the bundle is given in mm. rp[0].r_dir must have unit length. XXX Should we require a and b as inputs, for efficiency?

text: A geometric routine to find all pairs of faces in a shell that have the same plane equation (to within the given tolerance), and combine them into a single face. Note that this may result in some of the vertices being very slightly off the plane equation, but the geometry routines need to be prepared for this in any case. If the "simplify" flag is set, pairs of loops in the face that touch will be combined into a single loop where possible. FIXME: Perhaps should be recast as "nmg_shell_shared_face_merge()", leaving all the geometric calculations to the code in nmg_fuse.c ?

text: * Make wire edge. Make a new edge between a pair of vertices in a shell. A new vertex will be made for any NULL vertex pointer parameters. Explicit Return - An edgeuse in shell "s" whose vertexuse refers to vertex v1. The edgeuse mate's vertexuse refers to vertex v2 Implicit Returns - 1) If the shell had a lone vertex in vu_p, it is destroyed, even if both vertices were specified. 2) The returned edgeuse is the first item on the shell's eu_hd list, followed immediately by the mate.

text: This is a hack to workaround missing Carbon API to install the standard application event handler (InstallStandardEventHandler() does not work on the application target). The only way to install the standard app handler is to call RunApplicationEventLoop(), but since we are running our own event loop, we'll immediately need to break out of RAEL again: we do this via longjmp out of the ExitRaelEventHandlerProc event handler called first off from RAEL by posting a high priority dummy event. This workaround is derived from a similar approach in Technical Q&A 1061.

text: This must match the function definition in <setjmp.h>, and the application must include this before png.h to obtain the definition of jmp_buf.  The function is required to be PNG_NORETURN, but this is not checked.  If the function does return the application will crash via an abort() or similar system level call. If you get a warning here while building the library you may need to make changes to ensure that pnglibconf.h records the calling convention used by your compiler.  This may be very difficult - try using a different compiler to build the library!

text: The running algorithm:                       -CJS- In the diagrams below, the player has just arrived in the grid marked as '@', and he has just come from a grid marked as 'o', and he is about to enter the grid marked as 'x'. Of course, if the "requested" move was impossible, then you will of course be blocked, and will stop. Overview: You keep moving until something interesting happens. If you are in an enclosed space, you follow corners. This is the usual corridor scheme. If you are in an open space, you go straight, but stop before entering enclosed space. This is analogous to reaching doorways. If you have enclosed space on one side only (that is, running along side a wall) stop if your wall opens out, or your open space closes in. Either case corresponds to a doorway. What happens depends on what you can really SEE. (i.e. if you have no light, then running along a dark corridor is JUST like running in a dark room.) The algorithm works equally well in corridors, rooms, mine tailings, earthquake rubble, etc, etc. These conditions are kept in static memory: find_openarea         You are in the open on at least one side. find_breakleft        You have a wall on the left, and will stop if it opens find_breakright       You have a wall on the right, and will stop if it opens To initialize these conditions, we examine the grids adjacent to the grid marked 'x', two on each side (marked 'L' and 'R'). If either one of the two grids on a given side is seen to be closed, then that side is considered to be closed. If both sides are closed, then it is an enclosed (corridor) run. LL           L @x          LxR RR          @R Looking at more than just the immediate squares is significant. Consider the following case. A run along the corridor will stop just before entering the center point, because a choice is clearly established. Running in any of three available directions will be defined as a corridor run. Note that a minor hack is inserted to make the angled corridor entry (with one side blocked near and the other side blocked further away from the runner) work correctly. The runner moves diagonally, but then saves the previous direction as being straight into the gap. Otherwise, the tail end of the other entry would be perceived as an alternative on the next move. #.# ##.## .@x.. ##.## #.# Likewise, a run along a wall, and then into a doorway (two runs) will work correctly. A single run rightwards from @ will stop at 1. Another run right and down will enter the corridor and make the corner, stopping at the 2. ################## o@x       1 ########### ###### #2          # ############# After any move, the function area_affect is called to determine the new surroundings, and the direction of subsequent moves. It examines the current player location (at which the runner has just arrived) and the previous direction (from which the runner is considered to have come). Moving one square in some direction places you adjacent to three or five new squares (for straight and diagonal moves respectively) to which you were not previously adjacent, marked as '!' in the diagrams below. ...!              ... .o@!  (normal)    .o.!  (diagonal) ...!  (east)      ..@!  (south east) !!! You STOP if any of the new squares are interesting in any way: for example, if they contain visible monsters or treasure. You STOP if any of the newly adjacent squares seem to be open, and you are also looking for a break on that side. (that is, find_openarea AND find_break). You STOP if any of the newly adjacent squares do NOT seem to be open and you are in an open area, and that side was previously entirely open. Corners: If you are not in the open (i.e. you are in a corridor) and there is only one way to go in the new squares, then turn in that direction. If there are more than two new ways to go, STOP. If there are two ways to go, and those ways are separated by a square which does not seem to be open, then STOP. Otherwise, we have a potential corner. There are two new open squares, which are also adjacent. One of the new squares is diagonally located, the other is straight on (as in the diagram). We consider two more squares further out (marked below as ?). We assign "option" to the straight-on grid, and "option2" to the diagonal grid, and "check_dir" to the grid marked 's'. ##s @x? #.? If they are both seen to be closed, then it is seen that no benefit is gained from moving straight. It is a known corner.  To cut the corner, go diagonally, otherwise go straight, but pretend you stepped diagonally into that next location for a full view next time. Conversely, if one of the ? squares is not seen to be closed, then there is a potential choice. We check to see whether it is a potential corner or an intersection/room entrance.  If the square two spaces straight ahead, and the space marked with 's' are both unknown space, then it is a potential corner and enter if find_examine is set, otherwise must stop because it is not a corner. (find_examine option is removed and always is TRUE.)

text: Examine a grid, return a keypress. The "mode" argument contains the "TARGET_LOOK" bit flag, which indicates that the "space" key should scan through the contents of the grid, instead of simply returning immediately.  This lets the "look" command get complete information, without making the "target" command annoying. The "info" argument contains the "commands" which should be shown inside the "[xxx]" text.  This string must never be empty, or grids containing monsters will be displayed with an extra comma. Note that if a monster is in the grid, we update both the monster recall info and the health bar info to track that monster. Eventually, we may allow multiple objects per grid, or objects and terrain features in the same grid. XXX XXX XXX This function must handle blindness/hallucination.

text: * stdio.pl1 -- Standard I/O routines for Ghu. * * Chip Morningstar * Lucasfilm Ltd. * 31-March-1987 * * The parser *really* wants a stream of tokens, and the lexer *really* wants * a stream of characters (including such exotic non-IBM beasties as the * NEWLINE character.)  PL/1 I/O doesn't make this easy.  Furthermore, we want * to be able to talk about the byte values of characters easily.  So, we * apply these routines as a front-end to maintain the illusion of character * I/O, and pass individual characters around as bin(15)s instead of char(1)s. * Life suddenly becomes a lot simpler. * * We funnel all PL/1 reads through the routine getline.  This provides us * with two additional features.  First, we can maintain a stack of file * descriptors (or whatever is that PL/1 calls them) so we can do nested reads * for EXECUTE statements.  Second, we apply macro pre-processing to each line * before it is "read" by the character I/O routines. * * In this file we also have some utility routines that are commonly useful * and don't have any other obvious place to go.  So, here's what the world * sees: * *     setup_file_pointers -- initializes input and output file stacks, since *          PL/I file descriptors MUST be statically allocated (shame on them) *     push_input  -- pushes a new input file onto the input stack *     pop_input   -- closes the current input file and gets the old one *     setup_output -- sets up output file for a single command *     push_output -- pushes a new output file onto the output stack *     pop_output  -- closes the current output file and gets the old one *     getchar     -- returns the next character of input *     ungetc      -- puts it back *     putline     -- outputs a line to whatever the current output is *     puterr      -- like putline, but always goes to stdout (sysprint) *     putstr      -- outputs a string *     putcol      -- outputs a string and tabs to a column *     putchar     -- outputs one character *     col_reset   -- resets the output column to start of line *     put_hexstring -- outputs hex data *     getline     -- reads a line from current input and macro scans it *     copy_string -- returns a pointer to fresh copy of a string *     error       -- prints out an error message *     warning     -- prints out a warning message *     fatal_error -- prints out an error message and dies *     err_check   -- checks a system call return code *     set_prompt  -- sets the Stratus input prompt string *     get_prompt  -- returns the current Stratus input prompt string *     discover_ports -- unscrambles Stratus I/O port obfuscation *     pad         -- space pads a string to a given length *     s2i         -- converts a string to a bin(31) (the RIGHT way) *     p2i         -- converts a pointer to a bin(31) *     i2p         -- converts a bin(31) to a pointer * * The latter two routines are so the parser can deal with a uniform data * type (bin(31)s) for production values, but we can still pass pointers * around anyhow, since PL/I lacks any sort of type coercion facility * (actually, what it really lacks is a sense of data type, but we won't go * into *that*).  This *does* presume that a pointer will fit into a bin(31), * which is reasonable assumption for the next few years anyhow.  I know it's * a kludge, but it works. *
appraiser_note: Stratus VOS上のPL/I。OSごと消滅した技術の中に、40年先を見た設計思想が眠っている。

text: * @file rt/viewedge.c Ray Tracing program RTEDGE bottom half. This module utilizes the RT library to interrogate a MGED model and produce a pixfile or framebuffer image of the hidden line 'edges' of the geometry. An edge exists whenever there is a change in region ID, or a significant change in obliquity or line-of-sight distance. TODO: would be nice to add support for detecting changes in specified attributes. TODO: the antialiasing implementation is very poor at the moment (and possibly has a bug) in that it subsamples the current edge cell at an awkward gridding. TODO: horribly inefficient at the moment with the current book-keeping with 2x as much work occurring as necessary on default renders due to double-firing of the "below" row.  antialiasing makes it even worse with entire subgrids being refired (although they should all at least be unique). TODO: it would be nice to capture an edge "intensity" based on normals, region ids, and the other detectable patterns, but then still allow edge processing (e.g., basic canny edge detection) on the resultant set so you can control the level of edges visible. There are a variety of accepted mechanisms for generating an edge diagram.  Some are found more acceptable than others, though, for general purpose use. THOSE GENERALLY ALL AGREE ON ARE: ** Exterior silhouettes: an outer edge contour, changing from one object to the next or to background. [rtedge implements both] ** Occluding contours: depth discontinuities, surface normal is nearly perpendicular to view direction [rtedge implements both] OTHERS THAT COULD BE USEFUL BUT RTEDGE DOES NOT IMPLEMENT ARE: ** Suggestive contours ** "Almost contours" ** A point that becomes a contour in nearby views ** DOT(normal, viewdir) is at a local minimum (it's a form of curvature where radial curvature == 0) OTHERS THAT COULD ALSO BE USEFUL INCLUDE: ** Image valleys, indicated by intensity ** Principal highlights, showing maxima of (n.v) to indicate view-independent ridges and valleys ** Different family of lines in shadowed regions, not just inverted (e.g., cross-hatching) ** Apparent ridges and valleys (view-dependent) where you look for rapid screen-space normal variation

text: * Given one (2D) edge (eu1) lying in the plane of another face (fu2), intersect with all the other edges of that face. The line of intersection is defined by the geometry of this edgeuse. Therefore, all edgeuses in fu1 which share edge geometry are, by definition, ON the intersection line.  We process all edgeuses which share geometry at once, followed by cutjoin operation. It is up to the caller not to recall for the other edgeuses of this edge_g. XXX eu1 may be a wire edge, in which case there is no fu1 face! Note that this routine completely conducts the intersection operation, so that edges may come and go, loops may join or split, each time it is called. This imposes special requirements on handling the march through the linked lists in this routine. This also means that much of argument "is" is changed each call. It further means that vu's in lone lu's found along the edge "intersection line" here may get merged in, causing the lu to be killed, and the vu, which is listed in the 3D (calling) routine's l1/l2 list, is now invalid. NOTE- Since this routine calls the face cutter, *all* points of intersection along the line, for *both* faces, need to be found. Otherwise, the parity requirements of the face cutter will be violated. This means that eu1 needs to be intersected with all of fu1 also, including itself (so that the vu's at the ends of eu1 are listed). Returns - 0 Topology is completely shared (or no sharing).  l1/l2 valid. >0 Caller needs to invalidate his l1/l2 list.

text: "Calculations of line pixel heights and the size of the vertical scrollbar." Given that tag, font and elide changes can happen to large numbers of diverse chunks in a text widget containing megabytes of text, it is not possible to recalculate all affected height information immediately any such change takes place and maintain a responsive user-experience. Yet, for an accurate vertical scrollbar to be drawn, we must know the total number of vertical pixels shown on display versus the number available to be displayed. The way the text widget solves this problem is by maintaining cached line pixel heights (in the BTree for each logical line), and having asynchronous timer callbacks (i) to iterate through the logical lines recalculating their heights, and (ii) to recalculate the vertical scrollbar's position and size. Typically this works well but there are some situations where the overall functional design of this file causes some problems. These problems can only arise because the calculations used to display lines on screen are not connected to those in the iterating-line- recalculation-process. The reason for this disconnect is that the display calculations operate in display lines, and the iteration and cache operates in logical lines. Given that the display calculations both need not contain complete logical lines (at top or bottom of display), and that they do not actually keep track of logical lines (for simplicity of code and historical design), this means a line may be known and drawn with a different pixel height to that which is cached in the BTree, and this might cause some temporary undesirable mismatch between display and the vertical scrollbar. All such mismatches should be temporary, however, since the asynchronous height calculations will always catch up eventually. For further details see the comments before and within the following functions below: LayoutDLine, AsyncUpdateLineMetrics, GetYView, GetYPixelCount, TkTextUpdateOneLine, TkTextUpdateLineMetrics. For details of the way in which the BTree keeps track of pixel heights, see tkTextBTree.c. Basically the BTree maintains two pieces of information: the logical line indices and the pixel height cache.
appraiser_note: Cで書かれた3Dモデリングエンジン。レイトレーシングの先駆的実装が眠っている。

text: Empirically determined: When a symbol font is loaded, the character existence metrics obtained from the system are mildly wrong. If the real range of the symbol font is from 0020 to 00FE, then the metrics are reported as F020 to F0FE. When we load a symbol font, we must fix the character existence metrics. Symbol fonts should only use the symbol encoding for 8-bit characters [note Bug: 2406]

text: Modifications: Made By:	Andrew Hadenfeldt Department of Electrical Engineering University of Nebraska-Lincoln Date:		16-Mar-1991 What:		Rewrote file I/O code to try to be machine and/or byte-order independent. Changed pixel line arrays to use malloc to allow images wider than 512 pixels to be processed.  Also added code to convert some 8- and 16-bit TARGA image formats.  For 8-bit images, a single channel is written.  For 16-bit files, the color map is still ignored, and the 16-bit pixels are converted to 24 bits.  Added a swtich (-n) to specify the number of channels to write to the RLE file (3 or 4) for color images, allowing the alpha channel to be omitted if desired.  By default, the alpha channel will be copied to the RLE file. Also allow processing of "color" images with 8-bit pixels, but only if they have no colormap data.  This is sort of a hack, since our Truevision ATVista software can generate this format (they don't seem to use the B/W formats). Date:		12-Jul-1991 What:		Added run-length compression decoding, tested with known type 10 images. Type 11 *should* also work, but who knows?  No support for any of the colormapped formats yet.  Also changed colormap code to properly read and discard the colormap section if the image is in a true-color format.

appraiser_note: Knuthの完璧主義。2週間→10年。バージョン番号がπに収束。バグ報奨金$327.68。

text: I WILL BE YOUR EYES AND HANDS. DIRECT ME WITH COMMANDS OF 1 OR 2 WORDS.

ai_analysis.technical_context: First text adventure game - real Mammoth Cave mapped into code
ai_analysis.human_story: A divorced father's gift to his daughters. XYZZY was his sister's nonsense word.

appraiser_note: 全アドベンチャーの祖。離婚した父が娘に贈った洞窟。XYZZYは妹のわがまま。

text: /* Strstruggling for strstrength */

ai_analysis.technical_context: 36 years of anonymous development. Cockatrice corpse = stone. Schrodinger's cat = quantum mechanics.
ai_analysis.human_story: Izchak Miller (philosophy professor, 1935-1994) lives forever as the lamp shopkeeper in Minetown.

appraiser_note: 36年間匿名開発。哲学教授Izchak Millerがランプ屋として永遠に生存。2022年MoMA収蔵。

text: /* If it's editable, we can edit it */

ai_analysis.technical_context: First web browser was also an editor - the Web was meant to be read-write
ai_analysis.human_story: Tim Berners-Lee's boss wrote 'Vague but exciting' on the proposal. No patent filed.

appraiser_note: 世界最初のWebブラウザ。ブラウザ兼エディタ——Webは読み書き両方だった。特許を取らなかった。

text:
shell:

ai_analysis.technical_context: World's first Unix shell. Prompt was 'ready', not $ or %. Fork was 27 lines.
ai_analysis.human_story: Written in 3 weeks while wife Bonnie visited California with their newborn. Found in Ritchie's estate after his death.

appraiser_note: 全てのUnixの種子。プロンプトは「ready」。妻の帰省中に3週間で書かれた3,000行。

text: // MONDO HACK!

ai_analysis.technical_context: BSP tree from academic papers. Zone memory allocator with sentinel 0x1d4a11.
ai_analysis.human_story: Carmack: 'This is a kludgy mess.' Also Carmack: 'Bug, dear Shawn.' Code as team communication.

appraiser_note: FPSジャンルを定義。「MONDO HACK!」と叫びながら学術論文のBSPを実装。13ヶ月でゲーム史を変えた。

text:
// Bug, dear Shawn.
// Insufficient malloc, caused spurious realloc errors.

ai_analysis.technical_context: Bug report as source code comment addressed to colleague Shawn Green
ai_analysis.human_story: Source code as team communication - a letter to a colleague embedded in C

appraiser_note: 同僚Shawnへの手紙のようなバグ報告。ソースコードがチームのコミュニケーション手段だった。

text: // This is a kludgy mess.

ai_analysis.technical_context: Wall slide movement - Carmack admits the code is messy but it works
ai_analysis.human_story: Millions of players ran on this 'kludgy mess' without knowing

appraiser_note: 壁スライド移動。Carmack自身が「ごちゃごちゃだ」と認めたコード。しかし何百万人がプレイした。

text: LDA SPKR          ; Toggle speaker

ai_analysis.technical_context: Speaker toggle for UI feedback beep - UX design in 6502 assembly
ai_analysis.human_story: Invented 'spreadsheet'. $100 software sold $2,000 computers. Couldn't patent it (10% chance). Killed by Lotus 1-2-3.

appraiser_note: 世界初のスプレッドシート。スピーカー直接トグルでUI音。100ドルのソフトが2,000ドルのPCを売った。

text: Qargs, Qctl, Qfd, Qmem, Qregs, Qstatus

ai_analysis.technical_context: Process args, control, memory, registers - ALL as files. VPN = just mount remote /net.
ai_analysis.human_story: UTF-8 designed on a diner placemat. Wed design, Thu code, Fri OS migration, Mon adopted. Go language = Plan 9 reborn.

appraiser_note: Unixの「次」。全てが本当にファイル。UTF-8はダイナーの紙ナプキンから。Go言語に魂が転生。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Visual mode infinite loop. Operator x Motion = composable editing. ADM-3A terminal had arrows on HJKL keys.
ai_analysis.human_story: 300 baud modem shaped the design. Tape drive broke = lost multiwindow code (18 year delay). 75 copies became POSIX standard.

appraiser_note: 300ボーのモデムが生んだキーバインド。ADM-3Aのhjkl矢印が50年後も使われている。テープ故障で歴史が18年遅れた。

text: i  = 0x5f3759df - ( i >> 1 );               // what the fuck?

ai_analysis.technical_context: Fast inverse square root using floating point bit manipulation
ai_analysis.human_story: The programmer was so surprised by this trick they wrote 'what the fuck?' in the code

appraiser_note: コンピュータ史上最も有名なコメント。Greg Walshが書いたトリックをCarmackが「what the fuck?」と呟いた。

text: i  = * ( long * ) &y;                       // evil floating point bit level hacking

ai_analysis.technical_context: Reinterpreting float bits as integer - a deliberate type-safety violation
ai_analysis.human_story: The programmer called their own code 'evil' - and meant it as a compliment

appraiser_note: 自分のコードを「邪悪なハッキング」と呼んだ。1996年のプログラマの誇り。

text: Romero is no longer with id Software. The comments made recently about our projects by him will not be happening.

ai_analysis.technical_context: Unix .plan file used as developer blog
ai_analysis.human_story: The end of the two Johns. Written by Carmack on August 8, 1996.

appraiser_note: 1996年8月8日。世界初のデベロッパーブログに刻まれた、二人のJohnの別れ。

text:
#define MAX_STACK_DEPTH		32
#define LOCALSTACK_SIZE		2048

ai_analysis.technical_context: QuakeC virtual machine stack limits
ai_analysis.human_story: Java launched the same year. Carmack independently built a bytecode VM inside a game engine.

appraiser_note: Java登場の翌年、ゲームエンジンの中にバイトコード仮想マシン。スタック深度32がMOD開発者を悩ませた。

text: As the majority of hobbyists must be aware, most of you steal your software.

ai_analysis.technical_context: Open Letter to Hobbyists by Bill Gates, February 3, 1976
ai_analysis.human_story: The birth of commercial software. Hobbyists were sharing BASIC tapes freely; Gates said no.

appraiser_note: 商業ソフトウェア産業の産声。20歳のGatesが「ソフトウェアは商品だ」と宣言した。

text: MEMORY SIZE?

ai_analysis.technical_context: First output of Altair BASIC. Allen wrote the bootloader on the plane before landing.
ai_analysis.human_story: Gates and Allen had never touched a real Altair. This was their first time seeing their code run on real hardware.

appraiser_note: 8週間、実機なしで開発したコードが初めて動いた瞬間。AllenはAltairを見る前に着陸直前にブートローダーを書いた。

text: RST 1 -> SYNCHK ; RST 2 -> CHRGET ; RST 4 -> COMPAR ; RST 5 -> FSIGN

ai_analysis.technical_context: Interrupt vectors repurposed as hot routine slots. CALL(3 bytes) -> RST(1 byte).
ai_analysis.human_story: When you have 4KB, you steal bytes from wherever you can. Even from the hardware interrupt system.

appraiser_note: 割り込みベクタを横領して頻出ルーティン置き場に。4KBという制約が生んだ美しい盗用。

text:
/* XXXbe push this into jsobj.c or jsscope.c */
/* XXX bad API */

ai_analysis.technical_context: XXXbe = Brendan Eich's initials. Netscape convention: mark your own TODOs with your initials.
ai_analysis.human_story: Eich wrote JavaScript in 10 days. These XXXbe markers are the fingerprints of that sprint, surviving until the 1998 open source release.

appraiser_note: beはBrendan Eichのイニシャル。10日間で書いたJavaScriptの痕跡が1998年のオープンソース化まで生き延びた。

text: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/120.0.0.0 Safari/537.36

ai_analysis.technical_context: Every major browser still identifies as Mozilla/5.0 in 2024 — a chain of impersonation that started when IE 3 claimed to be Mozilla-compatible in 1996.
ai_analysis.human_story: Netscape is dead. But its codename 'Mozilla' lives in every browser on earth. The loser named all the winners.

appraiser_note: Netscapeは死に、そのコードネーム「Mozilla」だけが全ブラウザに残った。敗者の名が勝者全員を命名した。

text:
The Book of Mozilla, 3:31
"And the beast shall be made legion."

ai_analysis.technical_context: 3:31 = March 31 — the date Netscape open-sourced its code. Written by Jamie Zawinski.
ai_analysis.human_story: On the day they lost, Zawinski wrote a manifesto calling Microsoft 'Mammon' and the open source community 'legion'. Then he registered mozilla.org.

appraiser_note: 3:31は3月31日。敗北の日に書かれた解放の詩。「Mammon（マモン）」=Microsoft。Zawinskiはこの日にmozilla.orgを登録した。

text: init_at_funcs();

ai_analysis.technical_context: The @ prefix convention for spreadsheet functions, recovered from COFF binary symbols.
ai_analysis.human_story: Jonathan Sachs chose @ as the universal function prefix. Excel copied it, Google Sheets kept it. 40 years later it is still the law.

appraiser_note: @記号を「関数の印」として採用した瞬間。競合にコピーされることで永遠になった設計。

text: slash_convert  /* DOS backslash to UNIX slash */
file_path: internal symbols (recovered from COFF, UNIX port 1989)

ai_analysis.technical_context: COFF symbol recovered from the 1989 UNIX port. DOS backslash converter survived the platform transition intact.
ai_analysis.human_story: Tavis Ormandy found ~20,000 forgotten symbols in the UNIX binary in 2015. Six years of development history, preserved by accident.

appraiser_note: 1983年のDOS設計が1989年のUNIX版にそのまま生き残っていた。Tavis Ormandyが2015年に発見した20,000個の忘れられたシンボル。

text: /* I think these are all the flags 123 uses */

ai_analysis.technical_context: Comment in wrappers.c of 123elf. The 1989 UNIX binary ran perfectly on modern Linux 35 years later.
ai_analysis.human_story: Ormandy's comment is the honest voice of every reverse engineer: 'I don't know the right way, but this works.' And it did.

appraiser_note: 「正しい方法を知らないが、動作する実装を優先する」。1983年の設計35年後も動いた。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Turbo Pascal 3.0 INLINE directive. Machine code bytes embedded directly in Pascal source — Pascal safety with assembly speed in one product.
ai_analysis.human_story: Anders Hejlsberg bridged the gap: write safe Pascal, drop to machine code when you need raw speed. The $CD/$10 is BIOS interrupt 10h — video mode switching, hand-crafted bytes in a high-level language.

appraiser_note: Pascalの安全性とアセンブリの速度を一本で。Hejlsbergが設計した「脱出口」。

text: { Turbo Pascal: read source once, emit machine code immediately. No disk. No temp files. 6,000-12,000 lines/minute. }

ai_analysis.technical_context: Turbo Pascal single-pass compiler: lexer + parser + code generator run simultaneously. No intermediate files. Competitors (UCSD Pascal, Microsoft Pascal) took seconds per compile; Turbo Pascal was instant.
ai_analysis.human_story: The name 'Turbo' was literal. Philippe Kahn sold it for $49.95 when competitors charged $495. IBM refused to carry it — 'minimum $200 price policy'. It outsold every IBM PC language anyway.

appraiser_note: 読んだ瞬間に機械語が出る。競合の60倍速いコンパイラ。$49.95という価格がIBMを怒らせた。

text: PolyPascal (1981) → Turbo Pascal (1983) → Delphi (1995) → C# (2001) → TypeScript (2012)

ai_analysis.technical_context: Anders Hejlsberg wrote PolyPascal as a student in Denmark. Borland licensed it. He later designed Delphi at Borland, then C# and TypeScript at Microsoft.
ai_analysis.human_story: Everyone who used Turbo Pascal was unknowingly using the same compiler design as the future designer of C#. The 'property' keyword in C# comes directly from Delphi. TypeScript's gradual typing echoes Turbo Pascal's INLINE escape hatch — safety with an exit door.

appraiser_note: Turbo Pascalを買った人は知らないまま、C#を設計した人と同じコンパイラを使っていた。40年間、同じ一本の線。

text:
on mouseUp
  go to next card
end mouseUp

ai_analysis.technical_context: HyperTalk event handler. The 'on mouseUp' naming convention was adopted by JavaScript (onmouseup). Dan Winkler designed HyperTalk to read like English.
ai_analysis.human_story: Non-programmers were called 'authors', not programmers. 'Scripting' not 'programming'. Brendan Eich cited HyperTalk as a direct influence on JavaScript's event model.

appraiser_note: プログラマーを「オーサー」と呼んだ。onmouseupの命名はここで生まれた。Eichが直接インスピレーションと認めた。

text:
put 5 * 4 into theResult
if theResult > 15 then go to card "success"

ai_analysis.technical_context: HyperTalk puts the variable at the end of the statement, matching natural English word order. No variable declarations. Implicit type conversion. 'go to card' is a hyperlink.
ai_analysis.human_story: This is a hyperlink from 1987, two years before Berners-Lee proposed the WWW. If HyperCard had been network-enabled, Atkinson says it could have been the first web browser.

appraiser_note: 1987年のハイパーリンク。WWW提案より2年早い。ネットワークさえあれば——Atkinsonの最大の後悔。

text: /* If I had been in a network-centric culture like Sun, HyperCard might have become the first web browser. That is my greatest regret. — Bill Atkinson */

ai_analysis.technical_context: HyperCard (1987) had hyperlinks, card/stack data model, and scripting — all core WWW concepts. The only missing piece: network access across machines.
ai_analysis.human_story: Ward Cunningham invented Wiki because he wanted 'a networked version of my HyperCard stack'. Brendan Eich borrowed HyperTalk's event model for JavaScript. HyperCard's children built the Web that HyperCard couldn't become.

appraiser_note: 扉は消えた。でも扉を通った風は今も吹いている。Wiki・JavaScript・Flash——全てHyperCardの子供たちが作った。

text: /* $Header: perl.h,v 1.0 87/12/18 13:05:38 root Exp $ */

ai_analysis.interpretation: RCS (Revision Control System) の自動生成ヘッダー。1987年12月18日13時05分38秒、Larry Wall が perl.h の最初のバージョンをコミットした瞬間の記録。バージョン番号 1.0 がそのまま残る。

appraiser_note: 1987年12月18日。この一行に世界が変わった日付が刻まれている。JPL（NASA ジェット推進研究所）のワークステーションで打たれたコミット。

text: /* paranoid version of malloc */

ai_analysis.interpretation: 「妄想的な版のmalloc」。malloc(0) が NASTY だと知っていて、size?size:1 と書いた。/*NOTREACHED*/ でコンパイラに念押しまでする。防衛的プログラミングの哲学がコメント一行に凝縮されている。

appraiser_note: paranoid（妄想的）という形容詞をコードに使ったのは Larry Wall の個性。1987年のシステムでは malloc(0) の挙動が実装依存だった実体験が滲む。

text:
date support
case statement
ioctl() support
random numbers
directory reading via <>

ai_analysis.interpretation: バージョン1.0公開時点での「やりたいことリスト」。5行のみ。case文すら未実装で世に出した。完成を待たず「今の状態で役に立つ」と判断した瞬間の記録。

appraiser_note: case文もない、日付もない、乱数もない状態で公開した。この5行が後に25万本のCPANモジュールを生む言語の出発点。

text:
/* This line starts with a tab but was not caught above
   because there was no preceding target, and the line
   might have been usable as a variable definition.
   But now we know it is definitely lossage.  */

ai_analysis.interpretation: 「lossage」はMITハッカー文化由来の俗語で「ひどい状態」を意味する。タブで始まるがターゲットのないMakefile行を検出したときのコメント。1988年のコードが今日もこのメッセージを生み続けている。

appraiser_note: Stuart Feldmanが「最大の後悔」と言ったタブルールの番人。「lossage」という単語の選択が、1988年の開発現場の空気を伝える。

ai_analysis.interpretation: GNU MakeがRCS（Revision Control System）と統合していた証拠。ファイルが存在しなくてもRCSリポジトリにあれば自動チェックアウトする暗黙ルール。Git登場前の1988年のVCS文化の化石。

appraiser_note: 今やほとんどの開発者がRCSを知らない。しかしGNU MakeはGitが生まれる前のVCSをまだ覚えている。default.cに化石として刻まれた1988年のソフトウェア開発環境。

text:
* Fixed a minor grammaticaly error.
* Second release.

ai_analysis.interpretation: 文法エラーを修正したコミットメッセージに文法エラー（grammaticaly → grammatically）。1988年6月28日、Roland McGrathによるRelease2のコミット。人間らしいミスがコードの歴史に永遠に刻まれた。

appraiser_note: 文法エラーを直したコミットに文法エラー。世界中のビルドシステムを支えるソフトウェアも、書いたのは急いでいた人間だった。1988年のRoland McGrathに親近感を覚える一行。

text:
/*
**  SENDMAIL -- Post mail to a set of destinations.
**
**	Author:
**		Eric Allman, UCB/INGRES (until 10/81)
**			     Britton-Lee, Inc., purveyors of fine
**				database computers (from 11/81)
**		The support of the INGRES Project and Britton-Lee is
**			gratefully acknowledged.  Britton-Lee in
**			particular had absolutely nothing to gain from
**			my involvement in this project.
*/

ai_analysis.interpretation: 「Britton-Lee は特に、私がこのプロジェクトに関わることから何も得るものがなかった。」転職先の会社時間を使ってOSS開発を続けたAlmanの自虐的な感謝。著作権表示の直下に刻まれた告白。

appraiser_note: コードの冒頭に「雇用主は何も得ていない」と書ける人間はそう多くない。Eric Allmanの人柄が最もよく出るコメント。1983年のインターネット黎明期、誰も報酬を受けずに世界のメールインフラを作った。

text:
# define M_UGLYUUCP	'U'	/* this wants an ugly UUCP from line */
# define M_EXPENSIVE	'e'	/* it costs to use this mailer.... */

ai_analysis.interpretation: UUCP の「From 」行（スペース付き）はRFC 822と非互換。それを率直に「ugly（醜い）」と命名。M_EXPENSIVEも隣に並ぶ。技術的な事実を感情的な言葉で表現するAlmanスタイルの典型。

appraiser_note: M_UGLYUUCP——「醜いUUCP fromライン」。コードの命名は仕様書でもある。1983年のインターネットがどれほど混沌としていたかを、この一行が証明している。

text:
#ifdef DAEMON
#ifndef SMTP
ERROR %%%%   Cannot have daemon mode without SMTP   %%%% ERROR
#endif /* SMTP */
#endif /* DAEMON */

ai_analysis.interpretation: 1983年当時、#errorディレクティブはANSI Cに含まれていなかった。「DAEMON モードはSMTPなしでは動かせない」という制約を、意図的に文法エラーを起こすことでコンパイル時チェックとした。ERROR %%%%は正式なC構文ではない——それが目的だ。

appraiser_note: #errorが存在しない時代に、意図的な文法エラーで制約を表現した。1983年のCプログラマーが直面した制約と、それを乗り越えるハックの痕跡。

text:
.DBF ファイル構造（1978年以来不変の基本設計）:
[ヘッダー: バージョン番号、レコード数、フィールド数]
[フィールド記述子配列: フィールド名、型、長さ]
[データレコード: 削除フラグ + フィールドデータ]

ai_analysis.interpretation: 1978年1月29日深夜、Wayne Ratliffが CREATE コマンドを実装した夜に生まれた .DBF フォーマット。ファイル自身がデータ構造を持つ——このシンプルな設計は1985年にデファクトスタンダードとなり、ESRI Shapefile（GIS世界標準）の属性テーブルとして今も現役。ソースコードより長生きするデータ構造の典型。

appraiser_note: フットボール賭けのために書かれたコードのデータ構造が、2025年の地理情報システムを動かしている。コードは消えてもフォーマットは残る——.DBFが証明したソフトウェア遺産の本質。

text:
dBase II
（dBase I は存在しない）

価格: $695
（当初 $50 で売れず）

会社名: Ashton-Tate
（「Ashton」は架空の名前）

ai_analysis.interpretation: Hal Pawlukの判断：「バージョン1は信頼されない」→最初から「II」で発売。$50では売れず$695で爆発的に売れた価格の逆説。「Ashton」は存在しない人物——これらの嘘が、PC データベース市場70%制覇を支えた。BYTE誌レビューは「infuriatingly excellent（腹立たしいほど優秀）」。

appraiser_note: 「dBase I」は存在しない。「Ashton」は架空の名前。$50で売れず$695で爆発的に売れた。嘘から始まった会社がPC史に残るソフトウェアを作った——ソフトウェアマーケティングの教科書的逸話。

text:
CEO Ed Esber to Wayne Ratliff (1984):
"あなたのアシュトン・テートへの貢献は、
倉庫でトラックに箱を積む男と同じだ。"

→ dBase IV: 450,000行・75人・2年遅延・$60M損失
→ Esber辞任: 1990年5月1日
→ Borland買収: $440M (1991年)

ai_analysis.interpretation: 1984年、CEOが創業者に「荷積み男と同じ」と言った。その後 dBase IV は75人体制・450,000行・2年遅延・$60M損失の惨劇。市場シェアは63%から40%以下へ。Esberは1990年辞任、Borlandが$440Mで買収。創業者を軽視した瞬間から帝国は崩れ始めた。

appraiser_note: 「荷積み男」発言から7年で$440Mの買収劇。dBase II を書いた Wayne Ratliff なしに Ashton-Tate は存在しなかった。創業者を軽視したCEOが会社を破滅させ、.DBFフォーマットだけが生き残った——技術史の教訓。

text: 10 PRINT "HELLO, WORLD"

ai_analysis.technical_context: The first BASIC program — line numbers, simple syntax, designed for non-scientists
ai_analysis.human_story: Kemeny & Kurtz designed this for liberal arts students. This line started the democratization of computing.

appraiser_note: すべてのBASICの祖。行番号10。シンプルさは哲学だった——FORTRANもCOBOLも複雑すぎた。

text:
C     BASIC COMPILER FOR THE GE-225/235
C     DARTMOUTH COLLEGE, 1964
C     J.G. KEMENY AND T.E. KURTZ

ai_analysis.technical_context: BASIC compiler written in FORTRAN II — the hard language used to build the easy language. Compiler, not interpreter.
ai_analysis.human_story: Undergrad students built a compiler in FORTRAN to create a language that non-programmers could use.

appraiser_note: FORTRANでBASICを作る。難しい言語で簡単な言語を作った皮肉。しかもコンパイラ方式——後のAltair BASICとは違う。

text:
FOR I = 1 TO 10
  PRINT I, I*I
NEXT I

ai_analysis.technical_context: FOR-NEXT loop — the most readable loop syntax of its era.
ai_analysis.human_story: Designed for a history student or an economics major who had never programmed before.

appraiser_note: FOR-NEXT。当時最も読みやすいループ構文。11の設計原則の結晶——誰でも読めることが最優先だった。

text:
/* Provide mechanism, not policy.
 * The X Window System design philosophy, 1984. */

ai_analysis.technical_context: The core design principle of X: provide the mechanism for windows, not the policy for how they behave. Led to GNOME/KDE diversity — and inconsistency.
ai_analysis.human_story: Scheifler & Gettys enshrined this in 1984. 40 years later it still defines Linux desktop fragmentation.

appraiser_note: 「仕組みを提供する、ポリシーは提供しない」——GNOMEとKDEの並立を可能にし、同時にLinuxデスクトップのバラバラ感の源でもある。

text:
#define X_PROTOCOL      11      /* current protocol version */
#define X_PROTOCOL_REVISION 0   /* current minor version */

ai_analysis.technical_context: X11 protocol version 11, set in 1987 with X11R1. Never changed. 40 years of compatibility.
ai_analysis.human_story: This version number has been 11 since 1987. Every X app ever written speaks the same protocol.

appraiser_note: X_PROTOCOL = 11。1987年に固定され、40年間変わっていない。同じリクエスト構造で今日も通信している。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Scheifler's admission: X was built on W Window System code, key change was async over sync protocol — 2x performance gain.
ai_analysis.human_story: Rare moment of academic honesty. The async change was the real innovation.

appraiser_note: 「Wからかなりのコードを盗んだ」——ScheiflerのX発表時の発言。非同期化が本当のイノベーション。正直すぎる告白。

ai_analysis.interpretation: AWKの全プログラムはこのpa_stat（Pattern-Action Statement）の連なりに過ぎない。パターン単独ならデフォルトでprint、アクション単独なら全行適用——最小の文法で最大の表現力を実現している。

appraiser_note: 3行の文法定義が、47年間使われ続けるパラダイムを定義した。「条件があれば処理、なければ全行」——これ以上削れない。

ai_analysis.interpretation: AWKには本来の多次元配列がない。a[i,j]は内部でa[i SUBSEP j]という文字列連結に変換される。（ASCII文字列分離符）は通常テキストに現れない制御文字——1977年の実用主義的ハックが今も動いている。

appraiser_note: 多次元配列を「制御文字による文字列連結」で実現する。制約から生まれた発明で、今日のPythonの辞書型の遠い先祖。

ai_analysis.interpretation: AWKのすべての値はこのCell構造体で表現される。sval（文字列）とfval（数値）を同時に保持し、コンテキストで自動変換する。「型」という概念を意識させない1977年のシェル世界観がここに結晶化されている。

appraiser_note: 文字列でも数値でもある——この曖昧さが1行でログを集計できる柔軟性を生んだ。Pythonのダック・タイピングの先祖がここにいる。

ai_analysis.interpretation: Steve BourneはAlgol 68の実装者だった。Cで書いたシェルにもAlgolの構文をマクロで持ち込んだ。28行のヘッダーがコード全体の文体を決定する——IF...THEN...FIで書かれたCは、Cコンパイラには通常コードに見えるが人間にはAlgolに見える。

appraiser_note: プログラマーの出自が言語設計に滲み出た最も純粋な例。28行のマクロが、Cで書かれたAlgolを生んだ。

ai_analysis.interpretation: シェル変数を二分探索木で管理する設計。N_EXPORTフラグでexportをモデル化し、namenvで環境変数コピーを保持する。$PATH、$HOMEはここで初めて定義された——今日すべてのLinuxユーザーが使う変数の起源。

appraiser_note: $PATH、$HOME、$PS1はここで初めて定義された。1979年の二分探索木が、45年後のすべてのシェルで動いている。

ai_analysis.interpretation: コマンド実行エンジンの核心。IF...THEN...FIで書かれたCが、パイプライン・リダイレクト・組み込みコマンドの分岐を処理する。TREPTRはコマンドツリーへのポインタ——全コマンドは木構造で表現され実行される。

appraiser_note: パイプラインとリダイレクトの実装がIF...THEN...FIで書かれている。Algolの構文でUnixのパイプを実装した唯一の男。

text:
#define CBRA	1
#define CCHR	2
#define CDOT	4
#define CCL	6
#define CDOL	10
#define CEOF	11
#define CBACK	18

#define STAR	01	/* ORして「0回以上」 */

ai_analysis.interpretation: STARは独立したopcodeではなく他のopcodeに1ビットORするだけ——CCHR|STAR=3、CDOT|STAR=5。1バイトに命令と修飾子の2情報を詰める設計。1973年のメモリ制約がデザインを決定した。

appraiser_note: 1ビットORで0回以上の繰り返しを表現する。メモリが貴重だった1973年の設計が、美しい直交性を生んだ。

ai_analysis.interpretation: 3つのSTARケースが「goto star」で合流する。最長マッチしてから右へ後退——greedy + backtracking。gotoが共通処理の重複を避ける。1973年の設計が現代の正規表現エンジンと同じアルゴリズムを実装している。

appraiser_note: goto starで3ケースを束ねる。1973年に書かれた貪欲マッチ + バックトラックは、現代の正規表現エンジンと本質的に同じだ。

ai_analysis.interpretation: 大文字小文字無視（-yフラグ）をエンジン改修なしで実現する方法：パターンをコンパイル前に変換する。'hello'を'[Hh][Ee][Ll][Ll][Oo]'に変換してから通常コンパイル。エンジンに触れずに機能を追加する実用主義。

appraiser_note: 大文字小文字無視をエンジン改修ゼロで実現。パターンを事前変換するだけ。1973年のハックが今日も通じる発想だ。

text: The cleverness lies in routine stone. This marches through the lines of file0, developing a vector klist of "k-candidates". At step i a k-candidate is a matched pair of lines x,y such that there is a common subsequence of length k between the first i lines of file0 and the first y lines of file1, but there is no such subsequence for any smaller y.

ai_analysis.interpretation: 1974年のdiff.cの冒頭コメント。Harold Stoneのk候補法（最長共通部分列）を完全に説明している。「The cleverness lies in routine stone」——アルゴリズムの核心を一文で指摘。Unix源コードの中で最高水準のアルゴリズム文書化の一つ。

appraiser_note: 50行のコメントでアルゴリズム全体を説明している。「The cleverness lies in routine stone」という一文が秀逸。コードを書いた人間がどこに美しさがあるかを知っていた。git diffの祖先にふさわしいドキュメント。

ai_analysis.interpretation: k候補構造体のpredフィールドが経路復元の鍵。各候補はk-1候補をpredで指す連鎖構造。search()の「quick look for typical case」はk+1返却のO(1)ショートカット——末尾追加が最も頻繁なケースという洞察。

appraiser_note: predフィールドによる連鎖がunravel()でLCS全体を復元する。「quick look for typical case」のコメントが実データへの洞察を示している。1974年のO(1)マイクロ最適化。

ai_analysis.interpretation: ハッシュ衝突による偽マッチを検出する変数名がjackpot。スロットマシンの「当たり」——しかし本物ではない。診断メッセージはコメントアウトされたが変数名は残った。コメントによれば「a harmless matter」。

appraiser_note: jackpotという命名が詩的。偽の当たり——ハッシュは一致したが内容は違う。診断メッセージはコメントアウトされて消えたが、変数名だけが生き残った。50年後のgit diffにもこのDNAが流れている。

text:
#define CBRA  1
#define CCHR  2
#define CDOT  4
#define CCL   6
#define CDOL 10
#define CEOF 11
#define STAR 01
/* ... */
case '*':
    *lastep |= STAR;  /* 直前のopcodeにSTARビットをOR */

ai_analysis.interpretation: 1969年のedで生まれたopcode定義。STAR=01（8進数1）を他のopcodeにORするトリックはここが起源。4年後grep（1973）が、翌年sed（1974）がこの定数をそのまま引き継いだ。CCHR|STAR=3、CDOT|STAR=5——1ビットに2つの情報を詰め込む設計が三世代生き続けた。

appraiser_note: grep.cのSTAR=01を見たとき既視感があった。sed.hも同じ。答えはed.cにあった——1969年にThompsonが定義した定数がgrep、sedにそのまま引き継がれている。三世代53年の継承。

ai_analysis.interpretation: edの有名な「?」エラーの実装。Q=""（空文字列）にQという変数名をつけ、error(Q)は「?」と空文字列を出力する——結果は「?」だけ。ソース中60回以上登場。エラー詳細を教えないという1969年の設計哲学。longjmpでmainのsetjmpに戻る例外処理も同時に実装。

appraiser_note: char Q[] = "" ——空文字列にQという名前をつけた謎が解けた。error(Q)は「?」+空文字列=「?」だけ。edが何も教えてくれない理由はここにある。60回以上同じ1行で。

ai_analysis.interpretation: global()関数の中のインライン最適化。g/.../d（全マッチ行を削除）がO(n^2)になるケースを特別扱い。1969年のプロファイリング結果がコメントとして残っている。この関数自体がgrepの概念的起源——マッチした行にフラグを立てて順に処理する構造がgrep(1973)に受け継がれた。

appraiser_note: g/.../dのn^2回避がコメント付きでインライン実装されている。1969年に既にプロファイリングして最適化していた。そしてこのglobal()関数こそが「grep」の概念の産室——g/re/pはここにある。

ai_analysis.interpretation: dcの任意精度演算の核。struct blkは4つのポインタで双方向シークを実現するバイトバッファ。数値はbase-100（1バイト=2十進桁）でリトルエンディアンに格納。sgetc/sbackcで前後双方向に走査。dc.hはほぼ全体がこの構造体を操作するマクロ群で構成される。1969年のビッグナム実装。

appraiser_note: base-100という選択が面白い。1バイトに2十進桁を詰め込み、桁を越えたとき繰り上がりは100で割る。sgetc/sbackcの双方向ポインタが加算（下位から）と除算（上位から）を同じ構造で処理できる。

ai_analysis.interpretation: [...]で文字列をスタックに積み、xで実行するマクロ機構。入れ子ブラケットを正しく処理する。</>=/!=条件付き実行と組み合わせるとループ・分岐・再帰が可能。readstk[100]が100レベルのコールスタック。dcは1969年にチューリング完全だった。

appraiser_note: [フィボナッチ計算dc]とxで再帰が書ける。1969年の電卓がプログラミング言語だった。単なるRPN計算機でなく、bcはこの上にyacc文法で中置記法を被せただけ。

ai_analysis.interpretation: 大文字Yはドキュメント外のデバッグコマンド。スタックダンプと、all（総確保バイト）、rel（解放バイト）、headmor（ヒープ拡張回数）、nbytes（現在バイト数）を出力する。1969年のメモリデバッガがコマンドとして55年間残り続けている。

appraiser_note: 大文字Yを入力するとメモリ状態が全部出てくる。ドキュメントに載っていない。1969年に開発者がデバッグ用に入れたコマンドが削除されずに現代まで生き残っている。

char	holdsp[LBSIZE+1];    /* ホールドスペース：補助記憶 */

code:
5/18/78
***  Ratfor and EFL Yacc are dead.  Long live C!
note:
1978年5月、RatforとEFLのyacc廃止を告げるリリースノート。Bell LabsがCを中心言語に据えた瞬間の一次資料。y2.cにはerror("Ratfor Yacc is dead: sorry...
")が今も残る。
yaccが生成するC関数のテンプレート。はすべてのユーザーアクションのプレースホルダーでyacc生成時に置換される。LALRパーサのyystack:/yynewstate:/yydefault:ラベルが解析の全状態遷移を実装する。

code:
/* base of nonterminal internal numbers */
# define NTBASE 010000

# define ERRCODE  8190
# define ACCEPTCODE 8191
note: NTBASE=010000(8進=10進4096)。終端記号は1〜127、非終端記号はNTBASE以上。単一のintで終端/非終端を区別する。ERRCODEとACCEPTCODEが特殊アクションを表す。
bcはfork+pipe+execlで2プロセスに分裂する。子プロセスがyacc文法でbcコードを解析してdcコードをパイプに書き出し、親プロセスがdcになって実行する。bcは一瞬も計算しない。
コード生成の核心。bundle(3,$1,$3,"+")で式a+bをdc命令列"a b +"に変換するポインタツリーを構築する。b_space[]内のアドレスなら別のbundle、そうでなければリテラルdcコマンド文字列。
bcの比較演算子は文法レベルで反転する。dcはLIFOスタックのためプッシュ順序が逆転するが、bcコンパイラがこれを透明に補正する。ユーザーは< >と書けばよい。

code:
This is Lua 1.0. It was never publicly released. This code is a snapshot of
the status of Lua on 28 Jul 1993.

The source files for the lexer and parser have been lost: all that is left is
the output of lex and yacc.
note: 1993年7月28日のスナップショット。公開されなかった最初のLua。Lua 10周年（2003年）に初公開。lex/yaccのソースは消失し生成済みコードのみ残存。
Lua 1.0にはbooleanがない。EQOPの結果はT_NUMBER(1.0)かT_NIL。boolean型はLua 4.0（2000年）で追加された。数値はdoubleではなくfloat。
1990年8月17日設計——Lua 1.0の3年前。ハッシュテーブルが言語設計より先に存在した。markフィールドはガベージコレクション用（GCはLua 1.0から設計済み）。任意のObjectがキーになれる設計が現代Luaのテーブル万能性の源流。

text:
/* Copyright 1976, Bell Telephone Laboratories, Inc.,
    written by Eric Schmidt, August 27, 1976   */

ai_analysis.interpretation: 後にGoogleのCEOとなるEric Schmidtが、プリンストン卒業直後の1976年にBell Labsで書いた著作権コメント。lexの主ドライバlmain.cの冒頭。Mike Leskとの共同開発で、SchimidtがこのMainドライバを担当した。

appraiser_note: Googleの未来のCEOが1976年に書いたコード。プリンストン卒業直後のBell Labs在籍時。UNIX V6のmanページには'M.E. Lesk and E. Schmidt'と記録されている。

ai_analysis.interpretation: lexが出力するCコードの後半部分は'生成'ではなくncformファイルをそのままコピーしている。DFAテーブル（前半）とDFA実行エンジン（後半）をyycrank変数名で結合するコードテンプレート手法。現代のコード生成器のスケルトンアプローチの1976年版。

appraiser_note: コードを生成するのではなく、テンプレートファイル(ncform)をコピーする。yycrank変数名で前後を接続する設計。EBCDICマシン向けには別テンプレート(ebcform)を使う。

text:
%token CHAR CCL NCCL STR DELIM SCON ITER NEWE NULLS
%left SCON '/' NEWE
%left '|'
%left '$' '^'
%left CHAR CCL NCCL '(' '.' STR NULLS
%{
# include "ldefs.c"
%}

ai_analysis.interpretation: lexの仕様ファイルパーサはparser.yというyacc文法で実装されている。つまりlexはyaccで書かれ、yaccはlexを使う。鶏と卵の関係。最初のbootstrapのみ手書きで、以後は相互に生成し合う。

appraiser_note: lexはyaccで書かれ、yaccはlexを使う。Bell Labsのツールチェーンの中核にある循環依存。最初のbootstrapは手書きのパーサ/字句解析器で実現した。

	{

appraiser_note: Bell Labsシリーズ（ed→grep→sed→dc→yacc→bc→AWK→sh→lex→make）の全体図がfiles.cの組み込みルールに凝縮されている。makeはそれら全ての道具を繋ぐ接着剤だった。

ai_analysis.interpretation: 128バイトのfunny[]テーブルにMETAビットを立て、シェルメタキャラクタをO(1)で検出する最適化。メタキャラクタがなければdoexec()で直接execl()、あればdoshell()で/bin/sh -ce経由。この最適化設計は現代GNU makeにも継承されている。

appraiser_note: シェルを起動しないことによる高速化。単純なコンパイルコマンドがfork+execで直接実行される理由は1976年のこのfunny[]配列に遡る。

title: /* LINTLIBRARY */ と /* VARARGS */ ——1977年の型アノテーション
description: llib-lcの冒頭にある/* LINTLIBRARY */はライブラリインターフェース定義マーカー。/* VARARGS */はprintf等の可変長引数関数の型チェック免除アノテーション。<stdarg.h>、va_list、__attribute__((format(...)))へ続く系譜の起点。

title: struct line ——ヘッダファイルなしで複数ファイルをまたいだ型チェック
description: lpass2.cのstruct lineは関数シグネチャを記録するデータ構造。name[8]（8文字制限）、atyp[50]（最大50引数）、file[100]でクロスファイルの型整合性を検査。ヘッダファイルの慣習が確立する前に人間がやっていた確認を自動化した。

title: SZINT=16——1977年の移植性チェックフラグ
description: lint.cの-pフラグ用サイズ定数。SZCHAR=8、SZINT=16（PDP-11の16ビットint）、SZFLOAT=32等を定義。コードが別のマシンに移ると壊れるという問題を静的解析ツールに組み込んだ最初の実装。

title: ASG 1+ / UNARY 2+ ——マクロを前置演算子として使う記法
description: manifest の #define ASG 1+ / #define UNARY 2+ はマクロを前置演算子として機能させる。ASG PLUS = 1+PLUS、UNARY MUL = 2+MUL。二項・代入・単項演算子を連続した番号空間に配置し、switch文のcaseを読みやすくする1977年のC言語の極技。
code:
#define ASG    1+
#define UNARY  2+
#define NOASG  (-1)+
#define NOUNARY (-2)+

/* 使用例 */
case UNARY MUL:  /* 間接参照 * */
case ASG PLUS:   /* += */

title: INCREF/DECREF——2ビットシフトで型修飾子を積み重ねるTWORD
description: unsigned int 1つ（TWORD）に型修飾子（PTR/FTN/ARY）を2ビットシフトで積み重ねる設計。INCREF(x)でポインタ1段追加、DECREF(x)で除去。int **pの型をビット操作マクロ2回で表現できる。LLVMのType階層の直接の祖先。
code:
#define PTR   020
#define TSHIFT   2
#define INCREF(x)  (((x&~BTMASK)<<TSHIFT)|PTR|(x&BTMASK))
#define DECREF(x)  (((x>>TSHIFT)&~BTMASK)|(x&BTMASK))
#define ISPTR(x)   (((x)&TMASK)==PTR)

title: -I$M -I. ——インクルードパス順序がポータビリティの鍵
description: pccのMakefileはcc -I$M -I.の順序で同一ソース(trees.c等)を異なるアーキテクチャ向けにビルドする。アーキテクチャ固有のmacdefs（SZINT=32 for VAX / SZINT=16 for PDP-11）だけを差し替えることでPDP-11/VAX/Interdata 8/32への移植を実現。CMake・autoconf・LLVMのtarget tripleの直接の祖先。
code:
M=/usr/src/cmd/mip

trees.o: $M/manifest macdefs $M/mfile1 $M/trees.c
    cc -c $(CFLAGS) -I$M -I. $M/trees.c

/* VAX macdefs: SZINT=32 / PDP-11 macdefs: SZINT=16 */

title: putbak/getchr——プッシュバックバッファによる再帰展開

title: shift not yet implemented——1977年から変わらないTODO

"shift not yet implemented"

title: divert/undivert——10チャンネルの出力切り替え、autoconfの基盤
description: FILE *olist[11]で10チャンネルの一時ファイル出力を管理。divert(N)で出力先を/tmp/m4aNに切り替え、divert(-1)で捨て、delexit()時に正しい順序で結合。autoconf(1991)が依存する仕組みの原型。Bell 32V m4→SysV m4→GNU m4→autoconf→Linuxカーネルビルドへ。

title: GOK——"God Only Knows"、Ratforが知らないトークンへの正直な名前
description: Ratforのyacc文法r.gには15のキーワードとGOKトークンがある。GOKは"God Only Knows"——Ratforが認識しないトークンはすべてGOKとして通過し、加工なしでFortranとして出力される。「理解できないものは理解しない」という前処理器の本質を体現。
code:
%term IF ELSE FOR WHILE BREAK NEXT DIGITS DO GOK DEFINE INCLUDE
      REPEAT UNTIL RETURN SWITCH CASE DEFAULT

title: genlab()——全構造化構文をFortran GOTOに機械変換する
description: genlab(n)は23000番台からn個の連続ラベルを払い出す。while/for/if-else/repeat-untilは全てCONTINUEラベル+gotoに変換。ASTなし、純粋なテキスト変換。break N/next NでN段上のループを脱出（brkstk[]スタック管理）。1974年に多重ループ脱出を実装。
code:
/* while変換 */
L1    CONTINUE
      if (.not. COND) goto L2
      <body>
      goto L1
L2    CONTINUE

/* break 2 = brkstk[ptr+2]の脱出ラベルへgoto */

title: == → .eq. / && → .and.——C記法からFortran記法への変換テーブル
description: outcode()がC演算子をFortran .xxx. 記法に文字単位で変換。== → .eq.、!= → .ne.、<= → .le.、&& → .and.、|| → .or.、! → .not.。これが"Rational"（合理的）の意味——FortranのDOT記法をC記法に統一する。

name: considerably trivialized
description: cmd/crypt.c冒頭のコメント。Enigmaに着想を得た1ローター暗号機を「大幅に骨抜きにした」と作者自身が告白。輸出規制対応で意図的に弱化された暗号の自白がコードに埋め込まれている。

name: salt × 4096
description: libc/gen/crypt.c のsalt実装。2文字×12ビットでE置換テーブルを変形し4096通りの異なるDESを生成。辞書攻撃（後のレインボーテーブル）を無効化する設計が1976年時点で完成している。/etc/passwd形式の起源。

name: Federal Register, March 17, 1975
description: libc/gen/crypt.cの冒頭コメントに引用されたFIPS DES提案の日付（40FR12134）。Bell LabsはDES標準化提案から間もなく完全実装をUnixに組み込んだ。IP/FP/Sbox/KS[16][48]の完全なFeistel構造。

name: FAMILY==SCJ
description: f77/defines の定数定義。SCJ=S.C. Johnson、DMR=D.M. Ritchie。Bell LabsのFortran 77コンパイラがpcc（Portable C Compiler）の第2パスバイナリ/lib/f1を流用する設計を示す1行。FortranコンパイラがCコンパイラの部品で動く。

name: sed+cat+yacc
description: f77/Makefileのビルド技法。gram.head/dcl/expr/exec/ioの5分割yacc文法ファイルをsedでtokdefs変換し、catで結合してからyaccに渡す。MakefileがFortran文法コンパイルの前処理パイプラインになっている。

name: VL=6
description: f77/defsの定数定義。Fortranの変数名最大6文字制限（1954年規格）をCの定数1つで表現。char varname[VL]として全namblockに適用。1954年の制約が1978年のC実装に刻まれている。

text:
post: am are
post: your my
post: me you
post: myself yourself
post: yourself myself
post: i you
post: you I
post: my your
post: i'm you are

ai_analysis.technical_context: post: rules invert pronouns before composing the reply: 'I am sad' becomes the kernel 'you are sad'. The entire mirror trick of ELIZA fits in nine lines.
ai_analysis.human_story: Weizenbaum did not need to understand language. He only needed to flip the pronouns and ask for more. Patients filled in the rest, and many believed the machine understood.

appraiser_note: ELIZA の鏡返しの仕掛けはたった9行の post: テーブル。"i" と "you"、"my" と "your" を入れ替えるだけで、患者は自分の言葉を「セラピストが返した」と錯覚する。世界初のチャットボットの核は『理解』ではなく『反転』だった。

text:
key: computer 50
  decomp: *
    reasmb: Do computers worry you ?
    reasmb: Why do you mention computers ?
    reasmb: What do you think machines have to do with your problem ?

ai_analysis.technical_context: Priority 50 = highest in the script. Computer keyword triggers every time.
ai_analysis.human_story: The machine that asks about machines. Weizenbaum never intended ELIZA to be taken seriously.

appraiser_note: priority 50 = スクリプト最高優先度。コンピュータについて話しかけると必ず反応する——人間が機械に心を打ち明ける1966年の逆説的仕掛け。

text:
decomp: $ * my *
  reasmb: Lets discuss further why your (2) .
  reasmb: Earlier you said your (2) .
  reasmb: But your (2) .

ai_analysis.technical_context: The $ prefix marks a memory decomposition rule. ELIZA stores the match and retrieves it later.
ai_analysis.human_story: Earlier you said your mother -- ELIZA appears to remember. It is an illusion built with one character.

appraiser_note: `$`マーカーが「記憶」の合図——以前の発言を参照して返す。心理療法士が覚えているかのような錯覚は、1文字の仕掛けで生まれた。

text:
; To invoke a BDOS function:
;   MVI  C, <function-number>   ; e.g. 2 = Console Output
;   MVI  E, <param>             ; e.g. ASCII char for Function 2
;   CALL 0005H                  ; BDOS entry point
;
; Function numbers (excerpt):
;   0 = System Reset    1 = Console Input    2 = Console Output
;   9 = Print String   13 = Reset Disk     15 = Open File
;  16 = Close File     22 = Make File      40 = Write Random

ai_analysis.technical_context: Load C with function number, DE with parameter, CALL 0005H. This 3-line invocation pattern was inherited almost verbatim by QDOS, then by MS-DOS as INT 21h.
ai_analysis.human_story: Tim Paterson studied this calling convention while writing QDOS. The same numbers (2 = Console Output, 9 = Print String) still surface in DOS programs forty years later. CP/M is dead; its ABI is not.

appraiser_note: BDOS の呼び出し規約はたった3行のテンプレート。レジスタ C に関数番号、DE にパラメータ、CALL 0005H。これがそのまま QDOS、MS-DOS の INT 21h に継承され、Function 2 (Console Output) や Function 9 (Print String) は50年経った今もコマンドラインに息づく。

text:
Copyright (c) 1978, 1979, 1980
Digital Research, Box 579, Pacific Grove, California

ai_analysis.technical_context: Box 579 is a PO Box. Digital Research ran from Kildall's home in Pacific Grove, CA.
ai_analysis.human_story: A town of 15,000 people, a monarch butterfly sanctuary. The world PC OS standard shipped from a PO Box here.

appraiser_note: Pacific Grove, Box 579——私書箱。人口1万5千人の海辺の町から、世界のパーソナルコンピュータ標準OSが出荷された。

text:
FCB (File Control Block):
  byte 1-8:   filename (8 chars, space-padded)
  byte 9-11:  extension (3 chars)
  byte 12:    extent number
  byte 16-31: disk allocation map

ai_analysis.technical_context: FCB byte layout physically defines the 8.3 filename format. 36 bytes total.
ai_analysis.human_story: Windows 95 added long filename support in 1995. 20 years of 8-character filename limit from this struct.

appraiser_note: 8文字+拡張子3文字——「8.3形式」の物理的起源。36バイトのFCBの配置が、Windows 95まで20年間のファイル名制約を決めた。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Smalltalk has no statements, no operators, no control structures—only message sends. 3 + 4 is parsed as: object 3 receives the message +: with argument 4. Even ifTrue:/ifFalse: are messages, not keywords.
ai_analysis.human_story: Alan Kay later said: 'I'm sorry I coined OOP—the message-passing part is the essence.' Java and C++ borrowed the class part. Smalltalk-80 kept the message part. Erlang's actors, Ruby's blocks, and React's components all trace back to this one idea.

appraiser_note: Smalltalk のコア哲学は1行で表せる: 「<受信者> <セレクタ> [<引数>...]」。3 + 4 は「Integer 3 に +: メッセージを送る」。文も演算子も制御構文も存在せず、全てがメッセージ送信。Alan Kay の本意は class でも継承でもなく、この一行にある。

text:
arrayClass <- memory fetchClassOf: array.
self success: (arrayClass=ClassArrayPointer) | (arrayClass=ClassStringPointer).

ai_analysis.technical_context: fetchClassOf works on any object, including classes themselves. Everything is an object.
ai_analysis.human_story: Blue Book pseudocode and C++ implementation side by side. Dan Ingalls implemented the Blue Book spec literally.

appraiser_note: fetchClassOf——クラスもオブジェクト。Blue Book疑似コードとC++が同じファイルに共存する、Dan Ingallsの35年の実装の痕跡。

text:
[currentClass~=NilPointer] whileTrue:
    [(self lookupMethodInDictionary: dictionary) ifTrue: [^true].
    currentClass <- self superclassOf: currentClass].
messageSelector <- DoesNotUnderstandSelector.

ai_analysis.technical_context: Method lookup climbs the superclass chain. If not found, sends doesNotUnderstand: -- the foundation of metaprogramming.
ai_analysis.human_story: doesNotUnderstand: is not an error handler. It is a design feature. Ruby, Python, and JavaScript all inherited this idea.

appraiser_note: 継承ツリーを登りきっても見つからなければ `doesNotUnderstand:` を送る——エラーではなくメッセージ。Ruby、Python、JavaScriptの動的ディスパッチの源流。

text:
/*
** 2001-09-15
**
** The author disclaims copyright to this source code.  In place of
** a legal notice, here is a blessing:
**
**    May you do good and not evil.
**    May you find forgiveness for yourself and forgive others.
**    May you share freely, never taking more than you give.
*/

ai_analysis.technical_context: Public domain dedication via blessing rather than legal text. The same comment block appears at the top of every public SQLite source file from 2001 onward.
ai_analysis.human_story: D. Richard Hipp wrote this when SQLite was a side project to handle databases on naval destroyers. Twenty-five years later, SQLite is the most deployed software in history (>1 trillion devices), and the blessing still leads every file. No copyright. Only good wishes.

appraiser_note: SQLite の全ファイル冒頭に置かれた「祝福」。著作権の代わりに「善をなし、悪をなさぬように。自分を許し、他者を許すように。惜しみなく分かち合い、受け取る以上に奪わぬように。」と書かれている。1兆台超のデバイスにデプロイされた人類最大のソフトウェアは、ライセンス文ではなく祈りで始まる。

ai_analysis.technical_context: iPKey == -1 means no INTEGER PRIMARY KEY. When iPKey >= 0, that column IS the rowid -- no separate index needed.
ai_analysis.human_story: One of SQLite's quiet design gems: INTEGER PRIMARY KEY is not a column. It is an alias for the 64-bit rowid.

appraiser_note: `iPKey = -1`が「存在しない」を表す——INTEGER PRIMARY KEYは列ではなく64ビットrowidの別名。インデックス不要の設計がSQLiteの軽量さを支える。

text:
#define SQLITE_VERSION        "--VERS--"
#define SQLITE_VERSION_NUMBER --VERSION-NUMBER--

ai_analysis.technical_context: sqlite.h.in is a template. Build process replaces --VERS-- and --VERSION-NUMBER-- with real values to produce sqlite3.h.
ai_analysis.human_story: Richard Hipp's build system generates the public header from this template. The .in extension signals it is never shipped raw.

appraiser_note: `--VERS--`はプレースホルダー——ビルド時に置換される。sqlite.h.inはテンプレートであり、直接配布されることはない。Richard Hippの自動化の痕跡。

name: int from 0 to inf
description: eqnのユーザー構文。LaTeX（1985年）より10年以上前、Bell Labsの科学者はこの記法で積分記号を印刷していた。166行のyacc文法が `int from 0 to inf e sup {-x sup 2} dx` を解析し、troff呪文に変換する。
code: int from 0 to inf e sup {-x sup 2} dx
appraiser_note: LaTeX以前の数式組版がどう実現されていたかを初めて理解した。eqnは言語設計の問題として数式を解いた——パーサで。

name: approx = troff呪文
description: lookup.cの変換テーブル。左列が人間の言葉、右列がtroff呪文。`approx`（≈）は `\v'-.2m'\z\(ap\v'.25m'\(ap\v'-.05m'` ——垂直移動・オーバープリント・オフセットを組み合わせて≈を組版する。この変換知識が1974年のCコード1行に埋め込まれている。
appraiser_note: `approx`の定義が3つのtroff命令の連鎖だと知ったとき、美しいと思った。組版知識をCの文字列リテラルに圧縮している。

name: VERT(n)——3の倍数への丸め
description: VERTマクロ。Bell Labsの写植機では1pt=6座標単位。浮動小数点なしの整数演算で0.3m（0.3em）を計算し、3の倍数に揃える。EFFPSは写植機の最小サイズ6ptを保証する。1974年の精密組版の解。
code:
#define VERT(n)   ((((n)+1)/3)*3)
#define EFFPS(p)  ((p) >= 6 ? (p) : 6)
appraiser_note: 浮動小数点なしで精密な組版座標を計算する——整数演算の賢い使い方。写植機の物理的制約がマクロに刻まれている。

name: #define INCH 432
description: troffのコア定数。Graphic Systems CAT（Computer Aided Typesetter）フォトタイプセッタの物理的精度が1インチ=432単位。`#ifdef NROFF` で240（端末・ラインプリンタ向け）と切り替わる。同じ8,224行のソースから2つのバイナリが生まれる。
code:
#ifdef NROFF
#define INCH 240
#else
#define INCH 432   /* CAT写植機 */
#endif
appraiser_note: 432という数字に写植機の物理が凝縮されている。`#ifdef NROFF` 1行が2つの世界（印刷vs端末）を分ける。

name: PAIR——2文字リクエストの圧縮
description: troffのリクエスト解析マクロ。2文字のtroffリクエスト（.br, .sp, .ce 等）を1つのintに圧縮してswitch文で処理する。contab[]配列が全リクエストを格納。`PAIR('b','r')` は `'b'<<8 | 'r'` として比較される。
code:
