Then qsort() with a callback comparator sorts the array — K&R C had qsort in the standard library, and function pointer callbacks (high-order functions) were canonical idiom. After sort and output, free() releases the memory. The full malloc/realloc/free + qsort+callback combination is the minimal example of dynamic data structures in K&R C, used in countless utilities to handle 'unknown count of items to process'.
ai_analysis.human_story: How many symbols does an object file have? nm doesn't know in advance — could be 5 or 50,000. So it grows the array dynamically: start with malloc(1 entry), realloc to (i+1 entries) for each new symbol. Read, store, repeat. Then qsort() sorts the whole array with a callback comparator. After use, free() releases. This is the textbook 'unknown-size collection in C' pattern — but with a quadratic cost (each realloc may copy all prior items). Modern C uses doubling (capacity *= 2 when full) for amortized linear cost. In 1979 PDP-11 / VAX programs typically had 100-1000 symbols, so O(N²) overhead was a few milliseconds — irrelevant. The combination malloc + realloc + free + qsort + callback is the minimal example of dynamic memory + sorting in K&R C, the pattern every utility used for 'variable-size data'.

appraiser_note: nm が **malloc/realloc で動的にシンボル配列拡張**——ファイルから読みながらサイズ確定。 各新シンボル: symp==NULL なら malloc(1 entry)、 そうでなければ realloc(symp, (i+1) * sizeof(nlist))。 **インクリメンタル realloc は合計 O(N²) コスト** (各 realloc が以前の全エントリを copy する可能性)、 modern の amortized O(N) 倍々戦略 (capacity *= 2) ではない。 PDP-11/VAX プログラムは典型 100-1000 シンボル、 O(N²) オーバーヘッドは実用上無視できる (数 ms)。 その後 **qsort() + callback コンパレータ**でソート——K&R C 標準ライブラリに qsort、 関数ポインタ callback (高階関数) は canonical idiom。 ソート/出力後 free() で解放。 **malloc/realloc/free + qsort+callback の組み合わせ**は **K&R C の動的データ構造の最小例**——「件数不明のアイテム処理」 のためあらゆるユーティリティが踏襲したパターン。 modern style なら容量倍々で amortized O(N)、 でも 1979 年はシンプルさ優先。

text:
# nm as the canonical linker-error detective tool:

# Step 1: undefined references
$ cc main.c
ld: Undefined:
_printf
_write
*** Error code 1

# Step 2: nm shows what's missing
$ nm main.o | grep ' U '
         U printf
         U write

# Step 3: find which library provides them
$ nm /usr/lib/libc.a | grep ' T printf'
printf.o:00000000 T printf

# Step 4: add -lc to link line, problem solved
$ cc main.c -lc

# Modern equivalents (all derived from nm's T/D/B/U vocabulary):
#   GNU nm + c++filt    : nm with C++ name demangling
#   readelf -s          : ELF-specific, more detail
#   objdump -t          : disassembler with symbol table
#   otool -t            : Mach-O equivalent (macOS)
#   ldd                 : dynamic library dependencies (nm dynamic version)
#   IDE Symbol Browser  : GUI wrapper around nm output

# Shell one-liners using nm:
$ nm prog | awk '$2=="U" {print $3}' | sort -u    # all undefined refs
$ nm libfoo.a | awk '$2=="T" {print $3}'           # all global functions
$ comm -12 <(nm libfoo.a | grep ' T ' | awk '{print $3}' | sort) \
            <(nm prog | grep ' U ' | awk '{print $2}' | sort)
# functions from libfoo that prog actually uses

# Security audit:
$ nm libapp.so | c++filt | grep -E 'strcpy|gets|sprintf'
# unsafe function usage

$ nm program | awk '$2 ~ /[uU]/ {print $NF}' | xargs -I{} nm /lib/* | grep '{}'
# what shared libs provide what symbols

ai_analysis.technical_context: nm is the canonical Unix linker-error-detective tool. The workflow: see 'undefined reference to foo' linker error -> nm object | grep ' U ' lists missing symbols -> nm /usr/lib/*.a | grep ' T foo' finds providing library -> link with -lfoo to resolve. This 4-step debugging dance is the foundation of C development workflow for 50 years. nm composes powerfully with Unix shell: grep filters by type letter, awk extracts the name column, sort/uniq deduplicates, comm intersects between programs, c++filt demangles C++. Combined with ldd (dynamic linker view), objdump (full disassembly), readelf (ELF detail), nm is part of the 'binary inspection toolchain' that every systems programmer uses. The same patterns work for security audit (find unsafe function usage), library dependency analysis (what does prog use from libfoo), reverse engineering (what's inside this binary). 1979's 239-line nm seeded an entire workflow culture that persists across Linux, BSD, macOS, embedded systems.
ai_analysis.human_story: What's the most common C developer task? Fix linker errors. 'undefined reference to printf' has frustrated every C programmer since 1972. The fix dance starts with nm: pipe through grep ' U ' to see what's missing, then nm /usr/lib/*.a | grep ' T missing_name' to find which library has it, then add -llib to your compile command. Done in 30 seconds with experience. nm + shell composition is the entire binary-inspection toolchain: grep (filter by type), awk (extract names), sort/uniq (deduplicate), comm (intersect), c++filt (demangle), ldd (dynamic libs), objdump (full disassembly). Together they form the systems-programmer's microscope. Security auditors use nm to find strcpy/gets/sprintf calls. Library maintainers use it to track ABI changes. Reverse engineers use it as first reconnaissance. All from a 239-line program written in 1979, whose output format became the de facto language for talking about binaries.

appraiser_note: nm は **canonical Unix linker-error-detective ツール**。 ワークフロー: 「undefined reference to foo」 リンカエラー → nm object | grep ' U ' で不足シンボル一覧 → nm /usr/lib/*.a | grep ' T foo' で提供ライブラリ発見 → -lfoo でリンク解決。 この **4 ステップデバッグダンス**が 50 年間 C 開発の基礎。 nm は Unix シェルと強力に合成: grep でタイプフィルタ、 awk で名前カラム抽出、 sort/uniq で重複除去、 comm でプログラム間積集合、 c++filt で C++ demangling。 ldd (動的リンカビュー)、 objdump (フルディスアセンブル)、 readelf (ELF 詳細) と組み合わせ、 nm は **「binary inspection toolchain」** の一部——全 systems プログラマが使う顕微鏡。 同パターンで **セキュリティ監査** (unsafe 関数検出)、 **ライブラリ依存分析** (prog が libfoo から何使うか)、 **reverse engineering** (binary 内部) すべて可能。 1979 年の **239 行 nm** が、 Linux/BSD/macOS/組込みシステムまで継承する**ワークフロー文化**の種。

ai_analysis.technical_context: time exemplifies the canonical Unix fork+exec+wait process model. fork() splits the process; parent gets child pid (positive), child gets 0, errors give -1. Child calls execvp(argv[1], &argv[1]) to replace itself with the target program (PATH-searched). Parent ignores SIGINT/SIGQUIT to survive Ctrl-C, captures times() before/during wait, and loops `while (wait(&status) != p)` to wait for THIS specific child (in case other children exit first; modern code uses waitpid(p, ...)). Status decoding: low 8 bits = killing signal, high 8 bits = exit code — this layout is the de facto Unix convention encoded in POSIX macros WIFEXITED/WEXITSTATUS/WTERMSIG. The fork+exec+wait pattern is unchanged from 1979 to 2026 — every shell, init, cron, systemd, docker daemon uses it. This is the most stable systemcall API in computing history; changing it would break every Unix program ever written.
ai_analysis.human_story: How does a shell run a program? Three system calls: fork (split into parent + child), exec (child becomes the target program), wait (parent waits for child to finish). 1979's time command shows this in its purest form: time(&before) records start, fork() splits, child calls execvp to become argv[1], parent calls wait() to block until child exits. After wait, status holds child's exit code (high 8 bits) and possibly killing signal (low 8 bits). This three-syscall dance is unchanged since 1979 — every shell (sh, bash, zsh, fish), every supervisor (init, systemd, runit), every container runtime (docker, podman), every job scheduler (cron, at), all do fork+exec+wait. It's the most stable API in Unix history. Changing it would break literally every Unix program. The 1979 lesson: get the primitives right once, and they outlive everything else.

appraiser_note: time が **canonical Unix fork+exec+wait プロセスモデル**を体現。 fork() がプロセス分割、 親は子の pid (正)、 子は 0、 エラーは -1。 子は **execvp(argv[1], &argv[1])** で target プログラムに自身を置換 (PATH 検索)。 親は SIGINT/SIGQUIT を ignore して Ctrl-C 生存、 wait 前後で times() 取得、 **`while (wait(&status) != p)`** ループで**この**子を待つ (他の子が先に exit する場合に備えて、 modern は waitpid(p, ...))。 **status デコード**: 下位 8 bit = killing signal、 上位 8 bit = exit code——このレイアウトは Unix の defacto 慣習、 POSIX マクロ WIFEXITED/WEXITSTATUS/WTERMSIG にエンコード。 **fork+exec+wait パターンは 1979 年から 2026 年まで不変**——全シェル、 init、 cron、 systemd、 docker daemon が使用。 **Unix 史上最も安定した syscall API**——変えると全 Unix プログラムが壊れるため。 1979 年の教訓: **プリミティブを正しく設計すれば他のすべてより長く生きる**。

ai_analysis.technical_context: times() syscall fills struct tms with 4 cumulative CPU time values: tms_utime (user-mode CPU for this process), tms_stime (kernel-mode CPU for this process), tms_cutime (children's user, cumulative), tms_cstime (children's system, cumulative). time(1) uses cutime/cstime since it measures children. The user vs system distinction reflects kernel design: user-mode runs application code, kernel-mode runs syscalls (read, write, open, fork). CPU-bound programs show high user, I/O-bound show high system, waiting programs show low user+system but high real. Units: clock ticks (60Hz on PDP-11, 100Hz on modern Linux, accessed via CLK_TCK or sysconf(_SC_CLK_TCK)). times() is the ancestor of getrusage (more fields: maxrss, page faults, IO), clock_gettime(CLOCK_PROCESS_CPUTIME_ID) (nanosecond precision), /proc/[pid]/stat (kernel internal view), perf stat (with hardware counters). The 4-field tms struct established the data model for CPU accounting that every monitoring tool still uses.
ai_analysis.human_story: What's the difference between 'user time' and 'system time'? user = your code running. system = the kernel running on your behalf (handling syscalls like read, write, open). A pure-compute program (fibonacci) is mostly user. A grep over a huge file is heavy on system (lots of read syscalls). A `sleep` is neither — the process isn't running. times() syscall returns all four values: your user/system + your children's cumulative user/system. time(1) measures children, so it uses cutime/cstime (the children's time, accumulated). This data model — user vs system, separated for this process and its children — was established in 1979 and survives unchanged. getrusage() adds more fields (max memory, IO ops), clock_gettime adds nanosecond precision, /proc adds live view, perf stat adds hardware counters. But the fundamental categories are still user/system, established in struct tms's 4 fields.

appraiser_note: times() syscall が **struct tms に 4 つの累積 CPU 時間値**を格納: **tms_utime** (このプロセスの user-mode CPU)、 **tms_stime** (このプロセスの kernel-mode CPU)、 **tms_cutime** (子プロセスの user、 累積)、 **tms_cstime** (子プロセスの system、 累積)。 time(1) は子計測なので cutime/cstime 使用。 **user vs system 区別**はカーネル設計を反映: user-mode はアプリケーションコード、 kernel-mode は syscall (read、 write、 open、 fork)。 CPU バウンド = user 高、 I/O バウンド = system 高、 待機 = 両方低 + real 高。 単位は **clock ticks** (PDP-11 60Hz、 modern Linux 100Hz、 CLK_TCK / sysconf(_SC_CLK_TCK) で取得)。 times() は **getrusage の祖** (maxrss、 page faults、 IO 数追加)、 **clock_gettime(CLOCK_PROCESS_CPUTIME_ID) の祖** (ns 精度)、 **/proc/[pid]/stat の祖** (カーネル内部ビュー)、 **perf stat の祖** (ハードウェアカウンタ統合)。 **4 フィールド tms 構造体が CPU accounting データモデルを確立**——50 年経ってもすべての監視ツールが踏襲する categorization。

ai_analysis.technical_context: time(1) measures wall-clock elapsed time via the time() syscall, taking samples before fork() and after wait() returns. time() returns Unix epoch seconds (since 1970-01-01 UTC) with 1-second precision — coarse by modern standards, but adequate for 1979 commands that took seconds-to-minutes. The (after - before) * 60 multiplies by 60 to match the clock-tick units used elsewhere in printt(). The 'real time' value includes fork/exec overhead since time() is sampled outside the wait. Pitfall: time() returns wall-clock time, susceptible to NTP adjustments mid-measurement — modern code uses clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC) which never goes backward. The real/user/sys triad relationship: real >> user+sys means waiting (I/O, sleep); real ≈ user+sys means CPU-bound single-core; user > real means parallel execution across multiple cores (user accumulates per-core CPU time). This triad is the universal vocabulary for performance analysis.
ai_analysis.human_story: How does time measure 'real' elapsed seconds? Sample time() before fork, sample again after wait, subtract. time() returns Unix epoch seconds (1970 onwards), 1-second precision. Multiply by 60 to convert to the clock-tick units used for user/sys (60 Hz PDP-11). This includes fork/exec overhead — that's correct, because that overhead IS real elapsed time. The gotcha is NTP: if your system clock adjusts mid-measurement, real time can go backward or jump forward. Modern code uses clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC) which is immune to clock adjustments. The real/user/sys triad relationship reveals process behavior: real >> user+sys means waiting (sleep, I/O); real ≈ user+sys means CPU-bound single-core; user > real means parallel (multi-core CPU time accumulates faster than wall clock). This vocabulary — real, user, sys — is the universal language for talking about process performance, born in 1979.

appraiser_note: time(1) は **time() syscall** で wall-clock elapsed time を計測——fork() 前後と wait() 完了後にサンプリング。 time() は **Unix epoch seconds** (1970-01-01 UTC 起点) を **1 秒精度**で返す——modern からは粗いが、 1979 年は秒-分単位コマンドに十分。 **(after - before) * 60** で 60 倍して clock-tick 単位に揃える (printt() 内部で統一)。 real time には fork/exec オーバーヘッド含む——正しい (それも real elapsed time の一部)。 **罠**: time() が wall-clock を返すので NTP 調整中だと real time が backward / jump 可能、 modern は clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC) で monotonic 保証。 **real/user/sys 三項関係**がプロセス挙動を明かす: real >> user+sys = 待機 (sleep、 I/O)、 real ≈ user+sys = CPU-bound single-core、 user > real = 並列 (multi-core CPU 時間が壁時計より速く累積)。 この **「real、 user、 sys」 vocabulary** が、 プロセス性能議論の universal language——1979 年生まれ、 50 年継承。

ai_analysis.technical_context: time(1) installs signal(SIGINT, SIG_IGN) and signal(SIGQUIT, SIG_IGN) in the parent after fork, ensuring time survives Ctrl-C and Ctrl-\\. When user interrupts, shell sends SIGINT to the foreground process group; both time and child receive it. Child dies (default), but time stays alive, returns from wait(), prints partial timing info, exits with child's status. Without this protection, time would die alongside child, losing the timing measurement — exactly when the user most needs to know how long it ran. The asymmetry is enabled by execvp's signal-handler reset: when child calls execvp, user-installed handlers revert to default, so child's SIGINT default (die) is preserved while parent's SIGINT ignore takes effect. This 'parent survives, child dies, parent reports' pattern is the prototype for every Unix supervisor: shell (job control), init (orphan adoption), systemd, supervisord, runit, docker daemon, kubernetes pod manager. The 'measurement tool that outlives the measured process' is essential design for reliability — 1979 time established it in 2 signal() calls.
ai_analysis.human_story: What happens when you Ctrl-C a `time ./long_program`? Both time and long_program receive SIGINT. Without protection, both die — and you lose the timing info just when you wanted it most (the program was clearly too slow). time's fix: signal(SIGINT, SIG_IGN) in the parent only. When Ctrl-C arrives, child dies (default), time keeps running, calls wait(), reports 'real X user Y sys Z, Command terminated abnormally', exits cleanly. The asymmetry works because exec resets the child's signal handlers — child runs with SIGINT default (die), parent runs with SIGINT ignored (survive). This 'parent survives, child dies, parent reports' is the prototype of every Unix supervisor: shell job control, init, systemd, supervisord, docker, kubernetes. The measurement tool must outlive the measured process or the measurement is useless. 1979 time encoded this principle in 2 signal() calls.

appraiser_note: time(1) が fork 後の親に **signal(SIGINT, SIG_IGN) と signal(SIGQUIT, SIG_IGN)** をインストール、 time が Ctrl-C と Ctrl-\\ で生存。 ユーザーが中断時、 シェルが foreground process group に SIGINT 送信——time と子両方が受信。 **子は default で死亡、 time は生存、 wait() から戻り、 部分計測結果を出力、 子の status で exit**。 この保護なしだと time も子と一緒に死亡——**計測欲しかった瞬間に失う**。 非対称性は **execvp の signal handler reset** で実現: 子の execvp が user-installed handler を default にリセット、 親の signal() は子に影響しない——子は SIGINT default (死亡)、 親は ignore (生存)。 **「親生存、 子死亡、 親が報告」 パターン**は **Unix supervisor の原型**: シェル job control、 init (orphan 養子化)、 systemd、 supervisord、 runit、 docker daemon、 kubernetes pod manager。 **計測ツールは計測対象より長生きすべき**——信頼性のための essential design、 1979 年 time が **2 つの signal() 呼び出し**で確立。

ai_analysis.technical_context: printt() converts clock ticks to human-readable hh:mm:ss.dd via mixed-radix arithmetic. quant[]={6,10,10,6,10,6,10,10,10} are the bases for each digit position: 6 (1/60 sec from 60Hz ticks), 10 (1/10 sec), 10 (sec ones), 6 (tens of sec, base 6 = 60sec/min), 10 (min ones), 6 (tens of min, base 6 = 60min/hour), 10/10/10 (hour digits). The conversion loop digit[i] = a % quant[i]; a /= quant[i] is mixed-radix decomposition — same algorithm as base-conversion but with varying base per position. Output loop walks digits in reverse, choosing 3 character cases: actual digit ('0'+digit), zero-padding ('0' if leading nonzero seen), or suppression (pad[i] = ' ' for top digits). Separator strings sep[]/nsep[] encode positional punctuation: '.', ':', ':' between specific digit pairs. This 27-line function is the ancestor of every time-formatting library: POSIX strftime, Python timedelta.__str__, Java Duration.toString, Go time.Duration.String. The mixed-radix approach is more elegant than dedicated time-arithmetic — same algorithm formats any non-decimal positional system.
ai_analysis.human_story: How does '130 clock ticks' become '0:02.16'? printt() does mixed-radix arithmetic: digit 0 is 1/60 sec (PDP-11's 60 Hz tick), digit 1 is 1/10 sec, digit 2 is sec, digit 3 is tens of sec (with base 6 to overflow at 60), digit 4 is min, etc. quant[]={6,10,10,6,10,6,10,10,10} encodes the bases for each position. The loop a%quant[i]; a/=quant[i] decomposes the number into mixed-radix digits — same algorithm as binary/hex conversion but with varying bases. Output walks digits in reverse with 3 cases: digit, zero-pad, or space-suppress. Separator strings sep[]/nsep[] encode where '.' and ':' go. 27 lines that convert any clock-tick count into 'hh:mm:ss.dd' format. The same algorithm formats Python's timedelta, Java's Duration, Go's time.Duration — all descendants of this 1979 printt(). The lesson: time-of-day is mixed-radix (60/60/24/...), and the algorithm to format it is the same one that prints any positional number — just with non-uniform bases.

appraiser_note: printt() が clock ticks を人間可読の **hh:mm:ss.dd** に変換、 **mixed-radix 算術**で実現。 **quant[] = {6, 10, 10, 6, 10, 6, 10, 10, 10}** が各桁の基数: 6 (1/60 sec、 60Hz tick から)、 10 (1/10 sec)、 10 (sec の 1 桁)、 6 (sec の 10 桁、 6 で 60sec/min に繰り上げ)、 10 (min の 1 桁)、 6 (min の 10 桁、 60min/hour に繰り上げ)、 10/10/10 (hour 桁)。 **変換ループ digit[i] = a % quant[i]; a /= quant[i]** は **mixed-radix 分解**——2 進/16 進変換と同アルゴリズム、 桁ごとに基数可変。 **出力ループ**が桁を逆順に walk、 **3 ケース**で文字選択: 実際の数字、 zero-padding (leading nonzero 後)、 suppress (pad[i] = ' ' で上位桁)。 **sep[]/nsep[] 区切り文字列**が位置的 punctuation ('.', ':') をエンコード。 **27 行関数**が**全 time formatting library の祖**: POSIX strftime、 Python timedelta.__str__、 Java Duration.toString、 Go time.Duration.String。 **mixed-radix アプローチ**は専用 time-arithmetic より elegant——同一アルゴリズムが任意の非 10 進位取り表記をフォーマット。

ai_analysis.technical_context: pwd identifies the filesystem root by stat()ing '/' at startup and saving its device + inode numbers as (rdev, rino). The (dev, ino) pair uniquely identifies any file across the entire Unix system — inode alone is unique only within a single filesystem, but multiple mounted filesystems can have the same inode number. Loop termination compares current directory's (d.st_dev, d.st_ino) to root's saved (rdev, rino). This is far more robust than path-string comparison: symlinks, bind mounts, chroots, namespaces can make path strings misleading, but (dev, ino) is the kernel's ground truth. The same pattern is used in find -inum, rsync's --link-dest hardlink detection, du's hardlink deduplication, and modern container path resolution. Every modern OS that supports mount namespaces (Linux, FreeBSD jails, Solaris zones) still uses (dev, ino) as the canonical file identity. The pwd algorithm encodes this principle in 4 lines.
ai_analysis.human_story: How do you know when you've reached the root of the filesystem? You can't just check if the path is '/' — you don't have the path yet, that's what you're computing. pwd's answer: stat '/' at startup, save its (dev, inode). Loop: stat current dir; if its (dev, inode) matches root's, we've arrived. This is the Unix way of identifying files — (dev, ino) is unique system-wide, while inode alone is only unique within one filesystem. The trick survives every kind of trickery: symlinks, bind mounts, chroots, container namespaces. Modern tools that need 'is this the same file' (find -inum, rsync --link-dest, du with hard links, container path resolution) use the exact same (dev, ino) comparison. 4 lines of 1979 C teach the universal file-identity principle.

appraiser_note: pwd は起動時 stat('/') でルートディレクトリの **device + inode** 番号を (rdev, rino) として保存、 ファイルシステムのルート判定の anchor に。 **(dev, ino) ペアが Unix システム全体で unique ファイル識別子**——inode 単独は 1 FS 内のみ unique、 マウントされた複数 FS で同じ inode 番号が共存可能。 ループ終了は current dir の (d.st_dev, d.st_ino) と root の保存値の一致。 **path 文字列比較より遥かに堅牢**: symlink、 bind mount、 chroot、 namespace で path 文字列は欺けるが **(dev, ino) は kernel の ground truth**。 同パターン: find -inum、 rsync --link-dest hardlink 検出、 du の hardlink 重複排除、 modern container path 解決。 mount namespace サポートする modern OS (Linux、 FreeBSD jails、 Solaris zones) すべて **(dev, ino) を canonical file identity** として使用。 pwd アルゴリズムが**この原則を 4 行**でエンコード。

ai_analysis.technical_context: pwd's ascent loop uses 3 syscalls per level: open('..', O_RDONLY) gets a file descriptor to the parent for reading its entries; fstat(fd, &dd) reads parent's (dev, inode) through the fd — race-safe because the fd locks the directory's identity; chdir('..') moves process CWD to parent so '.' becomes the new starting point next iteration. The fstat-via-fd pattern (not stat-via-path) is the canonical race-safe access — between an open and a subsequent stat, the path could be renamed, but the fd refers to the same inode. This 'pin-then-inspect' pattern is the precursor to modern fexecve, openat, fchmod, fstatat — the whole *at() family. The chdir() side effect mutates process state, but pwd is itself a child process (forked from shell), so the shell's CWD is unaffected. Process state isolation: every Unix process has its own CWD inode reference. Modern containers use the same primitive: pivot_root changes the root for a process group, not the whole system.
ai_analysis.human_story: How does pwd climb to the parent? Three syscalls: open('..') to get a file descriptor to the parent (for reading its entries), fstat(fd) to get parent's (dev, ino) — race-safe through the fd, and chdir('..') to actually move the process CWD up. The fstat-through-fd trick is important: if you stat by path between open and use, the path could be renamed or removed, but the fd is pinned to one inode. This 'open-then-fstat' pattern is the ancestor of fexecve, openat, fchmod, fstatat, the entire *at() family of syscalls invented later. The chdir() mutates the process CWD, but pwd is a child process forked from the shell — shell's CWD is unaffected by the child mucking around. Process-private CWD is what makes pivot_root and container filesystems possible: each process can have its own view of 'where am I'.

appraiser_note: pwd の上昇ループは**レベル毎 3 つの syscall**: **open('..', O_RDONLY)** で親ディレクトリの fd 取得 (エントリ読み取り用)、 **fstat(fd, &dd)** で親の (dev, inode) を fd 経由で取得 (race-safe)、 **chdir('..')** でプロセス CWD を親に移動 (次イテレーションで '.' が新しい起点)。 **fstat-via-fd パターン** (stat-via-path ではない) が **canonical race-safe アクセス**——open と subsequent stat の間に path が rename される可能性があるが、 fd は同じ inode を pin。 「**pin-then-inspect**」 パターンが**modern fexecve、 openat、 fchmod、 fstatat、 *at() 系全 syscall の前駆**。 chdir() の副作用がプロセス状態を変更、 でも pwd 自体がシェルから fork された子プロセス——シェルの CWD は影響なし。 **プロセス状態の isolation**: 全 Unix プロセスは独自の CWD inode 参照を持つ。 modern container も同プリミティブ: pivot_root が**プロセスグループ単位**で root 変更、 system 全体ではない。

ai_analysis.technical_context: pwd finds its own name by reading the parent directory's entries and matching its own inode. struct direct in V7 filesystem is 16 bytes: unsigned short d_ino (16-bit inode) + char d_name[14] (NUL-padded filename). pwd reads these entries via read() on the directory fd — yes, directories were just files in V7. Two cases: same filesystem (d.st_dev == dd.st_dev) means inode comparison is sufficient; different filesystem (mount point crossing) requires stat()ing each entry because inode numbers are not unique across filesystems. The match-by-inode pattern works because Unix filesystems guarantee that a file appears in its parent directory's entries, with d_ino set to its inode. Modern Linux: directories are no longer read() directly (must use opendir/readdir), and filenames are variable-length (ext4) or hash-organized (btrfs), but the principle 'find self in parent's entries' is unchanged. getcwd() syscall does this inside the kernel using dcache.
ai_analysis.human_story: How does pwd discover its own name? By reading the parent directory and looking for itself. struct direct is a 16-byte fixed-size record: 2-byte inode + 14-byte name. pwd reads these entries one by one with read(). When d_ino matches its own inode, the d_name is its own name. Two cases: same filesystem means inode numbers are comparable (just look for matching d_ino); crossing a mount point means inodes might collide between filesystems, so stat() each entry and compare (dev, ino). This works because Unix filesystems guarantee every file appears in its parent's entries by inode. Modern filesystems use variable-length names (ext4) or B-trees (btrfs), and you can't read() a directory directly anymore — must use opendir/readdir. But the algorithm 'find self in parent by inode' is unchanged, and getcwd() syscall does exactly this inside the kernel using dcache.

appraiser_note: pwd は親ディレクトリのエントリを読み、 自分の inode と一致するものを探して**自分の名前**を発見。 **struct direct** (V7 FS) は **16 バイト固定サイズ**: unsigned short d_ino (16-bit inode) + char d_name[14] (NUL-padded、 最大 14 文字)。 pwd は read() でこれらエントリを読む——**V7 ではディレクトリも単なるファイル**だった。 **2 つのケース**: 同一 FS (d.st_dev == dd.st_dev) は inode 比較で十分、 マウントポイント越え (異 FS) は inode 番号が FS 間で衝突する可能性があるので**各エントリを stat() して (dev, ino) ペア比較**。 **inode マッチが動く理由**: Unix FS の不変条件——ファイルは必ず親ディレクトリのエントリに d_ino とともに存在。 **modern Linux**: ディレクトリの直接 read() は不可 (opendir/readdir 必須)、 ファイル名は可変長 (ext4) または B-tree (btrfs)、 でも **「親内で inode 検索」 という原理は不変**——getcwd() syscall が kernel 内で dcache を使い同じことを行う。

ai_analysis.technical_context: cat() prepends each discovered directory name to the path buffer. Since pwd discovers names from leaf to root (myapp, projects, keita, home), it needs to build the path in reverse — or prepend as it goes. cat()'s approach: compute new name length via inline strlen (while(d_name[++i]!=0)), check overflow against 512-byte buffer, shift existing path right by (i+1) bytes, place '/' at position i, prepend new name in positions [0..i-1]. The shift loop runs backward (j = off+1; j >= 0; --j) for overlap safety, like memmove. Without prepending, pwd would need to collect names in an array and reverse at output time — equivalent but uses more memory. The 1979 design keeps path always in correct order, no separate reverse step. Modern code would use snprintf or strncat with a tail pointer, but the shift-and-prepend logic is the textbook 'how to build a string from the right end' algorithm. Same pattern in: shell PATH manipulation (prepending to PATH), URL path reconstruction (proxy rewrites), filesystem mount path overlay.
ai_analysis.human_story: pwd discovers names from leaf upward — first myapp, then projects, then keita, then home. But the output should be /home/keita/projects/myapp. How? Two options: collect names in array, reverse at output; or prepend each name to the buffer as you go. 1979 pwd chose prepending. cat() shifts the existing buffer right by (name length + 1) bytes, places '/' as separator, copies the new name into the front. After each step, name[] holds the correct partial path. The backward shift loop (j = off+1; j >= 0; --j) is overlap-safe, like memmove. When root is reached, prname() prepends leading '/' and outputs. This 'always-correct-order' approach avoids a separate reverse step, but costs O(N) shifts per insertion = O(depth²) total. Modern code might use snprintf with a tail pointer, but the shift-and-prepend pattern is the textbook approach for 'build a string from the right end'.

appraiser_note: cat() が発見した各ディレクトリ名を path バッファに**前置 (prepend)**。 pwd は名前を**葉から root へ**発見 (myapp、 projects、 keita、 home)、 でも出力は逆順——前置で**常に正しい順序**を保つ。 cat() のアプローチ: インライン strlen で新名前長計算 (while(d_name[++i]!=0))、 512 バイトバッファ overflow チェック、 **既存 path を (i+1) バイト右シフト**、 位置 i に '/' 配置、 位置 [0..i-1] に新名前を前置コピー。 **シフトループは逆順** (j = off+1; j >= 0; --j) で overlap-safe (memmove と同じ)。 前置なしだと array に集めて出力時に reverse——等価だがメモリ余分使用。 **1979 年の設計は常に path を正しい順序**で保持、 別途 reverse 不要。 modern code なら snprintf や tail pointer 使用するが、 **shift-and-prepend ロジック**は「文字列を末尾から build する」 教科書アルゴリズム。 同パターン: shell PATH manipulation (prepending to PATH)、 URL path reconstruction (proxy rewrites)、 filesystem mount path overlay。

text:
/* Evolution of "get current directory":

   1979 Bell-32V pwd (this file, 80 lines):
     - userspace traversal of ".."
     - read() parent directory entries
     - find self by inode match
     - prepend each name
     - mount-point handling via stat()
   
   1983 4.2BSD getwd() syscall:
     - kernel does the work
     - O(depth) using kernel structures
     - race-free, no chdir side-effects
   
   1992 POSIX getcwd():
     - unified interface (buf, size)
     - overflow-safe
     - standardized across Unix variants
   
   2026 modern Linux:
     - getcwd() syscall using dcache (in-memory)
     - alternate: readlink("/proc/self/cwd")
     - kernel handles mount namespaces, bind mounts, chroots transparently

   Modern pwd implementation (GNU coreutils, simplified):
*/

int main(int argc, char **argv) {
    char *cwd = getcwd(NULL, 0);   /* kernel does it all */
    if (cwd) {
        printf("%s\n", cwd);
        free(cwd);
    }
    return 0;
}

/* From 80 lines (1979) to 5 lines (modern). The algorithm now lives
   inside the kernel's d_path() walking dcache. But the principle —
   ascend via .., find self by inode, prepend names — is unchanged. */

ai_analysis.technical_context: pwd's evolution from 80-line userspace traversal (1979) to 1-syscall kernel call (1983 4.2BSD getwd, 1992 POSIX getcwd) exemplifies the Unix design pattern of 'frequently-used userspace operations promoted to syscalls'. Original pwd: open '..', read entries, find self by inode, prepend name, chdir up, repeat. Modern getcwd(): kernel walks dcache (dentry cache) from current task's pwd to root, building the path in kernel memory, copying to user buffer. Benefits: O(depth) with in-memory dcache vs O(depth × entries_per_dir) with disk reads; race-free since kernel holds locks; no chdir side-effect; handles mount namespaces, bind mounts, chroots, overlayfs transparently. The same evolution happened with: directory listing (ls userspace -> opendir/readdir libc -> getdents syscall), file matching (find userspace recursion -> fts() / openat()), process info (ps reading /etc/ttytab -> /proc filesystem). The 1979 pwd is preserved as historical illustration of why kernel acceleration of common operations matters.
ai_analysis.human_story: pwd's 80 lines in 1979 became 5 lines by 1992. Why? Because the kernel learned to do the work. 1979: userspace, read parent dirs, find self by inode, build path. 1983 4.2BSD: getwd() syscall — kernel walks structures directly, faster + race-free. 1992 POSIX: getcwd() standardized. Modern: kernel's d_path() walks the in-memory dcache (dentry cache), no disk reads, handles mount namespaces, bind mounts, chroots, overlayfs all transparently. The algorithm (ascend via .., find self in parent, prepend name) is unchanged — it just moved into the kernel. This same evolution happened with: ls (userspace read of dir entries -> opendir/readdir -> getdents syscall), find (userspace recursion -> fts() / openat() *at family), ps (reading /etc/ttytab -> /proc filesystem). 1979 pwd is preserved as the textbook example of how user-space tools evolve into kernel features when they're used enough to warrant optimization.

appraiser_note: pwd の進化: **80 行 userspace traversal (1979) → 1 syscall kernel call (1983 4.2BSD getwd、 1992 POSIX getcwd)** が、 **「頻出 userspace 操作の syscall 昇格」 という Unix 設計パターン**の典型。 元 pwd: open '..'、 read entries、 inode で self 発見、 name 前置、 chdir up、 反復。 modern getcwd(): **kernel が dcache (dentry cache) を walk**、 task の pwd から root まで、 kernel メモリで path 構築、 user buffer へコピー。 **利点**: in-memory dcache で O(depth) (disk read なし)、 kernel が lock 保持で race-free、 chdir 副作用なし、 mount namespace/bind mount/chroot/overlayfs を透過処理。 同じ進化が起きた例: **ls** (userspace read of dir entries → opendir/readdir → getdents syscall)、 **find** (userspace recursion → fts() / openat() *at family)、 **ps** (/etc/ttytab read → /proc filesystem)。 **1979 年 pwd は教科書例**——user-space ツールが十分使われると **kernel 機能に進化**する歴史。 同じアルゴリズム ('..' で上昇、 親内で self 発見、 名前前置) は kernel 内で生存中。

ai_analysis.technical_context: date establishes Unix epoch (1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC) as time_t 0, with each subsequent second incrementing time_t by 1. gtime() converts YYMMDDHHMM[.SS] input to epoch seconds: accumulate days from 1970 to target year via dysize() (which returns 365 or 366 based on Gregorian leap rules), adjust for leap year if target year is leap and month >= 3, accumulate days per month from dmsize[12], add day-1 (1-indexed -> 0-indexed), then scale up: *24 hours, *60 minutes, *60 seconds. timbuf is `long` = 32 bits on PDP-11/VAX. The 32-bit time_t reaches max value 2^31-1 = 2,147,483,647 at 2038-01-19 03:14:07 UTC — this is the Y2038 problem, baked into Unix's design from 1979. The `year += 1900` line embeds Y2K: 2-digit year input is interpreted as 1900-1999. Both Y2K and Y2038 are direct consequences of the 1979 design choices encoded here. Modern systems use 64-bit time_t to push the wraparound to year 292 billion AD (~20x the universe's age), but embedded/legacy 32-bit systems remain vulnerable.
ai_analysis.human_story: Why is January 1, 1970 the Unix epoch? Because Unix development started in 1969 and 1970 was a clean cutoff. date(1) embeds this convention: timbuf = 0 at 1970-01-01, each second adds 1, scaled through days/hours/minutes/seconds. timbuf is `long` which on PDP-11 is 32 bits — max value 2,147,483,647 = January 19, 2038 at 03:14:07 UTC. That's the Y2038 bug, hardcoded into Unix's design 60 years in advance. The line `year += 1900` is Y2K in plain sight: 2-digit year `00` becomes 1900, not 2000. 1979 programmers thought their code wouldn't last 20 years. Modern systems use 64-bit time_t (good until year 292 billion AD), and 4-digit year input via %Y format. But embedded systems running 32-bit time_t in 2038 will see clocks jump to 1901 — date.c's design legacy.

appraiser_note: date が **Unix epoch (1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC) を time_t 0** として確立、 以降 1 秒毎に time_t が 1 増。 gtime() が YYMMDDHHMM[.SS] 入力を epoch seconds に変換: 1970 年から target year まで dysize() で日数累積 (365 or 366、 Gregorian うるう年ルール)、 target year がうるう年で月 >= 3 なら +1 補正、 dmsize[12] で月毎日数累積、 day-1 (1-indexed → 0-indexed)、 *24 hours、 *60 minutes、 *60 seconds で scale up。 **timbuf は `long` = PDP-11/VAX で 32 bit**。 **32-bit time_t の最大値**: 2^31-1 = 2,147,483,647 = **2038-01-19 03:14:07 UTC**——これが **Y2038 問題**、 1979 年 Unix 設計に組み込み済み。 **`year += 1900`** 行が **Y2K**: 2 桁年入力を 1900-1999 として解釈。 両方とも 1979 年設計選択の直接的帰結。 modern システムは **64-bit time_t** で wraparound を **year 292 billion** に押し戻し (宇宙年齢の ~20 倍)、 実用上無限。 でも embedded/legacy 32-bit システムは脆弱。 1979 年プログラマは「**コードが 20 年以上使われるとは思わなかった**」 ——それが Y2K/Y2038 の文化的真相。

ai_analysis.technical_context: gtime() parses YYMMDDHHMM[.SS] input with a clever in-place reversal trick: walk to end of string (ep), then swap from both ends inward until sp meets ep. After reversal, parse from start: gp() reads 2 digits and returns c + 10*d — because the string is reversed, the 1s digit comes first and 10s second, so c + 10*d correctly reconstructs the value. The optional .SS handling is elegant: read first 2 digits as 'secs', check if next char is '.'. If not, those were actually minutes; if yes, skip the dot and read minutes. gp() takes a default value for missing fields — gp(L->tm_mday) means 'if input ends, use current day'. This enables partial input like `date 1342` (just hour:min, keep date current). The reversal-then-forward-parse pattern avoids backward iteration and string length checks, expressing 'parse right-to-left with optional suffix' in minimal K&R C. Modern code would use sscanf or regex, but the 1979 approach is more compact and direct.
ai_analysis.human_story: How do you parse `7904061342.18` when the `.18` is optional and might not be there? 1979 date's solution: physically reverse the string in place, then parse from the start. Walk to end of string (ep), swap chars from both ends until sp meets ep — classic in-place reversal. Now the string reads `81.243160497`, parse from start: read 2 digits, if next char is '.', those were seconds and skip the dot. Otherwise, those were minutes. gp() returns c + 10*d (not 10*c + d) because the string is reversed — the 1s digit comes first. Partial inputs work via defaults: gp(L->tm_mday) means 'if input is exhausted, use today's day'. This 'reverse then forward-parse' avoids backward iteration and string length math. Modern code would use sscanf or regex; 1979 K&R C does it with pointer arithmetic and an in-place swap. The aesthetics are clear: an algorithmic insight worth more than a library call.

appraiser_note: gtime() が YYMMDDHHMM[.SS] 入力を**巧妙な in-place 逆転**でパース: 文字列末尾まで walk (ep)、 sp と ep が出会うまで両端から swap——classic in-place reversal。 逆転後、 先頭から parse: gp() が 2 桁読み **c + 10*d** で返す——文字列逆順なので 1 の位が先、 10 の位が後、 これで正しい数値復元。 **optional .SS の処理が elegant**: 最初の 2 桁を 'secs' として読み、 次の char が '.' なら本当に秒、 そうでなければそれは分。 gp() がデフォルト値を取る——**gp(L->tm_mday)** で「入力が尽きたら現在日を使う」、 これで `date 1342` のような部分入力 (時:分のみ、 日付は現在) が可能。 **「逆転 → 前方 parse」 パターン**は逆順 iteration や文字列長計算を回避、 「optional suffix 付き右→左 parse」 を**最小 K&R C** で表現。 modern code は sscanf や regex 使うが、 1979 年のアプローチは**コンパクト + 直接的**——library call より価値ある **algorithmic insight**。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Unix time uses dual representation: time_t (long integer, epoch seconds, suitable for arithmetic) and struct tm (year/month/day/hour/min/sec/wday/yday/isdst, suitable for display). localtime()/gmtime() convert time_t to struct tm in local/UTC timezones. asctime() formats struct tm into a fixed 26-byte string 'Wed Apr  6 13:42:18 1979\n'. date inserts timezone string between time and year via clever printf: %.20s prints first 20 chars (up to time), then tzn (e.g. 'JST'), then ap+19 (year+newline) — splicing TZ into asctime's fixed output. The dual representation pattern (canonical integer + structured access) is the prototype of every time API since: Python datetime (timestamp + datetime object), Java Instant + LocalDateTime, JavaScript Date (ms epoch + getter methods), Go time.Time (internal nanos + accessor methods). The string-splicing technique is replaced in POSIX strftime(), which uses format strings like %a %b %e %T %Z %Y — but 1979 date achieves the same with raw printf and asctime's fixed output.
ai_analysis.human_story: Why does Unix have two time representations? time_t (just a big integer counting seconds since 1970) is great for arithmetic and storage but useless for humans. struct tm (separate fields for year, month, day, hour, etc.) is great for display but tedious for arithmetic. So Unix gives you both, with localtime/gmtime to convert one to the other, and asctime/ctime to format struct tm as text. asctime returns a fixed 26-byte string 'Wed Apr  6 13:42:18 1979\n'. To insert the timezone name between time and year, 1979 date does printf %.20s (first 20 chars: 'Wed Apr  6 13:42:18 '), printf tzn (e.g. 'JST'), printf ap+19 (' 1979\n'). String splicing through pointer arithmetic. Modern POSIX strftime() does this cleanly with format strings ('%a %b %e %T %Z %Y'), but the dual representation — integer epoch + struct accessor — survives in every modern time API: Python datetime, Java Instant, JavaScript Date, Go time.Time. All descend from the 1979 time_t / struct tm pair.

appraiser_note: Unix 時刻は **二重表現**: **time_t** (long integer、 epoch seconds、 算術向き) と **struct tm** (year/month/day/hour/min/sec/wday/yday/isdst、 表示向き)。 **localtime()/gmtime()** が time_t を struct tm に変換 (local/UTC TZ)。 **asctime()** が struct tm を **固定 26 バイト文字列** 'Wed Apr  6 13:42:18 1979\n' にフォーマット。 date は**timezone 文字列を時刻と年の間に巧妙に挿入**: **%.20s** で先頭 20 字 (時刻まで)、 tzn (例 'JST')、 **ap+19** (年+改行) ——asctime の固定出力に**ポインタ算術で TZ をスプライス**。 **二重表現パターン** (canonical integer + 構造化アクセス) が後のすべての時刻 API の原型: Python datetime (timestamp + datetime object)、 Java Instant + LocalDateTime、 JavaScript Date (ms epoch + getter)、 Go time.Time (internal nanos + accessor)。 文字列スプライシングは POSIX strftime() の format string ('%a %b %e %T %Z %Y') に置換されたが、 **integer epoch + struct accessor** という二重表現は modern 全時刻 API に継承。

ai_analysis.technical_context: When date sets the system clock (stime syscall, root-only), it records the change in /usr/adm/wtmp — the login history file — using two special records with non-terminal-name markers: '|' (before-change timestamp) and '{' (after-change timestamp). These markers are invalid as terminal names so they can't be confused with login records, but exist in the same struct utmp format. The `last` command reads wtmp and can report 'date change occurred between X and Y' by detecting these markers. This is forensic auditing: time-stamp manipulation is a common technique for hiding intrusions or backdating evidence, so wtmp preserves the discontinuity. stime() requires root (CAP_SYS_TIME in modern Linux), and the EPERM check prints 'no permission'. lseek(wf, 0L, 2) appends to end-of-file (whence=2 SEEK_END). Modern Linux: wtmp lives in /var/log/wtmp, utmpx extends utmp, adjtimex/clock_settime replace stime, systemd-journald additionally logs time changes via systemd-timesyncd. The audit principle — preserve discontinuities for forensic analysis — is unchanged.
ai_analysis.human_story: When you change the system time, what gets logged? In 1979 date(1), it writes two records to /usr/adm/wtmp (login history) with special terminal names: '|' marks 'time was X before change', '{' marks 'time is Y after change'. These aren't real terminal names, so they're recognizable as time-change markers, not login events. The `last` command can detect them and warn 'time discontinuity between X and Y'. Why preserve this? Forensics. Attackers backdate system clocks to hide intrusions; setting time without leaving evidence would help cover tracks. Bell Labs in 1979 already thought about this — and the lseek(0L, SEEK_END) ensures the record is appended (not overwriting). Modern Linux moved wtmp to /var/log/wtmp, replaced stime() with adjtimex/clock_settime, and systemd-journald logs time changes too. But the principle — preserve clock-change events in audit logs — is unchanged 47 years later.

appraiser_note: date がシステムクロックを設定する時 (stime syscall、 root-only)、 **/usr/adm/wtmp** (ログイン履歴ファイル) に変更を**特殊レコード**で記録: **'|'** (before-change timestamp)、 **'{'** (after-change timestamp)——通常 terminal 名と衝突しない markers。 **`last` コマンド**が wtmp を読み、 これらマーカーを検出して「**X-Y 間で時刻変更**」 を報告可能。 **フォレンジック監査**: タイムスタンプ操作は侵入隠蔽や証拠 backdating の常套手段、 wtmp が**不連続を保持**する。 stime() は root 必須 (modern Linux の CAP_SYS_TIME)、 EPERM チェックで 'no permission' 出力。 **lseek(wf, 0L, 2)** で末尾追記 (whence=2 SEEK_END)。 modern Linux: wtmp は /var/log/wtmp に移動、 utmpx が utmp 拡張、 **adjtimex/clock_settime が stime() 置換**、 **systemd-journald** が時刻変更も追加ログ。 47 年後も**「audit log で不連続を保持する」 原則は不変**——1979 年 Bell Labs が既に「**時刻操作はフォレンジック対象**」 と認識した先見性。

ai_analysis.technical_context: date.c contains two notorious time bugs in plain sight: Y2K (year += 1900 assumes 1900-1999 range) and Y2038 (long timbuf is 32-bit signed, overflows at 2,147,483,647 seconds = 2038-01-19 03:14:07 UTC). Y2K was solved through cultural adaptation: 4-digit year input, windowing (interpret 00-69 as 2000-2069, 70-99 as 1970-1999), and ISO 8601 date format. The cost was ~$300-600 billion globally in remediation. Y2038 is harder: 32-bit time_t is embedded in filesystem metadata (ext2/3 ctime), network protocols (NTP era), embedded Linux kernels (industrial control, IoT devices that won't be updated), and applications that explicitly use int32 for time. Linux kernel introduced 64-bit time_t syscalls (clock_gettime64) starting 2019, filesystems migrated (ext4 inode extensions, XFS v5, btrfs), glibc 2.34 (2021) made 64-bit time_t default on 32-bit ABIs. But embedded systems running unmodified 32-bit code in 2038 will see clocks jump to 1901, with consequences for certificate validity, log timestamps, scheduled events, and any time-based logic. The 1979 design choice 'long is enough' becomes a 60-year fuse.
ai_analysis.human_story: Two bugs in date.c that became famous: Y2K (year += 1900 assumed 1900-1999) and Y2038 (long timbuf is 32 bits, overflows at 2038-01-19 03:14:07 UTC). Y2K cost the world ~$300-600B in cleanup; Y2038 is potentially worse because it's at the OS/library/firmware level, harder to fix in deployed systems. 32-bit time_t is in filesystem metadata, network protocols, embedded device firmware, and applications that hardcoded int32 for time. Modern fixes: 64-bit time_t (good until year ~292 billion), 4-digit years via %Y, ISO 8601 dates, glibc 2.34 (2021) made 64-bit time_t default. Linux kernel added time64_t syscalls in 2019. Filesystems updated metadata formats. But embedded Linux running unpatched 32-bit code in 2038 will see clocks jump to 1901 — certificates expire, logs go backward, schedulers misfire. The 1979 'long is enough' bet became a 60-year fuse. Lesson: assume integer time wraps; always think about the next century, not just the next decade.

appraiser_note: date.c は **2 つの有名な時刻バグ**を内包: **Y2K** (year += 1900 で 1900-1999 と想定) と **Y2038** (long timbuf が 32-bit signed、 2,147,483,647 秒 = **2038-01-19 03:14:07 UTC** で overflow)。 **Y2K** は文化的適応で解決: 4 桁年入力、 windowing (00-69 → 2000-2069、 70-99 → 1970-1999)、 ISO 8601 date format。 全世界で約 **$300-600 億**の remediation コスト。 **Y2038** はより難しい: 32-bit time_t がファイルシステムメタデータ (ext2/3 ctime)、 ネットワークプロトコル (NTP era)、 embedded Linux kernel (産業制御、 IoT デバイスで更新不可)、 int32 を時刻に明示利用するアプリに**埋め込まれている**。 Linux カーネルが 2019 年から **64-bit time_t syscall** (clock_gettime64) 導入、 ファイルシステム移行 (ext4 inode 拡張、 XFS v5、 btrfs)、 **glibc 2.34 (2021)** で 32-bit ABI でも 64-bit time_t デフォルト化。 でも 2038 年に**未更新 32-bit code を走らせる embedded システム**は時計が 1901 年に jump——証明書失効、 ログ逆行、 スケジューラ misfire。 **1979 年の「long で十分」 という賭けが 60 年フューズ**になった。 **教訓**: 整数時刻は必ず wraparound する、 次の 10 年でなく**次の世紀**を考えるべし。

ai_analysis.technical_context: kill is a 40-line K&R C program that does essentially one thing: invoke the kill(pid, signo) syscall. The actual work — dispatching the signal to the target process, switching kernel context, invoking the target's signal handler or default action — happens entirely inside the kernel. The kill command is the thinnest possible user-space wrapper around a kernel mechanism. This 'kernel does the work, user tool is a gateway' pattern is the canonical Unix design philosophy: complex functionality lives in the kernel where it can be optimized and shared, while user tools provide minimal CLI interfaces. The same pattern: cat (read+write syscalls), ls (getdents syscall), chmod (chmod syscall), mount (mount syscall), kill (kill syscall) — each is a thin wrapper. 50 years later, the kill(pid, signo) syscall API is unchanged; only the syntactic sugar above it (signal name expansion, -l flag, pkill/killall variants) has evolved.
ai_analysis.human_story: What does kill(1) actually do? Almost nothing. 40 lines of K&R C, and the only real work is one line: `res = kill(pid, signo)` — a syscall. The kernel does all the heavy lifting: validates permission, finds the target process, queues the signal, switches context, runs the signal handler or default action. The kill command is a 40-line gateway to one of Unix's most powerful IPC primitives. This 'kernel does the work, user tool is a thin gateway' is the canonical Unix design: complex shared functionality lives in the kernel, user tools provide minimal CLI access. Same pattern in cat (read/write), ls (getdents), chmod (chmod syscall), mount (mount syscall). The kill(pid, signo) syscall API hasn't changed since 1979; pkill, killall, and signal-name expansion are just syntactic sugar on top. The genuinely interesting code lives in the kernel signal dispatch, not in this 40-line wrapper.

appraiser_note: kill は **40 行 K&R C** で本質的に**1 つのことだけ**実行: kill(pid, signo) syscall 呼び出し。 **実際の仕事**——signal を target プロセスにディスパッチ、 kernel コンテキスト切替、 target の signal handler または default 動作実行——**すべて kernel 内**で発生。 kill コマンドは **kernel メカニズムへの最薄 user-space wrapper**。 「**kernel が仕事、 user tool は gateway**」 パターンが **canonical Unix 設計哲学**: 複雑機能は kernel に (共有・最適化可能)、 user tools は最小 CLI インターフェース提供。 同パターン: cat (read+write)、 ls (getdents)、 chmod (chmod syscall)、 mount (mount syscall)、 kill (kill syscall)——すべて薄い wrapper。 **50 年経っても kill(pid, signo) syscall API は不変**——変わったのは**糖衣構文** (signal 名展開、 -l フラグ、 pkill/killall variants) のみ。 **真に興味深いコードは kernel の signal dispatch にあり、 この 40 行 wrapper にはない**——でもこの薄さこそが Unix 設計の本質。

text:
if (*argv[1] == '-') {
    signo = atoi(argv[1]+1);     /* "-9" -> 9 */
    argc--;
    argv++;
} else
    signo = SIGTERM;             /* default: graceful */

/* Signal behavior comparison:
   SIGTERM (15): catchable, allows cleanup
     - handler runs: close files, save state, release locks
     - graceful exit with exit(0) if no handler
   
   SIGKILL (9): uncatchable, instant kill
     - kernel terminates immediately, no handler runs
     - data may be partially written, locks abandoned
     - last resort when SIGTERM is ignored
   
   kill PID  -> tries graceful (SIGTERM)
   kill -9 PID -> forces immediate (SIGKILL)
   
   The 'kindly default, override when needed' pattern is core Unix design.
*/

ai_analysis.technical_context: kill's default signal is SIGTERM (15) — a 'kindly' termination request that processes can catch and handle: flush files, save state, release locks, then exit. SIGKILL (9) is the uncatchable nuclear option — kernel terminates immediately without invoking handlers. The 'default SIGTERM, opt-in SIGKILL via -9' design embeds the Unix philosophy of 'kindly default, override when needed'. This pattern propagates: systemd's KillSignal=SIGTERM + SendSIGKILLAfter timeout, docker stop's SIGTERM + grace period + SIGKILL, kubernetes pod termination's preStop hook + SIGTERM + terminationGracePeriodSeconds + SIGKILL. All inherit the SIGTERM-then-SIGKILL pattern from 1979 kill(1). Signal-handler programming pattern: register handler with signal()/sigaction(), do cleanup atomically (signal-safe), set a flag, let main loop notice and exit cleanly. Without SIGTERM, you couldn't write robust daemons — every server process from sshd to nginx depends on graceful termination via SIGTERM.
ai_analysis.human_story: Why does `kill PID` not actually kill? It sends SIGTERM, which asks the process politely to terminate. The process can catch it, run cleanup (flush files, save state, release locks), then exit gracefully. SIGKILL (-9) is the uncatchable forced kill — kernel terminates instantly, no handler, possible data loss. The 'kindly default, force on demand' design is core Unix philosophy: `kill PID` tries graceful, `kill -9 PID` is for when graceful fails. This SIGTERM-then-SIGKILL pattern propagates everywhere: systemd waits for SIGTERM then escalates to SIGKILL, docker stop has --time=10 for graceful period, kubernetes has terminationGracePeriodSeconds. All inherit from 1979 kill(1). Daemon writers depend on SIGTERM to do cleanup: close listening sockets, drain connections, write state, exit 0. Without graceful termination, every server crash would risk data loss.

appraiser_note: kill のデフォルト signal は **SIGTERM (15)** ——**「親切な」 終了要求**、 プロセスが catch + ハンドル可能: file flush、 state 保存、 lock 解放後 graceful exit。 **SIGKILL (9) は uncatchable な核オプション**——kernel が即終了、 ハンドラ実行なし。 「**default SIGTERM、 -9 で SIGKILL opt-in**」 設計が **Unix 哲学の「親切な default、 必要なら override」 を体現**。 同パターンが伝播: systemd の **KillSignal=SIGTERM + SendSIGKILLAfter timeout**、 docker stop の **SIGTERM + grace period + SIGKILL**、 kubernetes pod termination の **preStop hook + SIGTERM + terminationGracePeriodSeconds + SIGKILL**。 すべて **1979 kill(1) の SIGTERM→SIGKILL パターン**継承。 **signal-handler プログラミングパターン**: signal()/sigaction() でハンドラ登録、 atomic に cleanup (signal-safe)、 flag 設定、 main ループが気づいて clean exit。 **SIGTERM なしでは robust daemon を書けない**——sshd から nginx までの全 server プロセスが graceful termination via SIGTERM に依存。

text:
if (*argv[1] == '-') {
    signo = atoi(argv[1]+1);       /* "-9" -> 9, "-15" -> 15 */
    argc--;
    argv++;
} else
    signo = SIGTERM;

/* 1979 Bell-32V kill: numeric signals only
   $ kill -9 1234       # OK (SIGKILL)
   $ kill -15 1234      # OK (SIGTERM)
   $ kill -TERM 1234    # FAIL: atoi("TERM") = 0, invalid signal
   
   POSIX (1992) added:
     kill -TERM PID     # signal name without SIG prefix
     kill -SIGTERM PID  # with SIG prefix
     kill -s TERM PID   # -s flag form
     kill -l             # list all signal names/numbers
     kill -l 9           # name from number: KILL
     kill -l TERM        # number from name: 15
   
   Common signals (1979 V7 numbering):
     1=HUP 2=INT 3=QUIT 4=ILL 5=TRAP 6=IOT 7=EMT 8=FPE
     9=KILL 10=BUS 11=SEGV 12=SYS 13=PIPE 14=ALRM 15=TERM
     17=STOP 18=TSTP 19=CONT 20=CHLD
*/

ai_analysis.technical_context: Bell-32V kill accepts only numeric signal specifications: -9 (SIGKILL), -15 (SIGTERM), -1 (SIGHUP), etc. Implementation: atoi(argv[1]+1) — skip the dash, convert digit string to integer. atoi('9') = 9, atoi('TERM') = 0 (invalid). Signal name expansion is a POSIX 1992 extension via -s flag or kill -SIGNAME PID. 1979 sysadmins memorized signal numbers: 1=HUP, 2=INT, 9=KILL, 11=SEGV, 13=PIPE, 15=TERM, etc. These numbers became cultural shorthand: 'kill dash nine' is universally understood as 'force kill'. The argv[1]+1 pointer arithmetic skips the leading dash without a copy — typical K&R C compactness. argc-- and argv++ shift the argument list after consuming the signal flag. Modern coreutils kill (~300 lines) adds: signal name table, -l listing, -L verbose, error messages, but the syscall invocation is still the same single line.
ai_analysis.human_story: How do you tell 1979 kill which signal to send? Numeric prefix: `-9` for SIGKILL, `-15` for SIGTERM, `-1` for SIGHUP. Implementation: atoi(argv[1]+1) — skip the dash, parse the rest as integer. atoi('9') = 9. atoi('TERM') = 0 (invalid). Signal names weren't supported until POSIX 1992 added -SIGNAME and -s flags. 1979 sysadmins memorized: 1=HUP, 2=INT, 9=KILL, 15=TERM, 11=SEGV. These numbers became cultural shorthand — 'kill dash nine' is universally understood as 'force kill', even to engineers who don't know what SIGKILL is. Modern coreutils kill is ~300 lines vs 1979's 40, mostly to handle signal name tables, -l (list), -L (verbose), better errors. But the actual syscall call is the same one line — the 1979 code is the irreducible core.

appraiser_note: Bell-32V kill は **数値シグナル指定のみ**: -9 (SIGKILL)、 -15 (SIGTERM)、 -1 (SIGHUP) など。 実装: **atoi(argv[1]+1)** ——dash を skip、 残りを整数化。 atoi('9') = 9、 atoi('TERM') = 0 (無効)。 **シグナル名展開は POSIX 1992 拡張** (-s フラグまたは kill -SIGNAME PID)。 1979 sysadmin はシグナル番号を**暗記**: 1=HUP、 2=INT、 9=KILL、 11=SEGV、 13=PIPE、 15=TERM、 など。 これらの数字が**文化的略号**化: 「kill dash nine」 は SIGKILL を知らないエンジニアにも「強制 kill」 として通じる。 **argv[1]+1 ポインタ算術**で先頭 dash を skip——コピーなし、 K&R C のコンパクトさ。 **argc-- + argv++** で signal flag 消費後の引数 shift。 modern coreutils kill (~300 行) は signal 名テーブル、 -l リスト、 -L verbose、 エラーメッセージ追加だが、 **syscall 呼び出しは同じ 1 行**——1979 年コードが irreducible core。

ai_analysis.technical_context: kill accepts multiple pids in one invocation, processes them in a loop, reports per-pid errors to stderr, and accumulates failure into errlev for the final exit code. The loop doesn't early-abort on failure: kill 1234 5678 9999 sends to all three even if 9999 doesn't exist; only 9999 reports an error. errlev=1 if any failed. sys_errlist[errno] looks up the human-readable error string from the kernel error number (modern: strerror(errno)). The 'best-effort, partial failure, accumulated exit code' pattern is universal in Unix multi-target operations: rm file1 file2 (continues on missing files), cp src1 src2 dest, chmod 644 *.c, git commit -a with conflicts, kubectl delete multiple. All inherit the principle: try every operation, report each failure, exit non-zero if any failed. Modern shell scripts rely on this: 'kill $(jobs -p) 2>/dev/null' tries to kill all jobs, succeeds for live ones, ignores errors for already-dead.
ai_analysis.human_story: What if you `kill 1234 5678 9999` and 9999 is already dead? 1979 kill continues to the next pid, prints '9999: No such process' to stderr, then exits with code 1 (one failure occurred). It doesn't abort early — best-effort delivery to all targets. This 'partial failure with accumulated exit code' is universal in Unix multi-target commands: rm processes each file independently, cp does multi-source copies, chmod handles multi-target, git commit with conflicts, kubectl delete multiple resources. All inherit the principle: try everything, report each failure, exit nonzero if any failed. Modern shell scripts depend on this: `kill $(jobs -p) 2>/dev/null` works even when some jobs are already dead — kill ignores the dead ones, succeeds for the live, exits gracefully. The 1979 design choice — best-effort, never abort on first failure — saved billions of shell scripts from awkward retry logic.

appraiser_note: kill は**複数 pid を一括処理**、 ループで反復、 per-pid エラーを stderr 出力、 errlev に集約して最終 exit code に。 ループは**失敗で early-abort しない**: `kill 1234 5678 9999` は 9999 が存在しなくても 3 つ全てに送信試行、 9999 のみエラー報告。 失敗があれば errlev=1。 **sys_errlist[errno]** で kernel エラー番号を人間可読文字列に変換 (modern: strerror(errno))。 **「best-effort、 partial failure、 accumulated exit code」 パターン**は Unix 多 target 操作全般に universal: **rm file1 file2** (missing file で続行)、 **cp src1 src2 dest**、 **chmod 644 *.c**、 **git commit -a** with conflicts、 **kubectl delete** 複数 resource。 すべて**原則継承**: すべての操作を試行、 各失敗を報告、 何か失敗したら exit nonzero。 modern shell スクリプトの依存: `kill $(jobs -p) 2>/dev/null` で全ジョブ kill 試行、 live なら成功、 dead は無視、 gracefully exit。 **1979 年の design choice** (best-effort、 first failure で abort しない) が**何十億のシェルスクリプトを awkward retry logic から救った**。

text:
/* 1979 Bell-32V kill: 40 lines, 1 syscall.
   But kill(pid, signo) is the gateway to Unix's async IPC primitive.
   
   Evolution of signal-based IPC:
     1971 Unix V1  : limited signals (PDP-7)
     1975 V6       : signal() syscall, ~13 signals
     1979 V7/32V   : 31 signals (this file)
     1983 4.2BSD   : sigvec, sigsetmask (reliable signals)
     1992 POSIX    : sigaction, sigprocmask, sigsuspend
     2000+ Linux   : RT signals 32-64, sigqueue with data
     2010+         : signalfd + epoll (event loop integration)
   
   Modern Linux signal mechanisms (all descend from kill syscall):
     raise(signo)             - send to self
     kill(pid, signo)          - send to specific pid
     killpg(pgid, signo)       - send to process group
     tgkill(tgid, tid, signo)  - send to specific thread
     pthread_kill(tid, signo)  - thread-targeted (libpthread)
     sigqueue(pid, signo, val) - signal with attached data
   
   Signal usage in production:
     SIGHUP   : daemon config reload (nginx, sshd, syslogd, postfix)
     SIGUSR1  : log rotate, dump stats (apache, varnish)
     SIGTERM  : graceful shutdown (systemd default, docker stop)
     SIGKILL  : forced kill (OOM killer, SIGKILL timeout escalation)
     SIGSTOP  : suspend (Ctrl-Z via shell)
     SIGCONT  : resume (fg/bg via shell)
     SIGCHLD  : child died (parent supervisor wakeup)
     SIGPIPE  : broken pipe (shell pipeline cleanup)
   
   40 lines of 1979 kill expose this entire mechanism to users.
*/

ai_analysis.technical_context: The 40-line kill command is the user-space entry point to Unix's asynchronous IPC primitive: signals. Signals are kernel-mediated async notifications between processes — small (signal number), unreliable (coalesced if pending), uncatchable in special cases (SIGKILL/SIGSTOP). Evolution: V1 limited signals -> V7 31 signals (this file) -> 4.2BSD reliable signals via sigvec -> POSIX 1992 sigaction with sigset_t -> Linux RT signals 32-64 + signalfd. Modern variations: killpg (process group), tgkill (specific thread), pthread_kill (thread via pthread API), sigqueue (signal with data payload). Signal usage taxonomy: SIGHUP for daemon reload (nginx/sshd/syslogd), SIGUSR1/2 for app-specific (log rotate), SIGTERM/SIGKILL for shutdown (systemd/docker), SIGSTOP/SIGCONT for job control (Ctrl-Z), SIGCHLD for parent supervision, SIGPIPE for pipeline cleanup. Every server process, every container runtime, every supervisor depends on these signals. The kill(1) command exposes this entire mechanism in 40 lines.
ai_analysis.human_story: What does 40 lines of 1979 K&R C give you? Access to Unix's most powerful async IPC primitive: signals. Signals are kernel-mediated notifications between processes — tiny (just a signal number), async (interrupt program flow anywhere), and some are uncatchable (SIGKILL/SIGSTOP). Evolution: V1's basic signals -> V7's 31 -> 4.2BSD's reliable signals -> POSIX's sigaction -> Linux's RT signals + signalfd. Modern variants address process groups (killpg), specific threads (tgkill, pthread_kill), data-carrying signals (sigqueue). Every modern server uses signals: nginx SIGHUP reload, sshd SIGTERM graceful shutdown, systemd's whole lifecycle, docker stop's SIGTERM-then-SIGKILL, kubernetes pod termination. The 40-line kill command from 1979 is the gateway to all of this — a tiny tool exposing a kernel mechanism that became the basis for daemon control, job management, and container orchestration.

appraiser_note: **40 行の kill コマンド**が Unix の**非同期 IPC プリミティブ (signal) への user-space 入口**。 signal は kernel mediated な非同期通知: 小さい (signal 番号のみ)、 unreliable (pending coalesced)、 特殊ケースで uncatchable (SIGKILL/SIGSTOP)。 **進化**: V1 限定 → V7 31 signals (本 file) → 4.2BSD reliable signals via sigvec → POSIX 1992 sigaction with sigset_t → Linux RT signals 32-64 + signalfd。 **modern variants**: killpg (プロセスグループ)、 tgkill (特定スレッド)、 pthread_kill (pthread API)、 sigqueue (data payload 付き signal)。 **signal usage 分類**: SIGHUP (daemon reload: nginx/sshd/syslogd)、 SIGUSR1/2 (app-specific: log rotate)、 SIGTERM/SIGKILL (shutdown: systemd/docker)、 SIGSTOP/SIGCONT (job control: Ctrl-Z)、 SIGCHLD (parent supervision)、 SIGPIPE (pipeline cleanup)。 全 server プロセス、 全 container runtime、 全 supervisor が依存。 **40 行が現代 OS の root core を expose**——daemon control、 job management、 container orchestration の基盤。

ai_analysis.technical_context: mkfs produces the canonical Unix V7 filesystem disk layout: block 0 = boot loader, block 1 = superblock (struct filsys), blocks 2..s_isize = inode list (8 inodes per 512-byte block), remainder = data blocks. The boot block is 512 bytes of a.out text+data, loaded by BIOS at power-on. The superblock at fixed block 1 holds total size (s_fsize), inode area size (s_isize), free block cache (s_free[NICFREE=50]), free inode cache, and modification flags. Inode 0 is reserved (NULL), inode 1 historically held bad blocks list, inode 2 is always the root directory '/'. Generation order matters: inode blocks zeroed FIRST (every inode is initially 'unused'), free list built SECOND (after knowing which blocks are bad and not inode-used), proto-driven tree LAST (allocates blocks from free list, fills inodes), superblock written FINALLY with all final sizes. The 'superblock at block 1' convention is preserved across BSD FFS (with multiple backup copies), ext2/3/4 (block 1 or larger offset), XFS, btrfs — every Unix descendant FS knows to look for the superblock near the start of disk. fsck depends on this fixed location to recover from corruption.
ai_analysis.human_story: How do you turn a raw disk into a usable filesystem? mkfs writes 4 distinct regions: boot block (where BIOS loads), superblock (fs metadata), inode list (all file metadata), data blocks (file contents). Order matters: zero out inode blocks first (so every inode is 'unused'), build the free block list next (knowing which blocks aren't bad or already used by inodes), populate initial files/dirs from the proto file, write the superblock LAST with the final sizes. This 4-region layout — boot, superblock at block 1, inodes, data — is the V7 design that ext2, ext3, ext4 still follow today (with bitmap and journaling added). Even fsck depends on the superblock living at a known location. Every disk you've ever used in Linux/Unix/macOS/BSD descended from this 615-line file's vision of how to initialize a filesystem.

appraiser_note: mkfs が **canonical Unix V7 FS disk layout** を生成: **block 0 = boot loader、 block 1 = superblock、 block 2..s_isize = inode list (NIPB=8 inodes/block)、 残り = data blocks**。 boot block は 512 byte の a.out text+data、 BIOS が起動時にロード。 **block 1 の superblock** が全体サイズ (s_fsize)、 inode 領域サイズ (s_isize)、 free block cache (s_free[NICFREE=50])、 free inode cache、 変更フラグ保持。 **inode 0 = 未使用 (NULL)、 inode 1 = bad block list (歴史的)、 inode 2 = root directory '/'**。 **生成順序が重要**: inode blocks を**最初に**ゼロクリア (全 inode 初期 unused)、 **次に** free list 構築 (bad/inode 使用 block 判明後)、 **次に** proto file からツリー作成 (free list から alloc + inode 設定)、 **superblock は最後** (最終サイズ確定後)。 「**block 1 = superblock**」 convention が BSD FFS (backup copy 複数)、 ext2/3/4 (block 1 or larger offset)、 XFS、 btrfs に継承——全 Unix descendant FS が disk 先頭近くで superblock 検索。 **fsck は固定位置依存**で corruption recovery。

ai_analysis.technical_context: mkfs accepts a 'proto file' — a declarative DSL describing the initial filesystem structure. Syntax: type-permission-uid-gid for each entry, with 'd' for directory, '-' for regular file, 'c' for character device, 'b' for block device. Directories nest with '$' as the end marker (like Lisp parens). Regular files name a source file to copy contents from. Devices specify major/minor numbers. cfile() is recursive: for IFDIR it reads entries until '$', for IFREG it copies a source file, for IFCHR/IFBLK it just records the rdev number. The proto syntax makes filesystem construction REPRODUCIBLE (same proto = same FS), TEXT-BASED (grep/diff/version-control), and DOCUMENTABLE (proto file is also the FS spec). This 'declarative filesystem description' pattern is the conceptual ancestor of: Kickstart (RedHat installer config), preseed.cfg (Debian), cloud-init user-data (cloud VM provisioning), Ansible/Puppet/Chef (config management), Dockerfile (container layer definitions), Nix expressions (entire OS as data). All express 'this is what I want the system to look like' rather than 'follow these steps to build it'.
ai_analysis.human_story: How do you describe the initial contents of a filesystem? mkfs's answer is a declarative DSL — a proto file. Each line describes one file or directory: type ('d', '-', 'c', 'b'), permission octal, uid, gid, optional source path. Directories nest with '$' marking the end (like Lisp parens or YAML indentation). cfile() recursively walks the proto: for a directory, read entries until '$' and recurse; for a regular file, copy a source file's bytes into newly allocated blocks; for a device, just record the major/minor numbers. The proto file makes FS construction REPRODUCIBLE (same proto = same disk), textual (versionable, diffable, greppable), and self-documenting (the proto IS the spec). This 'declare what you want' pattern shows up everywhere later: Kickstart/preseed for OS installs, cloud-init for VM provisioning, Ansible/Puppet for config, Dockerfile for containers, Nix for entire systems. 1979's proto file is the ancestor of all declarative infrastructure-as-code.

appraiser_note: mkfs は **proto file** ——FS 初期構造を記述する**宣言的 DSL**を受け付ける。 構文: 各エントリに type + permission + uid + gid——'d' (directory)、 '-' (regular file)、 'c' (character device)、 'b' (block device)。 directory は **'$' を end marker** として nest (Lisp parens のように)。 regular file は**コピー元 source ファイル**を指定、 device は **major/minor** 番号。 **cfile() は再帰**: IFDIR なら '$' まで読んで再帰、 IFREG なら source ファイルを新規 allocate した block にコピー、 IFCHR/IFBLK なら rdev 番号記録。 **proto 構文**が FS 構築を **REPRODUCIBLE** (同 proto = 同 FS)、 **TEXT-BASED** (grep/diff/version control)、 **DOCUMENTABLE** (proto = FS spec) にする。 「**宣言的 filesystem description**」 パターンは概念的祖先: **Kickstart** (RedHat installer)、 **preseed.cfg** (Debian)、 **cloud-init user-data** (cloud VM provisioning)、 **Ansible/Puppet/Chef** (config management)、 **Dockerfile** (container layer)、 **Nix expressions** (entire OS as data)。 すべて「**こうあって欲しい**」 を表現、 「**こう作れ**」 ではない。 **1979 年 proto file が declarative infrastructure-as-code 全ての祖**。

ai_analysis.technical_context: V7 FS tracks free blocks via a chained list, not a bitmap. The superblock holds an inline cache of 50 free block numbers (s_free[NICFREE]). alloc() pops from this cache; when cache empties, the popped block's contents ARE the next chain link (df_nfree count + df_free[50] addresses). bfree() pushes to cache; when cache fills, the cache is written to the freed block itself, and a new cache begins. The freed block serves dual purpose: data storage AND chain link. Compare to bitmap: bitmap uses N/8 bytes for N blocks (fixed overhead) and allows O(1) per-block status check; chained list uses ~256 bytes in superblock + O(1) chain blocks on-demand. For 1979's small RAMs and small disks, chained list was more memory-efficient. Drawbacks: harder to allocate contiguous blocks (no spatial info), fsck must walk entire chain to verify consistency, LIFO order (without interleaving) causes seek-unfriendly allocation. BSD FFS (1984) moved to bitmap with cylinder groups for both spatial locality and faster fsck. ext2 (1993) uses block bitmap. Modern FSes use bitmaps universally. But the V7 chained list was the right choice for 1979's resource-constrained PDP-11/VAX systems.
ai_analysis.human_story: How does V7 FS track which blocks are free? Not a bitmap — a chained linked list. The superblock holds an inline cache of 50 free block numbers. alloc() pops one; when the cache empties, the popped block's CONTENTS are the next chain link (also 50 addresses). bfree() pushes; when full, the cache spills to the freed block itself. The freed block IS the link — clever dual use. No separate bitmap needed: just the superblock cache plus chain blocks scattered through the disk. For 1979's tiny RAMs (256KB on PDP-11), this was memory-efficient — bitmap would need ~16KB just for a 64MB disk. Drawbacks emerged: no spatial info (can't find contiguous free regions), fsck has to walk the whole chain, LIFO order causes seek-unfriendly allocation without the f_m/f_n interleaving trick (see trace 4). BSD FFS in 1984 moved to bitmap with cylinder groups, ext2 in 1993 used straight bitmap. Modern FSes universally use bitmaps. But the V7 chained list was the right choice when RAM was scarce and disks were small.

appraiser_note: V7 FS は **chained list** で free block 管理——**bitmap ではない**。 superblock が **inline cache 50 個** (s_free[NICFREE]) 保持。 **alloc()** が cache から pop、 cache 空になると **pop した block 自体が次の chain link** (df_nfree + df_free[50])。 **bfree()** が cache に push、 cache 満杯になると**現 cache を解放 block 自体に書き出し**、 新 cache 開始。 **解放 block の dual purpose**: data storage AND chain link。 **bitmap 比較**: bitmap は N/8 byte (fixed overhead)、 O(1) per-block status check、 chained list は ~256 byte in superblock + chain blocks on-demand。 1979 年の小 RAM (PDP-11 256KB) と小 disk では chained list が**memory-efficient** ——bitmap だと 64MB disk に 16KB 必要。 **欠点**: 連続 block 確保困難 (空間情報なし)、 fsck が chain 全体 walk 必要、 LIFO 順序が interleaving (痕跡 4) なしだと seek-unfriendly。 **BSD FFS (1984)** が cylinder group + bitmap に移行、 **ext2 (1993)** が straight bitmap。 modern FS は全 bitmap。 でも **V7 chained list は 1979 年制約への正解**——RAM 希少 + 小 disk への最適化。

ai_analysis.technical_context: V7 mkfs implements (m,n) rotational interleaving when building the initial free list, to optimize sequential file access on rotating disks. The chained free list is LIFO by default, so freeing blocks 0,1,2,... sequentially would cause alloc() to return ...,2,1,0 — reverse-order allocation forcing disk arm to seek backward for sequential writes. The fix: permute the free order. adr[j] = (i + f_m) % f_n produces a sequence where consecutive entries are f_m apart. Default m=3, n=500: each next allocation lands 3 blocks away from the previous one. This matches the disk's rotational latency — after reading/writing block N, blocks N+1, N+2 will have already rotated past the head; block N+3 is the next reachable block. By spacing allocations 3 apart, sequential I/O avoids waiting for a full disk rotation. The technique was state-of-the-art for late-1970s rotating disks. BSD FFS (1984) improved this with explicit cylinder group awareness. Modern SSDs and NVMe have no rotational latency, so interleaving was eventually dropped. But 1979's f_m/f_n is a textbook example of FS designers embedding hardware knowledge into software algorithms — and the 10-line bflist() permutation could yield 2-5x throughput improvement on V7-era hardware.
ai_analysis.human_story: Why does mkfs free blocks in a weird permuted order? To optimize sequential file access on 1970s rotating disks. The chained free list is LIFO, so if you free blocks 0, 1, 2, ..., alloc() returns ..., 2, 1, 0 — backward order. Writing a sequential file would constantly seek backward. The fix: permute the free order so adjacent free entries are f_m=3 blocks apart. adr[] = [1, 4, 7, 10, 3, ...] — every step jumps 3 forward. After freeing in this order, sequential allocations land 3 blocks apart on disk. Why 3? After reading block N from a rotating disk, blocks N+1 and N+2 have already passed the read head — N+3 is the next sector you can read without waiting a full rotation. By spacing allocations 3 apart, you avoid the 16ms-per-rotation penalty. 10 lines of code that yielded 2-5x throughput improvement on V7 hardware. BSD FFS (1984) replaced this with explicit cylinder group awareness; modern SSDs made it obsolete (no rotational latency). But 1979's f_m/f_n is the textbook example of 'encode hardware knowledge in the algorithm'.

appraiser_note: V7 mkfs が初期 free list 構築時に **(m,n) 回転式 interleaving** を実装、 rotating disk での sequential ファイルアクセスを最適化。 chained free list は LIFO ——block 0,1,2,... を順次 free すると alloc() が ...,2,1,0 を返す (逆順)、 sequential 書き込みで disk arm が**後ろへ seek** ——大ペナルティ。 **修正**: free 順序を**permute**——adr[j] = (i + f_m) % f_n で **連続エントリが f_m 個離れる**。 default m=3, n=500: 次の alloc は前の 3 block 先。 これが**rotational latency にマッチ**: disk arm が block N を読み終わった瞬間、 block N+1、 N+2 は既に head を通過済、 **N+3 が次に到達可能な sector**。 allocation を 3 個間隔にすれば、 **full rotation 待ち (16ms) を回避**。 1970 年代後半の rotating disk への state-of-the-art 最適化。 **BSD FFS (1984)** が explicit cylinder group awareness で更に洗練、 **modern SSD/NVMe** は rotational latency ゼロで interleaving 廃止。 でも 1979 年 f_m/f_n は **「ハードウェア知識をアルゴリズムに encode」** の教科書例—— **10 行のコード**で V7 ハードウェア 2-5 倍スループット向上。

ai_analysis.technical_context: V7 inode contains a 40-byte di_addr[] field (13 3-byte block numbers). First 10 are direct pointers — data block numbers. Position 10 is single-indirect (points to a block of pointers, 128 entries × 512 bytes = 64 KB more). Position 11 is double-indirect (128 × 128 × 512 = 8 MB). Position 12 is triple-indirect (128³ × 512 = 1 GB). Total max file ~1 GB, vast for 1979. Design rationale: small files (typical 1979 source code, docs) fit in 10 direct pointers = 5 KB, requiring just 1 disk read per data block (no pointer chasing). Large files (databases, binaries) use indirect chains. The 'small fast, large possible' tradeoff. Inode is fixed 64 bytes so 8 fit per block — good for inode block I/O efficiency. This direct+indirect structure survived UNCHANGED in BSD FFS (1984, expanded to 12+3), Linux ext2/ext3 (1993, 12+3), Minix FS. Only ext4 (2008) replaced indirect with extents (contiguous block ranges, much better for huge files), and XFS/btrfs/ZFS use B-trees. But 25+ years (1979-2008) of file systems used essentially this 1979 design. Every C file you compiled, every text document you edited, every binary you ran in that era — all addressed by direct+indirect block pointers from this template.
ai_analysis.human_story: How does V7 store a file? The inode contains 13 'block pointers'. First 10 are direct — they point to the actual data blocks. Pointer 11 is 'single indirect' — points to a block that contains 128 more pointers (8 MB total reach). Pointer 12 is 'double indirect' — points to a block of pointers to blocks of pointers (8 MB reach). Pointer 13 is 'triple indirect' (1 GB reach). Why? Small files (5 KB or less) fit entirely in direct pointers — just 1 disk read per data block, no pointer chasing. Large files (databases, binaries) use the indirect chain, paying 2-4 reads per data block but enabling files up to 1 GB. The 'small fast, large possible' tradeoff. This design survived 25+ years: BSD FFS (12+3), ext2 (12+3), ext3 (12+3) all used direct+indirect. Only ext4 (2008) replaced it with extents (contiguous ranges) for better huge-file performance. But every C program, every config file, every email you read in the 1980s-2000s was addressed by direct+indirect block pointers from this 1979 design. That's how persistent good data structures are.

appraiser_note: V7 inode は **40 byte di_addr[]** フィールド (13 個の 3-byte block 番号) 保持。 **最初 10 = direct pointers** ——data block 番号直接。 **位置 10 = single-indirect** (block of pointers、 128 entries × 512 byte = 64 KB)。 **位置 11 = double-indirect** (128 × 128 × 512 = 8 MB)。 **位置 12 = triple-indirect** (128³ × 512 = 1 GB)。 **総最大ファイル ~1 GB** ——1979 年として広大。 **設計理由**: 小ファイル (1979 年 typical source code、 docs) は 10 direct で 5 KB、 **1 disk read = 1 data block** (pointer chasing なし)。 大ファイル (database、 binary) は indirect chain 使用。 **「small fast、 large possible」 trade-off**。 inode は固定 64 byte、 8 個/block——inode block I/O 効率良。 この **direct+indirect 構造**が **BSD FFS (1984, 12+3 に拡張)**、 **Linux ext2/ext3 (1993, 12+3)**、 **Minix FS** で**そのまま継承**。 **ext4 (2008)** が初めて indirect を **extent (連続 block range)** に置換 ——巨大ファイル効率向上。 **XFS/btrfs/ZFS は B-tree**。 でも **25+ 年間 (1979-2008)** のファイルシステムが基本的に **1979 年のこの design**を使用。 **「**良いデータ構造は時を超える**」 ——コンパイルした全 C プログラム、 編集した全テキスト、 実行した全バイナリが direct+indirect block pointer で addressing されてきた。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Bell-32V (1979) has no rmdir(2) syscall. The rmdir command (SUID root) deletes a directory by manually unlinking its three links in order: name/.. then name/. then the directory itself, reusing one char buffer and truncating it with '\0' between steps. This is exactly what rm forks and execs /bin/rmdir to do. rmdir(2) arrives in 4.2BSD (1983).
ai_analysis.human_story: A directory is just an inode plus the links that point at it. Without rmdir(2), removing one meant performing surgery by hand, in the right order, with root privileges. /bin/rmdir was the small SUID knife that did it. When rm needed to delete a directory, it could not; it had to call this program.
