appraiser_note: rmdir(2) システムコールが無い1979年、ディレクトリ削除は name/.. → name/. → 本体 の3回の unlink を正しい順序で唱える儀式だった。1つの char バッファを末尾から '\0' で削りながら逆向きにたどる。これは rm(#141) が fork+execl で /bin/rmdir を呼んだ「呼ばれる側」そのもの。ディレクトリのリンクを unlink できるのは root だけなので rmdir は SUID root だった。

ai_analysis.technical_context: stat("", &cst) stats the empty string, which V6/V7-era kernels resolve to the current directory (equivalent to stat(".")). rmdir compares st_dev+st_ino of the target against the cwd to refuse removing the current directory. Later POSIX makes stat("") return ENOENT, so this idiom stops working.
ai_analysis.human_story: In 1979 an empty path quietly meant 'here'. The code leans on that loose behavior to ask 'am I being told to delete the ground I stand on?' Identity of a directory is dev+inode, the same definition rm used to detect copying a file onto itself.

appraiser_note: stat("") ——空文字列を stat するとカレントディレクトリ(.)が返る、V6/V7時代の緩いパス解決を利用。target と cwd の st_dev+st_ino を比べて「カレントディレクトリは消せない」を実装。後年の POSIX では stat("") は ENOENT になりこの書き方は通用しなくなる。同一性の定義は dev+inode——rm の自己コピー検出と同じ。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Without readdir(3), rmdir opens the directory as a plain file and reads raw 16-byte struct direct records. Emptiness check: skip d_ino==0 (deleted slot) and the . / .. entries; any other live entry means 'not empty'. Same world as rm.c reading directories with raw read(2).
ai_analysis.human_story: A directory was just a file full of struct direct records, and you read it with read() like anything else. The opendir/readdir abstraction came later. To know if a directory was empty you scanned its bytes and counted what was still alive.

appraiser_note: readdir(3) が無いので、ディレクトリを通常ファイルとして open し 16バイトの struct direct を生 read。空判定は d_ino==0(削除済みスロット)と ./.. を読み飛ばし、他に生きたエントリが1つでもあれば not empty。rm.c がディレクトリを生読みして再帰したのと同じ世界観——opendir/readdir の抽象は後の時代。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Bell-32V has no mkdir(2). mkdir uses mknod (normally for device nodes) with mode 040777 = S_IFDIR | 0777 to create the directory inode, then chown(d, getuid(), getgid()) to hand ownership from root back to the real user. Only root can mknod, so mkdir is SUID root. The mirror of rmdir's triple unlink.
ai_analysis.human_story: To make a directory you reached for the device-node maker, because that was the only general way to create an inode of an arbitrary type. mknod needed root; so mkdir, like rmdir, was a small SUID program that borrowed privilege and immediately gave the result back to you.

appraiser_note: mkdir(2) が無い1979年、mknod(d, 040777, 0) でディレクトリ inode を生む。040777 = S_IFDIR(040000) | rwxrwxrwx(0777)。mknod は本来デバイスファイル用だが「任意種別の inode を作る」唯一の汎用手段だった。mknod は root 専用ゆえ mkdir も SUID root。直後に chown(d, getuid(), getgid()) で所有権を本人へ返す。rmdir の3連 unlink の鏡像。

ai_analysis.technical_context: After mknod creates the bare inode, mkdir builds the directory by linking it twice: link(d, "d/.") makes the self reference, link(pname, "d/..") makes the parent reference. On failure it rolls back by unlinking what it made. Exactly inverts rmdir's unlink(d/..)/unlink(d/.)/unlink(d).
ai_analysis.human_story: A freshly mknod'd inode is an empty box. It becomes a directory only once . points at itself and .. points at the parent. Build is link, link; teardown is unlink, unlink, unlink. The same three links, run forwards or backwards.

appraiser_note: mknod で作った空 inode に、link を2回張ってディレクトリを完成させる。link(d, d/.)で自己参照、link(pname, d/..)で親参照。失敗時は作ったものを unlink で巻き戻す手作業ロールバック。rmdir の unlink(d/..)→unlink(d/.)→unlink(d) を正確に裏返した姿——同じ3本のリンクの組立と解体。

ai_analysis.technical_context: mkdir ignores five signals at startup so the mknod -> link(.) -> link(..) sequence cannot be interrupted half-done, which would leave an inconsistent directory for fsck to find. A user-space approximation of the atomicity an atomic mkdir(2) would have provided for free.
ai_analysis.human_story: Making a directory was three steps, and being killed between them left a half-built thing on disk. So mkdir closed the door on every signal it could before it began. It is the ancestor of the critical section, written in 1979 with five calls to signal().

appraiser_note: mkdir は起動時に5つのシグナルを SIG_IGN で無視する。mknod→link(.)→link(..) の途中で殺されると、半分だけできた不整合なディレクトリが残り fsck が首をかしげるため。原子的な mkdir(2) があれば不要だった原子性を、signal でユーザー空間に近似——クリティカルセクションの素朴な原型。

ai_analysis.technical_context: egrep's match loop is a single DFA table lookup per input character: cstat = gotofn[cstat][c], with out[cstat] marking accepting states. No backtracking at all, so matching is strictly linear in input length. This is the opposite of grep (#65/#166), whose advance() VM backtracks on '*' and can go exponential. The &0377 mask keeps signed chars as positive table indices.
ai_analysis.human_story: Where Thompson's grep ran a backtracking bytecode VM, Aho's egrep compiled the regex into a deterministic automaton and then just read a table. Each input byte is examined exactly once and never revisited. Theory chose the implementation: a regular language is recognized by a finite automaton, so build the automaton and let it run.

appraiser_note: egrep のマッチは1文字につき DFA 遷移表を一発引き: cstat = gotofn[cstat][*p&0377]、out[cstat] で受理判定。バックトラッキングは皆無ゆえ入力長に厳密に線形。grep(#65/#166) の advance() が * で再帰・後戻りし最悪指数だったのと正反対。&0377 は符号付きcharを正の添字に保つマスク。RE2(Russ Cox) へ繋がる『DFAで確実に線形』の源流。

ai_analysis.technical_context: cgotofn builds the DFA transition table directly from the regex syntax tree using the follow-position construction (cfoll/follow/cstate compute follow sets and nullability). A DFA state IS a set of leaf positions. The classic worklist loop 'for(s=0; s<=n; s++)' grows n as new states are discovered; notin() dedupes state sets, add() registers new ones. This is algorithm 3.5 of the Dragon Book (Aho, Sethi, Ullman 1986) running in 1979.
ai_analysis.human_story: Alfred Aho wrote the regex-to-DFA construction that would later appear in the Dragon Book, the bible of compiler courses. egrep.y is that algorithm as a living tool, seven years before the textbook. The same Aho is the 'a' in awk and the author of fgrep's Aho-Corasick automaton.

appraiser_note: cgotofn() は構文木から follow位置構成で DFA 遷移表を直接生成（cfoll/follow/cstate が follow集合と nullable を計算）。DFA の状態とは葉位置の集合。古典的ワークリスト for(s=0;s<=n;s++) が処理中に新状態を発見して n を伸ばし、notin() で状態集合の重複排除、add() で新規登録。ドラゴンブック(Aho,Sethi,Ullman 1986)のアルゴリズム3.5が1979年に既に動いていた。

ai_analysis.technical_context: egrep expresses extended regex (| + ? and grouping) directly as a yacc grammar. %left declarations encode operator precedence (postfix STAR/PLUS/QUEST bind tightest, then CAT, then OR); 'r r %prec CAT' gives juxtaposition its precedence. Actions build a syntax tree in parallel arrays name[]/left[]/right[]/parent[] via node/unary/enter. The empty rule b prepends .* (enter(DOT)+unary(STAR)) so the search is unanchored.
ai_analysis.human_story: Where grep hand-coded its parser, egrep let yacc parse the regex, the same Bell Labs habit that built bc and eqn from grammars. And the quiet trick: the grammar silently prefixes .* to every pattern, turning anchored match into search-anywhere.

appraiser_note: egrep は拡張正規表現(|・+・?・グループ化)を yacc 文法で直接表現。%left が優先順位を符号化(後置STAR/PLUS/QUESTが最強→CAT→OR)、r r %prec CAT が連接に優先順位を与える。アクションは node/unary/enter で並列配列 name/left/right/parent に構文木を生やす。空規則 b が .*(enter(DOT)+unary(STAR))を前置し、アンカーなし検索(どこでも一致)を実現。grep が手書きパーサだったのに対し yacc に解析を委ねる Bell Labs 流。

ai_analysis.technical_context: The entire program: read 8 bytes (key) + 2 bytes (salt) from stdin, call crypt(), write 13 bytes to stdout. No argument parsing, no error handling, raw read(2)/write(2). A pure byte-transform filter, composable in a pipeline, invoked by editors (ed -x / vi -x) to turn a typed password into an encryption key. read() return values are unchecked: it trusts the caller to send exactly 10 bytes.
ai_analysis.human_story: Nineteen lines, three of them the body. makekey is the Unix filter philosophy at its most distilled: input goes in, transformed output comes out, and the program is just a part you bolt into a pipe. It does not compute crypto itself; it shapes crypt() into a tool.

appraiser_note: プログラム全体: stdin から8バイト(key)+2バイト(salt)を読み、crypt() を呼び、13バイトを stdout に書く。引数解析もエラー処理もなく生の read(2)/write(2)。純粋なバイト変換フィルタで、ed -x / vi -x 等が打ち込まれたパスワードを暗号鍵に変えるために呼ぶ。read の戻り値は未チェック——呼ぶ側が必ず10バイト送る契約に依存。本シリーズ最短級19行(本体3行)。

ai_analysis.technical_context: makekey computes nothing itself; it calls crypt(key, salt) (the one-way DES-based hash from appraisal #79). key is 8 bytes (DES 56-bit key), salt is 2 printable chars = 12 bits = 4096 variations. Salt makes identical passwords hash differently and defeats precomputed tables; the 13-byte output is 2 salt chars + 11 hash chars, embedding the salt so it can be recovered at verification. The salt-prefix format survives all the way to bcrypt's $2b$ strings.
ai_analysis.human_story: The 2-byte salt is the quiet hero: it hides that two users share a password and breaks rainbow tables, and by living in the output it can be read back to re-check. A small idea from 1979 that every password store still uses.

appraiser_note: makekey 自身は計算せず crypt(key, salt)(#79 の一方向DESハッシュ)を呼ぶ。key=8バイト(DES 56bit鍵)、salt=2印字文字=12ビット=4096通り。塩は同一パスワードのハッシュ衝突を隠し事前計算表(レインボーテーブル)を無力化、出力13バイト=塩2文字+ハッシュ11文字で塩を埋め込み検証時に取り出せる。この塩前置形式は bcrypt の $2b$ まで継承される発明。

text:
/*
 * The transformation is expensive to perform
 * (a significant part of a second).
 */

ai_analysis.technical_context: The header comment proudly notes the transform is 'expensive (a significant part of a second)'. crypt ran DES 25 times on purpose. Slowness IS the brute-force defense: a legitimate login waits once, an attacker pays the cost millions of times. This is key stretching / deliberately slow hashing, the idea later formalized in bcrypt (1999), PBKDF2 (2000), scrypt (2009), Argon2 (2015). In computing 'faster is better' is the rule; password hashing is the exception where slower is better.
ai_analysis.human_story: On a 1979 machine, 'a significant part of a second' was enormous. Making it slow was not an apology, it was the feature. The comment declares in two lines a principle the whole security field would spend decades reinventing: for passwords, be slow on purpose.

appraiser_note: ヘッダコメントが変換は『高価(コンマ数秒)』と誇らしげに記す。当時の crypt は DES を意図的に25回反復。遅さこそがブルートフォース耐性——正規ログインは1回待つだけ、攻撃者は何百万回その代償を払う。これが鍵ストレッチング/意図的に遅いハッシュで、bcrypt(1999)/PBKDF2(2000)/scrypt(2009)/Argon2(2015)へ続く思想。『速いほど良い』が常識の計算機で、パスワードハッシュだけは『遅いほど良い』という逆説を1979年に2行で宣言。

ai_analysis.technical_context: newgrp changes the process group with setgid(grp->gr_gid), then done() drops privilege with setuid(getuid()) (newgrp is SUID root so it must drop priv before handing the user a shell), closes fds 3..14, and execl("/bin/sh",...) replaces itself with a fresh shell. exec preserves the gid, so the new shell runs with the new group. This is the gid-changing mirror of su (#104), which changed uid the same way.
ai_analysis.human_story: newgrp does not return; it becomes a new shell with a new group and disappears. The same login/su/newgrp pattern: do the privileged change, carefully drop root, then exec /bin/sh so the change persists in the shell that lives on.

appraiser_note: newgrp は setgid(grp->gr_gid) でプロセスのグループを変え、done() で setuid(getuid()) して特権を捨て(newgrp は SUID root ゆえシェルを渡す前に必ず権限放棄)、fd 3..14 を閉じ、execl("/bin/sh") で自分を新シェルに置換。exec は gid を引き継ぐので新シェルは新グループで動く。uid を変える su(#104) の gid 版の鏡像。login/su/newgrp 共通の『シェルになって去る』パターン。

ai_analysis.technical_context: A nearly forgotten Unix feature: groups in /etc/group could have their own password (gr_passwd). newgrp verifies it the same way as passwd(#103) and makekey(#205): getpass() reads it without echo, crypt(input, gr_passwd) hashes it using the salt extracted from the stored hash. Quirky trigger: only prompted when the group has a password AND the user has no personal password of their own. Group passwords were rarely used and faded away; the second field of /etc/group is usually x or empty today.
ai_analysis.human_story: Almost nobody remembers that groups once had passwords. The mechanism was real in 1979, guarding a narrow path for members who had no password of their own. The empty second field of /etc/group today is the fossil of that forgotten gate.

appraiser_note: ほぼ忘れられた Unix の機能——/etc/group の各グループはパスワード(gr_passwd)を持てた。検証は passwd(#103)/makekey(#205) と同作法: getpass() がエコー無しで読み、crypt(入力, gr_passwd) が保存ハッシュから塩を取り出して照合。発動条件が独特で『グループにパスワードがあり、かつ自分には個人パスワードが無い』ユーザーだけが問われる。グループパスワードはほぼ使われず消え、今日 /etc/group の2番目フィールドが x か空なのはその忘却の化石。

ai_analysis.technical_context: newgrp scans grp->gr_mem[] (the member list from /etc/group) for the user's name. If absent it refuses with 'Sorry' -- unless the group name is "other", which anyone may join (the traditional catch-all group, gid 1). And if getpwuid(getuid()) returns NULL -- the uid has no /etc/passwd entry -- it prints the famous 'You do not exist!'. Same terse refusals as login and su.
ai_analysis.human_story: The error messages have personality. Not a member? 'Sorry.' Running as a uid with no passwd entry? 'You do not exist!' 1979 Unix often refused you with a wink.

appraiser_note: newgrp は grp->gr_mem[](/etc/group のメンバー名配列)を走査して自分のユーザー名を探す。無ければ『Sorry』で拒否——ただしグループ名が "other"(誰でも入れる伝統の受け皿グループ, gid 1)なら免除。そして getpwuid(getuid()) が NULL=uid に passwd エントリが無いと、有名な『You do not exist!』。login/su と同じ素っ気ない拒絶だが、1979年 Unix はしばしばユーモアで突き放した。

ai_analysis.technical_context: There is no chgrp(2) syscall in 1979. chown(path, uid, gid) changes BOTH owner and group at once. To change only the group, chgrp first stat()s the file to read the current owner (stbuf.st_uid), then calls chown(path, stbuf.st_uid, gid): writing the SAME uid back and only the new gid. Same syscall-absence pattern as rm->rmdir, mkdir->mknod, mv->link. status=1 on failure but it keeps going and exit(status) at the end.
ai_analysis.human_story: To change one thing you must read and rewrite another unchanged. chgrp keeps the owner by explicitly handing chown the owner it just read. It is the same quiet trick the whole Bell-32V command set used to fill in a missing syscall with the primitives that did exist.

appraiser_note: 1979年に chgrp(2) システムコールは無い。chown(path, uid, gid) は所有者とグループを同時に変える。グループだけ変えるため chgrp はまず stat() で現在の所有者(stbuf.st_uid)を読み、chown(path, stbuf.st_uid, gid) で同じ uid を書き戻しグループだけ新 gid に。rm→rmdir/mkdir→mknod/mv→link と同じ syscall 不在パターン。失敗で status=1 だが続行し最後に exit(status)。

ai_analysis.technical_context: chgrp accepts either a numeric gid or a group name. isnumber(argv[1]) (a 7-line helper: every char must be isdigit, using the K&R while(c=*s++) idiom) decides; if numeric, atoi(); otherwise getgrnam() looks it up in /etc/group for gr_gid, or 'unknown group' + exit(4). Unlike newgrp (#206) which took only a name, chgrp takes both forms.
ai_analysis.human_story: A tiny hand-rolled isnumber() before reaching for atoi, the small tool sharpened by hand. chgrp meets you halfway: spell the group by number or by name, either works.

appraiser_note: chgrp は数値 gid でもグループ名でも受ける。isnumber(argv[1])(7行ヘルパー: 全文字が isdigit か、K&R の while(c=*s++) 定型)で判定し、数値なら atoi()、名前なら getgrnam() で /etc/group を引いて gr_gid、無ければ unknown group + exit(4)。名前のみだった newgrp(#206) と違い両形式を通す。atoi の前に自作 isnumber を置く小道具自前主義。

ai_analysis.technical_context: newgrp (#206) changes the group of the PROCESS (your shell) via setgid, deciding the group of files you will create. chgrp changes the group of an EXISTING file via chown. Two sides of Unix group ownership: future files vs existing files. chgrp is not SUID; it calls chown(2) raw and lets the kernel enforce permission. The owner+group pair that ls -l (#110) prints is exactly what chown writes and chgrp rewrites half of.
ai_analysis.human_story: newgrp and chgrp are a pair: one sets the group of what you are about to make, the other re-tags what already exists. Beneath both sit chown(2) and /etc/group, the bedrock of Unix group ownership.

appraiser_note: newgrp(#206) は setgid でプロセス(自分のシェル)のグループを変え、これから作るファイルの所属を決める。chgrp は chown で既存ファイルのグループを変える。Unix グループ所有権の両面=これから作るもの vs すでにあるもの。chgrp は SUID でなく chown(2) を素で呼びカーネルが権限判定。ls -l(#110) が表示する所有者+グループの組こそ chown が書き chgrp が片方だけ書き換える対象。

ai_analysis.technical_context: dump takes a level 0-9 (incno). getitime() reads /etc/ddate (struct idates: fs name, level, date) and finds the most recent dump of a STRICTLY LOWER level. The rule: a level-N dump saves everything changed since the most recent dump of level < N. Level 0 has no lower level so c_ddate=0 (epoch) = full backup; higher levels are incremental. putitime() records this dump back to /etc/ddate, but only when the 'u' (update) flag is set (dump 0u). This level model underlies GNU dump/restore, xfsdump, and Towers-of-Hanoi backup schedules to this day.
ai_analysis.human_story: This is where incremental backup was defined. A single number picks how far back to diff, /etc/ddate remembers the past, and the famous 'forgot the u flag' footgun is born right here: no u, no recorded date, broken next increment.

appraiser_note: dump はレベル0-9(incno)を取る。getitime() が /etc/ddate(struct idates: FS名/レベル/日時)を読み、自分より厳密に低いレベルの最新ダンプ日時を探す。規則: レベルNは『level<Nの最新ダンプ以降の変更』を保存。level0は下位が無く c_ddate=0(epoch)=フル、上位は増分。putitime() が今回を /etc/ddate に書き戻すが u(update)フラグ時のみ(dump 0u の u)。このレベルモデルは GNU dump/restore・xfsdump・Towers of Hanoi スケジュールへ継承。u 付け忘れで次の増分が壊れる名物バグの出どころ。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Unlike tar (#192) or cpio (#193) which open and readdir the file tree, dump reads the raw disk device, reads the superblock after sync(), and scans the inode table directly. mark() builds three bitmaps: clrmap (allocated inodes), dirmap (directories), nodmap (to-save: di_mtime/di_ctime >= c_ddate). Reading the FS structure is fast, complete (sees unlinked inodes), and consistent, but tightly coupled to the V7 FS layout mkfs (#201) created.
ai_analysis.human_story: dump does not walk files; it reads the filesystem itself, inode by inode, off the raw device. That is why it is fast and never misses, and why it must know the on-disk format intimately. tar is a portable box of files; dump is the physical diff of one filesystem.

appraiser_note: tar(#192)/cpio(#193) がファイル木を open/readdir するのと違い、dump は生ディスクデバイスを読み、sync() 後にスーパーブロックを読み、inode テーブルを直接走査する。mark() が3枚のビットマップを育てる: clrmap(使用中inode)/dirmap(ディレクトリ)/nodmap(保存対象=di_mtime/di_ctime>=c_ddate)。FS構造直読は速く・漏れず(孤立inodeも見える)・一貫するが、mkfs(#201)が作った V7 FS レイアウトに密結合。tar はポータブルな箱、dump は1つのFSの物理差分。

ai_analysis.technical_context: dump runs four passes over the inode table (the iconic I/II/III/IV output), reusing one higher-order pass(fn,map) scanner. Pass I marks. Pass II is a fixpoint loop: add() / dsrch() promote any directory that contains a to-save inode into nodmap, repeated until nadded==0, because restoring /a/b/c needs directories a and b dumped to provide the name. Reachability climbs from files up to the root. Pass III dumps directories, pass IV dumps file inodes+data.
ai_analysis.human_story: Saving the changed files is not enough; their names live in their parent directories. So dump climbs the tree from each file to the root, one level per pass, until nothing new is added. The four single letters I II III IV that everyone has watched scroll by are four full inode scans.

appraiser_note: dump は inode テーブルを4回走査(名物の I/II/III/IV 出力)し、高階の pass(fn,map) を使い回す。I=印付け。II=不動点ループ: add()/dsrch() が保存対象inodeを含むディレクトリを nodmap に格上げし nadded==0 まで反復——/a/b/c の復元には名前を与える a,b のディレクトリも要るため。到達可能性がファイルから根へ這い上がる。III=ディレクトリ書出し、IV=ファイルinode+データ書出し。

ai_analysis.technical_context: The dump tape format is self-checking: spclrec sets c_magic=MAGIC and c_checksum = CHECKSUM - s so the whole control record sums to the constant CHECKSUM; restor verifies by summing. Record types TS_TAPE/TS_INODE/TS_BITS/TS_ADDR/TS_CLRI/TS_END live in dumprestor.h, shared with restor. flusht() queues disk addresses (tapsrec) and reads them in ascending order (pick the smallest tdaddr each iteration via goto loop) -- an elevator/SCAN seek optimization to read the slow 1979 disk in one sweep. When asize>tsize it prints 'change tapes' and read(0,...) waits for the operator: multi-volume backup.
ai_analysis.human_story: Tape is unreliable, so every record carries a magic number and a checksum that makes the whole block sum to a constant. And to drag data off a slow disk, dump sorts the blocks it needs by address and reads them in one forward sweep of the head. Even multi-tape backups, with a pause for the operator to swap reels, are already here.

appraiser_note: dump のテープ形式は自己検査式: spclrec が c_magic=MAGIC と c_checksum=CHECKSUM-s を置き、制御レコード全体の総和が定数 CHECKSUM になる(restor は総和で検証)。レコード種別 TS_TAPE/TS_INODE/TS_BITS/TS_ADDR/TS_CLRI/TS_END は dumprestor.h で restor と共有。flusht() はディスクアドレスをキューし(tapsrec)、毎回最小の tdaddr を選んで昇順に読む(goto loop)——エレベーター法(SCAN)で遅い1979年ディスクを片方向一掃。asize>tsize で『change tapes』表示し read(0,...) でオペレーター交換待ち=マルチボリューム。

ai_analysis.technical_context: restor's r mode restores a whole dump onto a raw filesystem device, so it reimplements the kernel filesystem in userspace: balloc()/bfree() manipulate the superblock free list (s_free[]/s_nfree chain, the very structure mkfs #201 created), bmap() maps logical block numbers to physical addresses allocating direct/single/double/triple indirect blocks, getdino/putdino read/write the inode table directly, dwrite/dread do cached raw disk I/O. Before doing so it prints the famous 'Last chance before scribbling on <disk>.' and waits for a newline -- the one place 1979 Unix stops to confirm.
ai_analysis.human_story: rm -rf / would not stop you, but restor does: 'Last chance before scribbling.' Because what follows is not copying files, it is writing inodes and blocks straight onto the raw disk, rebuilding a filesystem by hand. restor carries a tiny filesystem driver inside it.

appraiser_note: restor の r モードは dump 全体を生ファイルシステムデバイスに復元するため、カーネルのFSをユーザー空間に再実装する: balloc()/bfree() がスーパーブロックの空きリスト(s_free[]/s_nfree チェーン=mkfs #201 が作った構造)を操作、bmap() が論理ブロック番号を物理アドレスに変換し直接/単/二重/三重間接を確保、getdino/putdino が inode テーブルを直書き、dwrite/dread がキャッシュ付き生ディスクI/O。その前に名物『Last chance before scribbling on <disk>.』を出し改行待ち——1979年Unixが唯一立ち止まって確認する場所。

ai_analysis.technical_context: restor is the exact reading counterpart of dump (#208). dump set c_checksum = CHECKSUM - s so a control record sums to the constant CHECKSUM; restor's checksum() sums the block and verifies it equals CHECKSUM, plus c_magic == MAGIC. gethead/ishead/checktype/checkvol/readhdr/readbits mirror dump's spclrec/taprec/bitmap, all sharing the tape format via dumprestor.h. Record types TS_TAPE/TS_INODE/TS_BITS/TS_CLRI/TS_ADDR/TS_END are written by dump and read by restor.
ai_analysis.human_story: dump and restor are one program split across a tape, bound by a single header file. One signs each record with a checksum; the other verifies the sum is the constant they agreed on. Backup is a round trip, and the checksum is what makes the trip survivable over unreliable tape.

appraiser_note: restor は dump(#208) の正確な読み手側。dump は c_checksum=CHECKSUM-s で制御レコードの総和を定数 CHECKSUM にし、restor の checksum() は総和が CHECKSUM か(+c_magic==MAGIC)を検証。gethead/ishead/checktype/checkvol/readhdr/readbits が dump の spclrec/taprec/bitmap と対をなし、dumprestor.h でテープ形式を共有。TS_TAPE/TS_INODE/TS_BITS/TS_CLRI/TS_ADDR/TS_END を dump が書き restor が読む。バックアップは往復で、チェックサムが信頼できないテープ越しの往復を成立させる。

ai_analysis.technical_context: dump records unallocated blocks (holes) as c_addr[i]==0. restor's getfile() distinguishes them: nonzero -> readtape real data and f1 (rstrfile) bmap-allocates and writes; zero -> f2 (rstrskip) only advances curbno WITHOUT allocating, so the restored file keeps its holes. Sparse files are reconstructed faithfully rather than filled with zeros. Extract mode uses xtrfile (write) and skip (lseek over the hole, which the FS turns into a hole). One getfile() does both raw-restore and file-extract via swappable f1/f2, mirroring dump's icat.
ai_analysis.human_story: A hole stays a hole. If restor filled gaps with zeros, a huge sparse file would devour the disk. By advancing without allocating, it brings the file back exactly as sparse as it left. The same getfile, given different callbacks, restores to raw disk or extracts to a file, holes intact.

appraiser_note: dump は未割当ブロック(穴)を c_addr[i]==0 で記録。restor の getfile() はこれを区別: 非ゼロ→readtape で実データ+f1(rstrfile)が bmap で確保して書く、ゼロ→f2(rstrskip)が確保せず curbno だけ進める=復元後も穴のまま。スパースファイルをゼロ埋めせず忠実に再現。抽出モードは xtrfile(write)と skip(lseek で飛ばす→FSが穴にする)。1つの getfile() が f1/f2 差替で生復元とファイル抽出の両方を穴を保ったままこなす——dump の icat の鏡像。

ai_analysis.technical_context: For 'restor x /etc/passwd' there is no live filesystem to resolve the name against -- only the tape. pass1() scans the tape, and for each directory inode writes its entries into a temp file (rstXXXXXX) via putdir/putent while recording inode->seekpt in inotab[]. This rebuilds a miniature directory structure on disk from scattered tape data. Then psearch() resolves a pathname from root inode 2, walking each component via search() (inotab lookup + getent of directory entries). restor reconstructs the kernel's namei in userspace, from tape alone.
ai_analysis.human_story: To pull one file out by name, restor first rebuilds a tiny filesystem's directories from the tape, then walks paths through it from the root. It recreates name resolution itself, with no live disk to ask -- the only map it has is the one it just built from the backup.

appraiser_note: restor x /etc/passwd では名前を照合する生FSが無く、あるのはテープだけ。pass1() がテープを舐め、各ディレクトリ inode の中身を一時ファイル rstXXXXXX に putdir/putent で書き、inotab[] に inode→seekpt を記録——散らばったテープのディレクトリ情報からミニチュアのディレクトリ構造をディスク上に再構築。psearch() がルート inode 2 から各成分を search()(inotab 引き+getent)でたどりパス名を解決。restor はカーネルの namei をテープだけからユーザー空間に再現する。

ai_analysis.technical_context: cb is a C beautifier (code formatter), the ancestor of indent/gofmt/clang-format/prettier. It parses no grammar and builds no AST: it reads one char at a time and reacts in a giant switch(c). '{' does tabs++ (deeper indent), '}' does tabs--, ';' ends a statement, '(' ')' track paren depth so for(;;) semicolons are not mistaken for statement ends, and string/char/comment literals are copied verbatim. A heuristic one-pass stream formatter -- not a strict parser, but good enough for ordinary C.
ai_analysis.human_story: Long before gofmt, a program reformatted source code by reacting to punctuation. cb does not understand C; it just watches for braces and semicolons and indents accordingly. The whole idea that a machine should tidy your code starts here, in 357 lines that never parse a thing.

appraiser_note: cb は C beautifier(コード整形ツール)で indent/gofmt/clang-format/prettier の祖先。文法を解析せず AST も作らない: 1文字ずつ読み巨大な switch(c) で反応。'{' で tabs++(字下げ深く)、'}' で tabs--、';' で文を区切り、'(' ')' で括弧深度を追って for(;;) の ; を文末と誤認しない、文字列/文字/コメントは中身をそのままコピー。ヒューリスティックな1パスのストリーム整形——厳密なパーサではないが普通のCには実用十分。

ai_analysis.technical_context: The hardest part of formatting C is brace-less control statements. 'if (x) y();' has no braces yet y() must indent one level; 'else' must dedent back to its if. cb tracks this invisible level with pflg[level] (pending indent) and ind[level], and aligns else by saving if-context (tabs/pflg/ind) into 2D arrays stabs/spflg/sind indexed by [clevel][iflev] -- a stack keyed on brace-level and if-nesting. On '(' after 'if' it pushes; gotelse() pops and restores so else lines up with its matching if. It approximates the dangling-else that a grammar resolves, by hand, with array save/restore.
ai_analysis.human_story: Braces are easy: just count them. The pain is when there are none -- the dangling else, the one-line if body. cb wrestles this with a two-axis stack of if-contexts, re-aligning each else to the if it belongs to. The same problem gofmt later dodged by forcing braces, and prettier solved with a real AST.

appraiser_note: Cの整形の最難所は波括弧なしの制御文。if (x) y(); は波括弧が無いのに y() を1段下げ、else は if の高さに戻す。cb はこの見えない段を pflg[level](保留インデント)と ind[level] で追い、else 整列は if 文脈(tabs/pflg/ind)を2次元配列 stabs/spflg/sind の [clevel][iflev](ブレースレベル×ifネスト深度のスタック)に退避して解く。'(' で直前が if ならpush、gotelse() でpop・復元し else を対応する if に揃える。文法解析が解く dangling else を、配列の退避と復元という手作業で近似。

ai_analysis.technical_context: cb special-cases the keywords if/else/for/case/default via lookup(), which compares the accumulated string[] against keyword tables (wif/welse/wfor/wds) and checks the next char is not lowercase -- a word-boundary test so 'ifx' is not read as 'if'. The cleverest case is 'for': for(i=0;i<n;i++) has two semicolons that would falsely break the statement, so on '(' after 'for' it uses gets() to swallow the whole for(...) controlling the paren balance with ct, leaving those semicolons untouched. Literals and comments are passed through verbatim so their inner ; { } do not trigger formatting.
ai_analysis.human_story: cb knows just five keywords, and recognizes them with a hand-rolled word-boundary check. Its neatest trick is eating the entire for(...) header so the semicolons inside never fool it. It does not know C grammar, but it knows C tokens.

appraiser_note: cb は if/else/for/case/default の5キーワードだけ特別扱いし、lookup() が蓄積 string[] をキーワード表(wif/welse/wfor/wds)と比較、直後の文字が小文字でない(単語境界)かを確認——'ifx' を 'if' と誤認しない素朴だが正しいチェック。圧巻は for: for(i=0;i<n;i++) の2つの ; が文を誤って区切るため、'(' で直前が for なら gets() で for(...) を括弧対応(ct)を取りつつ丸ごと読み飛ばし、その ; を触らせない。文字列/コメントは中身を素通しし内部の ;{} に反応しない。文法は知らずとも字句(トークン)は押さえる。

ai_analysis.technical_context: number converts digits to English words (123 -> 'one hundred twenty three'). English numerals are irregular, so it bakes the irregularity into four string tables: unit (one-nine), teen (ten-nineteen, a special set when the tens digit is 1), decade (twenty-ninety), and card (scale words hundred/thousand/million up to vigintillion = 10^63). ones()/tens() index them via d-'0'. The card table reaching 10^63 is gloriously excessive for any real use -- the small-tool completeness Unix loved.
ai_analysis.human_story: English numbers are not regular, so number does not try to compute them; it lists them. And then it lists scale words all the way to vigintillion, 10^63, a number nobody would ever spell aloud. That little excess, complete just because it could be, is the charm of a Unix toy.

appraiser_note: number は数字を英単語に変換(123→'one hundred twenty three')。英語数詞は不規則なので4つの文字列テーブルに焼き込む: unit(one-nine)、teen(ten-nineteen=10の位が1のときの特別集合)、decade(twenty-ninety)、card(規模語 hundred/thousand/million…vigintillion=10^63)。ones()/tens() が d-'0' で添字。card が 10^63 まで届くのは実用上過剰だが、Unixが愛した小道具の完全主義。不規則は規則化せず表に並べる(tr/bcd と同じ流儀)。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Numbers are read in 3-digit groups, each with a scale word (million/thousand/...). number normalizes the digit count by padding two '0's in front so the top group is always 3 digits and r=i/3 gives a clean group count regardless of length. conv(p,c) builds the words recursively: for huge numbers (c>max=21) it recurses on the high part then appends the scale word; otherwise while(c>1) it converts each 3-digit group (conv(p,1)) followed by its scale word and advances p+=3. The innermost conv(p,1) does ones(hundreds) + 'hundred' + tens + ones(units). Arbitrary length, by chunking and recursion.
ai_analysis.human_story: People read big numbers three digits at a time. number does too, padding the front with two zeros so the math always lands on a group boundary, then recursing group by group, appending thousand, million, and on. A clean recursive spine that can spell a number of any length.

appraiser_note: 数は3桁ずつ規模語付きで読む(million/thousand…)。number は先頭に '0' を2つ詰めて最上位グループを必ず3桁にし、r=i/3 で桁数によらずグループ数をきれいに出す正規化トリック。conv(p,c) が再帰で組む: 巨大数(c>max=21)は高位を conv(p,c-max) で再帰後に規模語、通常は while(c>1) で各3桁グループ conv(p,1)+規模語、p+=3 で次へ。最深 conv(p,1) が ones(百)+'hundred'+tens+ones(1の位)。チャンク化と再帰で任意桁。

ai_analysis.technical_context: When the tens digit is 1, tens() prints a teen word (thirteen) for both digits at once -- but conv() then calls ones(p[2]) which would add 'three', giving 'thirteen three'. So tens() destructively writes p[2]='0' into the input buffer, making the later ones('0') print nothing. A side effect on the buffer communicates 'these two digits are already spoken'. Output spacing is handled by one global flag: print() adds a leading space when flag is set; cprint() prints a scale word only if something was already printed (skips empty 000 groups); nline() emits a '.' separator and resets flag.
ai_analysis.human_story: thirteen already contains the three, so number reaches into its own buffer and erases the ones digit to stop it being spoken twice. A small destructive trick, and a single flag bit that quietly handles spacing, empty groups, and separators all at once.

appraiser_note: 10の位が1のとき tens() は teen語(thirteen)で2桁分をまとめて印字するが、conv() は続けて ones(p[2]) を呼び 'three' まで出て 'thirteen three' になる。そこで tens() は入力バッファに p[2]='0' を破壊的に書き込み、続く ones('0') を無印字にする。バッファへの副作用で『この2桁は綴り済み』を下流に伝える。出力間隔はグローバル flag 一つ: print() は flag 時に前スペース、cprint() は何か出した後だけ規模語(空の000グループをスキップ)、nline() は '.' 区切りで flag リセット。

ai_analysis.technical_context: sum computes a 16-bit checksum, but before adding each byte it rotates the running sum right by one bit (sum&01 carried into bit15 via +0x8000). Why: a plain additive sum is order-independent (A+B+C == C+A+B), so it cannot detect byte transposition -- the most common transfer corruption. Rotating before each add makes a byte's contribution depend on its position, turning an order-blind total into a position-sensitive fingerprint. The rotate (not a plain >>1 which would lose the bit) keeps all information while stirring it. Five lines are the essence; the rest is scaffolding.
ai_analysis.human_story: Addition does not care about order, so a naive checksum cannot tell ABC from CBA -- exactly the transposition that tape and modems produce. One bit of rotation before each add fixes it: now position matters, and the sum becomes a fingerprint instead of a total.

appraiser_note: sum は16ビットチェックサムを計算するが、各バイトを足す前に走行和を1ビット右回転(sum&01 を +0x8000 で bit15 へ)。理由: 単純加算和は順序非依存(A+B+C==C+A+B)でバイト転置(転送で最頻の化け)を検出できない。足す前に回すとバイトの寄与が位置に依存し、順序を見ない『合計』が位置依存の『指紋』に変わる。単なる >>1 ではビットが消えるが、回転なら情報を保ったまま撹拌。5行が本質、残りは足場。

ai_analysis.technical_context: sum prints two numbers: a 5-digit zero-padded checksum (%05u) and a block count (%6ld), computed as the ceiling division (nbytes+BUFSIZ-1)/BUFSIZ -- the same round-up idiom dump (#208) used. Both matter for integrity: the checksum verifies content, the block count verifies length (a truncated file may collide on checksum but differ in size). It handles multiple files and stdin, names the file when argc>2, and accumulates errors into errflg (+10 open, +1 read) returned via exit(errflg).
ai_analysis.human_story: Content and length, side by side. A checksum alone can be fooled by a truncated transfer, so sum also prints how many blocks it saw. You compared the two numbers at each end of the tape or modem link and trusted the copy only if both matched.

appraiser_note: sum は2つの数を印字: 5桁ゼロ詰めチェックサム(%05u)とブロック数(%6ld=(nbytes+BUFSIZ-1)/BUFSIZ の切り上げ除算、dump #208 と同じ定番)。両方が整合性に効く: チェックサムは中身、ブロック数は長さを検証(切れたファイルは偶然衝突しても長さが違う)。複数ファイル/stdin対応、argc>2 で名前を付け、エラーを errflg(+10 open/+1 read)に集約し exit(errflg)。

ai_analysis.technical_context: sum is the root of file-integrity verification. Its 16-bit rotate-sum has limits: only 65536 values so collisions happen, and it gives no protection against deliberate tampering. Integrity checking then split two ways: stronger error detection -- BSD sum (this rotate version) vs System V sum (a different algorithm) caused incompatibility, later standardized by POSIX cksum (CRC-32); and tamper detection -- one-way cryptographic hashes md5sum/sha1sum/sha256sum (the lineage of #79 crypt, #205 makekey). Contrast dump/restor's checksum (#208/#209), a plain additive sum to a constant CHECKSUM for tape-record corruption; sum's rotate also catches order.
ai_analysis.human_story: From one little rotating sum grew CRC and then the cryptographic hashes that sign every download. The same 1979 Unix already held several checksums for different jobs; sum was the simplest, and the seed of all of them.

appraiser_note: sum はファイル整合性検証の根。16ビット回転和には限界(65536通りで衝突、改竄耐性なし)。整合性検証はここから二方向に進化: 誤り検出強化=BSD sum(この回転版) vs SysV sum(別アルゴリズム)の非互換→POSIX cksum(CRC-32)で標準化、改竄検出=一方向暗号ハッシュ md5sum/sha256sum(#79 crypt/#205 makekey の系譜)。dump/restor の checksum(#208/#209、定数 CHECKSUM への加算和でテープ破損検出)との対比——sum の回転は順序まで見る。

ai_analysis.technical_context: ptx builds a KWIC (Key Word In Context) permuted index. Its core trick: for each line, rotate it so the keyword leads -- write 'keyword-onward TILDE pre-keyword-part'. TILDE (0177, the byte just below printable range) is the pivot: when the rotated lines are sorted, the keyword-onward part is at the front and sorts alphabetically, while the wrapped-around prefix hides after TILDE and does not affect the key. One delimiter byte makes 'sort by keyword while keeping surrounding context' work. A 2D index becomes a set of rotated 1D lines.
ai_analysis.human_story: An index is the same line repeated once per keyword, each rotated to lead with a different word. ptx encodes that as a rotation around a single TILDE: sort puts the keywords in order, and the context simply wraps around behind the pivot.

appraiser_note: ptx は KWIC(Key Word In Context)順列索引を作る。核心: 各行をキーワードが先頭に来るよう回転し『キーワード以降 TILDE キーワード前』と書く。TILDE(0177=印字可能直前のバイト)が折り返し点で、回転後の行をソートすると『キーワード以降』が行頭でアルファベット順に並び、回り込んだ前半は TILDE の後ろに隠れソートキーに影響しない。1文字の区切りで『キーワードで並べつつ文脈を保持』を両立。二次元の索引を回転した一次元の行の集合に変換。

ai_analysis.technical_context: A good index omits common words (the, and, of). ptx reads a stop-word file, default /usr/lib/eign (McIlroy's list of the most frequent English words). It matches words against a self-built hash table: hash() multiplies the first two and last two letters, shifts and XORs into MASK (2047); storeh() is open-addressing with linear probing that wraps from end to start. One hash lookup decides if a word is a stop word, leaving only meaningful terms as index entries. Same 'match many words fast' instinct McIlroy used with a Bloom filter in spell (#85).
ai_analysis.human_story: An index of every 'the' is useless, so ptx hashes a list of the commonest English words and skips them. The hash table is hand-built, multiplicative, open-addressed -- McIlroy's signature move: pick the data structure that makes the lookup disappear.

appraiser_note: 良い索引は the/and/of を載せない。ptx はストップワードファイル(既定 /usr/lib/eign=McIlroy がまとめた英語頻出語リスト)を読む。語の照合は自作ハッシュ表: hash() が先頭2文字と末尾2文字を掛けシフト+XORで MASK(2047)に収め、storeh() は末尾から先頭へ巡回する線形探査のオープンアドレス法。ハッシュ1回でストップワード判定し、意味語だけを索引語に残す。spell(#85) で Bloom filter を使ったのと同じ『大量の語を高速照合する』McIlroy の十八番。

ai_analysis.technical_context: ptx contains no sort code. It writes the rotated lines to a temp file /tmp/ptxsXXXXX, fork()s, and execl()s /usr/bin/sort (#87) to do the sorting (+0 -1 +1 select the key, -o writes back). This is the Unix philosophy: one program does one thing and calls another when needed, like rm->rmdir (#202), bc->dc (#71). Sorting is hard (external merge sort, memory), but that is sort's specialty; ptx avoids reinventing it and inherits sort's 50 years of improvement. The -f (fold) flag even switches the sortopt passed to the child.
ai_analysis.human_story: ptx does not sort; it asks sort to. A few lines of fork+exec let it skip the hardest part of the job and lean on a tool already perfected for it. Compose, do not duplicate -- the whole point of Unix.

appraiser_note: ptx はソートコードを1行も持たない。回転した行を一時ファイル /tmp/ptxsXXXXX に書き、fork()して /usr/bin/sort(#87) を execl で呼びソートを丸投げ(+0 -1 +1 でキー指定、-o で書き戻し)。Unix哲学そのもの: 一つのプログラムは一つをうまくやり必要なら他を呼ぶ(rm→rmdir #202、bc→dc #71)。ソートは難問(外部マージ・メモリ管理)だが sort の専門であり、ptx は再発明を避け sort の50年の進化を享受。-f(fold)フラグは子へ渡す sortopt も切り替える。

ai_analysis.technical_context: After sort, getsort finds the TILDE and reassembles the rotation into a readable index line: keyword centered in a fixed-width line, context split left/right via rtrim()/ltrim() (trim to half-width without breaking word boundaries, append '/' to mark truncation), emitted as a troff '.xx ".." ".." ".." ".."' macro -- final typesetting left to troff (#82), like tbl/eqn/pic. The whole design (rotate / sort / format / hash) is modular with minimal contracts (TILDE, temp file). KWIC is exactly the example David Parnas used in his 1972 paper 'On the Criteria To Be Used in Decomposing Systems into Modules' to teach information hiding; ptx is the Unix answer: decompose with processes, pipes, and files.
ai_analysis.human_story: ptx splits 'make an index' into rotate, sort, format, each blind to the others. That is not incidental: KWIC is the very example Parnas chose to define information hiding. McIlroy's tool is that software-engineering principle as running code.

appraiser_note: ソート後、getsort が TILDE を見つけ回転を読みやすい索引行に組み直す: 固定幅行の中央にキーワード、前後の文脈を rtrim()/ltrim()(語境界を壊さず半行幅に切り詰め、溢れたら '/' で truncation 表示)で左右に振り分け、troff の .xx ".." マクロとして出力——最終組版は troff(#82) に委譲(tbl/eqn/pic と同じ)。全体(回転/ソート/整形/ハッシュ)がモジュール化され、TILDE と一時ファイルという最小の契約だけで繋がる。KWIC索引は David Parnas 1972年の名論文『On the Criteria To Be Used in Decomposing Systems into Modules』で情報隠蔽を説いた題材そのもの。ptx はその Unix流の答え=プロセスとパイプとファイルで分解する。

text:
if(c1=='E' && c2=='Q') eqn();		/* .EQ -> skip equation */
else if(c1=='T' && (c2=='S'||c2=='C'||c2=='&')) tbl();	/* .TS -> skip table */
else if(!inmacro && c1=='d' && c2=='e') macro();	/* .de -> skip macro def */
else if(c1=='s' && c2=='o') { getfname(); ... }	/* .so -> include */

ai_analysis.technical_context: deroff strips troff/eqn/tbl formatting to leave plain prose -- the only 'reverse' tool in the troff family. Where troff (#82), eqn (#81), tbl (#83), pic (#84) GENERATE troff commands, deroff REMOVES them, so 'deroff file | spell' or 'deroff | wc' work on the text without formatting noise. comline() dispatches on the two request letters: .EQ->eqn() (skip to .EN), .TS->tbl() (skip to .TE), .de/.ig/.am->macro() (skip the body), .so->include. It knows the troff family's grammar inside out, in reverse.
ai_analysis.human_story: The whole troff family builds formatting up; deroff is the one tool that tears it back down. To strip .EQ equations and .TS tables correctly it must know exactly how eqn and tbl wrote them -- the generator's mirror image.

appraiser_note: deroff は troff/eqn/tbl の組版を剥がして素の文章を残す——組版一族で唯一の『逆』ツール。troff(#82)/eqn(#81)/tbl(#83)/pic(#84) が troff 命令を生成するのに対し deroff は除去し、deroff file | spell や deroff | wc が組版ノイズ無しで効く。comline() がコマンド2文字でディスパッチ: .EQ→eqn()(.ENまで飛ばす)、.TS→tbl()(.TEまで)、.de/.ig/.am→macro()(本体を飛ばす)、.so→インクルード。troff一族の文法を逆から知り尽くしている。

text:
/* All input is through the C macro; the most recently read character is in c. */
#define C ( (c=getc(infile)) == EOF ? eof() : ((c==ldelim)&&(filesp==files) ? skeqn() : c) )

ai_analysis.technical_context: Every read in deroff goes through one macro C, not raw getc. C does three jobs at once: getc one char; on EOF call eof() (close current file, pop the .so file stack filesp, or open the next argument file); and if the char is the inline-equation left delimiter ldelim ($), call skeqn() to skip the $...$ math. So 'read a character' silently handles file boundaries and inline equations; callers just write C. Centralizing the read policy in one macro keeps every getc-site from repeating EOF and equation checks -- Kernighan using the C preprocessor as a container for policy, like troff's PAIR macro.
ai_analysis.human_story: deroff reads the whole world through a single macro. File switches and inline math vanish into the act of reading, so the rest of the code never has to think about them. One #define carries the entire input policy.

appraiser_note: deroff の全入力は生 getc でなく1つのマクロ C を通る。C は3仕事を同時に: getc で1文字読む、EOF なら eof()(現ファイルを閉じ .so スタック filesp を戻すか次の引数ファイルを開く)、文字がインライン数式の左デリミタ ldelim($)なら skeqn() で $...$ を飛ばす。『1文字読む』にファイル境界とインライン数式の処理が織り込まれ、呼ぶ側は C と書くだけ。読み取りポリシーを1マクロに集約し、各 getc 箇所が EOF・数式判定を繰り返さずに済む——troff の PAIR マクロと同じ、Cプリプロセッサをポリシーの容れ物に使う Kernighan 流。

ai_analysis.technical_context: troff documents split across files via .so (source/include) and .nx (next file). deroff follows them faithfully: .so pushes the current file on files[] (up to 15 deep) and opens the target; eof() pops back. The clever guard is loop detection: getfname() records each opened filename in a linked list namechain and strcmp-checks it; a file seen before is set to '' and never reopened, preventing infinite .so a -> .so a recursion. Same 'remember what you have seen' structure as refer's hash (#86) and ptx's inotab (#213).
ai_analysis.human_story: Following includes is easy; not looping forever is the hard part. deroff keeps a linked list of every file it has opened and refuses to open one twice -- expanding the document like real troff, but smarter about the trap troff itself can fall into.

appraiser_note: troff 文書は .so(source/include)と .nx(next file)で複数ファイルに分かれる。deroff は忠実に辿る: .so は現ファイルを files[] に積み(最大15段)対象を開き、eof() が戻る。巧妙なのはループ検出: getfname() が開いたファイル名を連結リスト namechain に記録し strcmp 照合、既読なら '' にして二度と開かず .so a→.so a の無限再帰を防ぐ。refer のハッシュ(#86)や ptx の inotab(#213)と同じ『見たものを覚える』構造。

ai_analysis.technical_context: backsl() strips troff backslash constructs one by one: \" (comment to EOL), \f/\n/\* (two-char font/register/string), \(xx (special char), \h/\v/\w/\l (delimiter-bracketed motions), recursing on nested backslashes. Each construct has its own skip rule, inverting troff's interpretation. After all formatting is removed, -w runs putwords() to emit one word per line, classifying chars via chars[] (LETTER/DIGIT/APOS/SPECIAL) and accepting runs with >=2 letters (comment: 'MDM definition of word'), trimming trailing ' and &. The point: 'deroff doc | spell' (#85) checks only pure words, undisturbed by formatting. That bridge is deroff's reason to exist.
ai_analysis.human_story: Troff hides format commands inside the prose as backslash escapes; deroff dismantles each one, recursing into nested ones. Strip the formatting, split into words, hand the bare text to spell and wc. deroff exists to turn a typeset document back into mere language.

appraiser_note: backsl() は troff のバックスラッシュ構文を1つずつ剥がす: \"(行末までのコメント)、\f/\n/\*(2文字フォント/レジスタ/文字列)、\(xx(特殊文字)、\h/\v/\w/\l(デリミタ付きモーション)、入れ子は再帰。各構文に固有の除去規則=troff 解釈の逆。組版を剥がし終えると -w が putwords() で1行1語に分解、chars[] で文字分類(LETTER/DIGIT/APOS/SPECIAL)し2字以上の英字連なりを語と認め(コメント 'MDM definition of word')末尾の '・& を落とす。狙いは deroff doc | spell(#85) が組版に邪魔されず純粋な単語だけ検査すること。この橋渡しが deroff の存在理由。

ai_analysis.technical_context: col flattens the up-and-down head motion in nroff/troff output for forward-only devices (line printers, dumb terminals). tbl aligns columns, eqn places super/subscripts, .2C does multi-column -- all emit reverse feeds: ESC-7 (reverse full line), ESC-8 (reverse half), ESC-9 (forward half), RLF (\013). col's line counter ll runs in HALF-lines (\n = incr twice); the 'half' variable accumulates half-line shifts and only triggers a real line move when it exceeds +-1, positioning superscripts precisely in an integer line model. -f keeps half-line motions for capable devices; otherwise rounds to full lines.
ai_analysis.human_story: Typesetters move the paper up to stack exponents and align tables. A cheap printer cannot go back. col reads those reverse feeds and rebuilds the page so it can flow strictly downward, even tracking half-lines so x squared lands where it should.

appraiser_note: col は nroff/troff 出力の上下ヘッド移動を前進専用装置(ラインプリンタ/単純端末)向けに平坦化する。tbl の桁揃え、eqn の上付き下付き、.2C の多段組みが逆送りを出す: ESC-7(全行逆)/ESC-8(半行逆)/ESC-9(半行前)/RLF(\013)。col の行カウンタ ll は半行単位(\n で incr 2回)、half 変数が半行ずれを溜め±1超で実際の行移動を起こし整数行モデルで上付きを正確に位置づける。-f は半行モーションを残し、無ければ全行に丸める。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Because reverse motion writes onto past lines, col must keep several lines in memory, not just the current one: page[256] (256 half-lines = 128 lines), used as a ring via ll % PL. When a line scrolls above the window (older than mustwr), reverse feeds can no longer reach it, so col emit()s and free()s it. store/fetch swap the current line buffer lbuff with page[]. This lets col handle documents of any height with only a 128-line window -- reverse motion only reaches the near past (table alignment, exponents span a few lines), so a fixed sliding window streams the whole document.
ai_analysis.human_story: Reverse feeds only ever reach back a few lines, so col keeps a 128-line window and slides it forward, flushing lines once nothing can move back to them. A document of any height fits through a fixed-size buffer.

appraiser_note: 逆行モーションは過去の行に書き込むため、col は現在行だけでなく近傍数行をメモリ保持: page[256](256半行=128行)を ll % PL でリング使用。窓より上に出た行(mustwr より古い)は逆送りで戻れないので emit() して free()。store/fetch が現在行 lbuff と page[] を出し入れ。これで任意の高さの文書を128行ぶんの窓だけで処理——逆行は近い過去(桁揃え・指数は数行)にしか戻らないので固定窓を滑らせストリーム処理。sort の N-way マージや dump のリングバッファと同じ節約術。

ai_analysis.technical_context: When two characters land on the same line and column -- underline (_ over a letter), bold (same char twice), accents -- troff expresses it as char/reverse/char overstrike. col's outc() weaves a literal '\b' (backspace) into the line buffer (a\bc), encoding 'these two characters overlap' inside a 1D line; on emit the backspace is sent so the head returns and the overstrike is reproduced. -b drops overstriking and keeps only the last char (for devices that cannot backspace). A 2D overlap folded into 1D via one delimiter byte, like ptx's TILDE (#213).
ai_analysis.human_story: Bold and underline are two characters in the same spot. col records that by literally inserting a backspace between them, so the line stays one-dimensional yet the printer's head goes back and strikes twice. One byte carries the overlap.

appraiser_note: 同じ行・同じ桁に2文字が来るとき(下線=_を文字に重ねる、太字=同字2回、アクセント)、troff は 文字/逆送り/文字 の重ね打ちで表す。col の outc() は行バッファに '\b'(backspace) を物理的に織り込み(a\bc)、『この2文字は重なる』を一次元の行に保存、emit 時に backspace が送られヘッドが戻り重ね打ちを再現。-b は重ね打ちを諦め最後の文字だけ残す(backspace 不可装置向け)。二次元の重なりを区切り1文字で一次元に畳む、ptx の TILDE(#213) と同じ流儀。

ai_analysis.technical_context: tabs sets a hardcopy terminal's PHYSICAL tab stops, the way you would on a typewriter. On 1970s daisy-wheel printers (DASI 300/450), Teletype 37, HP 2645, a tab stop was a physical position. ESC '1' (SET) drops a stop at the carriage's current position, so tabs(n) prints 8 spaces to move the head 8 columns, then SET, repeated n times -- walking the carriage and driving a peg every 8 columns. margset/margin handle the physical left-margin offset. Tab stops were mechanical positions, not a numeric setting.
ai_analysis.human_story: Setting a tab was a physical act: move the carriage to the column and press 'set tab', exactly like a typewriter. tabs reproduces that by printing eight spaces and then an escape, again and again, walking the print head across the paper.

appraiser_note: tabs はハードコピー端末の物理的なタブストップを、タイプライターのように設定する。1970年代のデイジーホイール(DASI 300/450)/Teletype 37/HP 2645 では、タブストップは物理的な停止位置。ESC '1'(SET) がキャリッジの現在位置に停止を刻むので、tabs(n) は8スペースを打ってヘッドを8マス進め SET、を n 回——キャリッジを歩かせ8マスごとに杭を打つ。margset/margin が物理的な左マージンを扱う。タブストップは数値設定でなく機械上の位置だった。

ai_analysis.technical_context: delay(n) sends n DEL (0177) characters, which most terminals treat as no-ops. tabs uses them purely as a timing pad: a mechanical operation like 'clear all tabs' takes real physical time as gears and solenoids move, and sending the next byte too soon makes the terminal drop it. So DELs are sent to wait -- each DEL's transmission time is the delay. The slower the terminal, the more DELs: tty37 uses clear(40), dasi450 uses clear(8). The classic fill-character technique: software pacing itself to the machine's physical speed, like troff vs the CAT phototypesetter and getty vs baud rate.
ai_analysis.human_story: The terminal is a machine, and clearing tabs takes time the gears need. So tabs sends a string of do-nothing DEL bytes just to wait, counting them out to match how slow the machine is. Code pacing itself to hardware, one filler byte at a time.

appraiser_note: delay(n) は DEL(0177) を n 個送る——多くの端末で no-op の文字。tabs はこれを純粋な時間稼ぎに使う: 『全タブ消去』のような機械動作は歯車やソレノイドが動く物理時間がかかり、次のバイトを早く送ると端末が取りこぼす。だから DEL を送って待つ——DEL 1個の送信時間がそのまま遅延。遅い端末ほど多く: tty37 は clear(40)、dasi450 は clear(8)。古典のフィル文字技法で、ソフトが機械の物理速度に合わせる(troff と CAT 写植機、getty とボーレートと同じ)。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Most of tabs is per-model dialect: DASI 300/300S/450, Teletype 37, Terminet/TN300, HP 2645 each get a function knowing that model's escape sequences, delays, margins, and quirks (tty37 sends SI then 40 DELs; dasi450 does bs(16) + ESC margin; hp2645 uses ESC '3'). Auto-detection is grimy: read $TERM, else gtty(0) (the old sgtty interface) and guess the model from baud rate and flags -- CRMOD at 300 baud => Terminet, no CRMOD/LCASE at 150 baud => Model 37. This hardcoded model table is the rough ancestor of termcap/terminfo, taming the exploding zoo of incompatible terminals one model at a time.
ai_analysis.human_story: Every terminal spoke its own dialect, so tabs hardcodes a function per machine and, lacking a name, sniffs baud and flags to guess which beast it is talking to. It is the rough draft of termcap, taming the terminal zoo by hand.

appraiser_note: tabs の大半は機種ごとの方言: DASI 300/300S/450、Teletype 37、Terminet/TN300、HP 2645 にそれぞれ専用関数があり、その機種固有のエスケープ・遅延・マージン・癖を知る(tty37 は SI 後に40 DEL、dasi450 は bs(16)+ESCマージン、hp2645 は ESC '3')。自動判別は泥臭く、$TERM を見て無ければ gtty(0)(旧 sgtty)でボーレートとフラグから機種推測——CRMOD で300ボーなら Terminet、CRMOD/LCASE 無しで150ボーなら Model 37。このハードコード機種テーブルは termcap/terminfo の素朴な原型で、非互換端末の動物園を一機種ずつ飼い慣らす。

ai_analysis.technical_context: quiz is a Computer-Aided Instruction game; its heart is a flexible answer-pattern language so grading is not exact-match. Answers in the data files are patterns: | (alternatives, airplane|aeroplane), [...] (required group with alternations), {...} (optional, a{eroplane} accepts 'a' or 'aeroplane'), \ (literal). cmp/disj/string/eat are a mutually-recursive descent matcher; fold() folds case. Return convention 0=no match, 1=match, >=2=syntax error propagates through the recursion. A distant cousin of regex (grep #65, egrep #204), but aimed at tolerating the variation in a human's answer rather than searching.
ai_analysis.human_story: Grading a quiz by exact string match is useless: people spell aeroplane or airplane, say York or New York. So quiz answers are little patterns, and a recursive-descent matcher accepts any reasonable phrasing, case and all. Regex technique pointed at human forgiveness.

appraiser_note: quiz は CAI(教育)ゲームで、心臓は柔軟な解答パターン言語(完全一致でない採点)。データの答えはパターン: |(選択肢 airplane|aeroplane)、[...](選択を含む必須グループ)、{...}(省略可 a{eroplane} は 'a' でも 'aeroplane' でも通る)、\(リテラル)。cmp/disj/string/eat が相互再帰下降マッチャ、fold() で大小無視。返り値規約 0=不一致/1=一致/2以上=構文エラーを再帰で伝播。grep(#65)/egrep(#204) の正規表現の遠い親戚だが、目的は検索でなく『人間の答えの揺れを許す』こと。

ai_analysis.technical_context: The same pattern language serves three roles in quiz. (1) Answer matching via cmp (the recursive matcher). (2) Display via publish/pub1: print the CANONICAL form of a pattern -- take the first | alternative, include {...} contents -- to show the question and the correct answer. (3) Column-name matching via perm: 'quiz africa capitals' resolves 'capitals' against the data file's column definitions using cmp again, so column names can also be flexible (capital{s}). Each colon-separated fact column is written in this language; 'quiz X Y' shows column X and asks for column Y (reverse for the other direction). One small grammar drives input parsing, output generation, and argument resolution -- the Bell Labs habit of one representation, many uses.
ai_analysis.human_story: quiz writes one little grammar and uses it three ways: to grade answers, to print the right answer, and to match the column names you typed. The same pattern, read forward to match and forward again to display.

appraiser_note: 同じパターン言語が quiz の三役を担う。(1)解答照合=cmp(再帰マッチャ)。(2)表示=publish/pub1: パターンの正準形を印字(| の最初の選択肢を採り {...} の中身も含める)し出題と正答を見せる。(3)列名照合=perm: 'quiz africa capitals' の 'capitals' をデータの列定義に cmp で対応づけ、列名も柔軟に書ける(capital{s})。コロン区切りの各事実列がこの言語で書かれ、'quiz X Y' は X列を見せ Y列を答えさせる(逆順で双方向)。一つの小さな文法が入力解釈・出力生成・引数解決を担う——Bell Labs の『一つの表現を多目的に』。

ai_analysis.technical_context: quiz adapts to the learner. na (number active) starts below the total; next() picks randomly within na via a linear congruential generator (inc*3125 & 077777, time-seeded) but SKIPS items with score>0 (already answered correctly). Each correct answer increments score[i] and does na++ to widen the active range. So mastered items retire and new ones enter; missed items stay and recur. Correct -> retire, wrong -> re-ask, mastery -> grow the pool: this is exactly modern spaced repetition / adaptive learning (Anki, Duolingo, Khan Academy), implemented in 1979 with just a score[] array and na++. -t (test) flips scoring; SIGINT shows 'Rights X, wrongs Y, score Z%'.
ai_analysis.human_story: A 472-line game already knew how people learn: show a few items, retire the ones you get right, keep the ones you miss, and add more as you master them. The core of every spaced-repetition app today, written with one array and one increment in 1979.

appraiser_note: quiz は学習者に適応する。na(出題対象数)は全項目より小さく始まり、next() が線形合同法(inc*3125&077777, 時刻シード)で na 内をランダム選択するが score>0(正解済み)はスキップ。正解ごとに score[i] を増やし na++ で範囲を広げる。習得済みは退役し新項目が加わり、間違えた項目は残り再出題。正解→退役/不正解→再出題/習熟→範囲拡大、これは現代の間隔反復学習/適応学習(Anki/Duolingo/Khan)そのもので、1979年に score[] 配列と na++ だけで実装。-t(test)で採点モード、SIGINT で『Rights X, wrongs Y, score Z%』。

text:
for(i=0;i<range;i++) left[i] = right[i] = i;	/* each number once */
...
i = skrand(range); j = skrand(range);	/* pick from the arrays */
...
/* on wrong answer: */
left[range] = left[i];
right[range++] = right[j];	/* add the missed numbers, raising their odds */

ai_analysis.technical_context: arithmetic is a four-function drill game. Its adaptation is wonderfully simple: the operand arrays left[]/right[] double as a weighted lottery box. Initially each number 0..range appears once (equal odds). On a WRONG answer it appends the missed operands (left[range]=left[i]; right[range++]=right[j]), increasing that number's multiplicity, so it is selected more often. The numbers you miss get drilled more. Same adaptive-learning idea as quiz (#217) from the other side: quiz retires mastered items, arithmetic multiplies the numbers you struggle with. No special data structure -- just multiplicity in the existing array (capped at MAX=100).
ai_analysis.human_story: Miss 7x8 and soon 7s and 8s keep coming back. arithmetic does this by dropping an extra copy of the numbers you missed into the same box it draws problems from -- weighting the lottery by hand, one array slot at a time.

appraiser_note: arithmetic は四則のドリルゲーム。適応が見事に素朴: 出題配列 left[]/right[] が重み付き抽選箱を兼ねる。初期は各数字 0..range が1回ずつ(等確率)。不正解で間違えた数字を末尾に追加(left[range]=left[i]; right[range++]=right[j])し多重度を上げ、選ばれやすくする。間違えた数字ほどドリルされる。quiz(#217)の適応学習を別角度から: quiz は習得済みを退役、arithmetic は苦手な数字を増やす。専用データ構造なし——既存配列の多重度だけ(MAX=100で打止)。

text:
case '-':
	t = left[i] + right[j]; ans = left[i];
	printf("%d - %d =   ", t, right[j]);	/* t - right[j], answer = left[i] >= 0 */
case '/':
	while(right[j] == 0) j = random(range);	/* avoid divide-by-zero */
	t = left[i] * right[j] + random(right[j]); ans = left[i];
	printf("%d / %d =   ", t, right[j]);	/* t / right[j] = left[i] exactly */

ai_analysis.technical_context: To avoid confusing beginners with negatives or fractions, arithmetic constructs problems backwards from a chosen answer. Subtraction: pick answer left[i], form t = left[i]+right[j], ask t - right[j] (answer is non-negative by construction). Division: form t = left[i]*right[j] + random(right[j]) (a remainder below the divisor), ask t / right[j], whose integer quotient is exactly left[i] -- always evenly divisible. A while-loop reselects to avoid divide-by-zero. Building only well-formed problems from the answer, rather than retrying random ones, is the same 'assemble correct output up front' care as number (#211).
ai_analysis.human_story: A kid's drill must never show 3 - 8 = -5 or 7 / 3. So arithmetic picks the answer first and builds the question around it, guaranteeing non-negative subtractions and clean divisions by construction.

appraiser_note: 初学者を負数や分数で混乱させないため、arithmetic は答えから逆算して問題を作る。引き算: 答え left[i] を決め t=left[i]+right[j] を作り t - right[j] を問う(構成上、答えは非負)。割り算: t=left[i]*right[j]+random(right[j])(除数未満の余り)を作り t/right[j] を問えば整数商はちょうど left[i]=割り切れる。while ループで0除算回避。ランダムな問題を引き直すのでなく最初から整った問題だけ構成する——number(#211)と同じ『正しい出力を最初から組み立てる』几帳面さ。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Two RNG strategies. Operation choice (+ - x /) uses random() = uniform. But operand choice uses skrand() = a TRIANGULAR distribution: sum two uniforms and fold at the midpoint (017777), so mid-sized numbers are more likely and the dull extremes (0+0, 10+10) are rare -- tuning difficulty via distribution. And hmul reduces a random to [0,range) via (rand * 8*range) >> 16 -- multiply then take the high bits -- which avoids the modulo bias of rand()%range. This is the multiply-shift / Lemire range-reduction technique, used here in 1979. The comment is signed: 'replaces an old PDP-11 assembler language subroutine. -- dks.'
ai_analysis.human_story: arithmetic even shapes its randomness: operations uniform, but operands drawn from a triangle so the questions sit at a comfortable middle. And it reduces ranges by multiply-and-shift, dodging modulo bias decades before it had a name -- with a programmer's initials left in the comment.

appraiser_note: 乱数2戦略。演算選択(+ - x /)は random()=一様。だが数字選択は skrand()=三角分布: 2つの一様乱数を足し中央値(017777)で折り返す、中くらいの数字が出やすく退屈な端(0+0,10+10)は稀——分布で難易度調整。hmul は乱数を [0,range) に (rand*8*range)>>16=乗算して上位ビットで縮小し、rand()%range の剰余バイアスを回避。今日 multiply-shift / Lemire の手法として知られる範囲縮小を1979年に使用。コメントに署名: 『古いPDP-11アセンブリのサブルーチンを置換 -- dks.』

text:
/* Spline fit technique ... (R W Hamming, Numerical Methods for Engineers and Scientists, 2nd Ed, p349ff)
   h[i]y"[i-1] + 2(h[i]+h[i+1])y"[i] + h[i+1]y"[i+1] = 6[...]   symmetric tridiagonal system */
...
d = a - hi*hi/d;		/* d[i] = a[i] - h[i]^2/d[i-1] */
r[i] = rhs(i) - hi*r[i-1]/d;	/* r[i] = b[i] - h[i]r[i-1]/d[i-1] */

ai_analysis.technical_context: spline is a cubic-spline interpolation tool (part of the graph/plot family: spline data | graph | plot). Of its 333 lines, ~140 are a comment containing the FULL mathematical derivation -- setting up the symmetric tridiagonal system for the second derivatives, the triangularization recurrence, back substitution, boundary conditions, the periodic case -- citing R. W. Hamming's textbook (the Bell Labs giant of Hamming codes/distance/window). The code is a one-to-one transcription of that math: comment 'd[i]=a[i]-h[i]^2/d[i-1]' becomes d=a-hi*hi/d. The programmer's work is not inventing the algorithm but faithfully translating established mathematics into floating-point recurrences.
ai_analysis.human_story: Before you read the code you must read the mathematics: 140 lines deriving the cubic spline from Hamming's textbook, then a few dozen lines transcribing it. The code is the shadow of the math, as close as software and mathematics ever get.

appraiser_note: spline は三次スプライン補間ツール(graph/plot 一族: spline data | graph | plot)。333行中 ~140行が、完全な数学的導出のコメント——二階微分の対称三重対角系の立て方、三角化の漸化式、後退代入、境界条件、周期の場合——を R.W.Hamming の教科書(ハミング符号/距離/窓の Bell Labs の巨人)を引用して記す。コードはその数式の一対一の転写: コメント 'd[i]=a[i]-h[i]^2/d[i-1]' が d=a-hi*hi/d に。プログラマの仕事はアルゴリズムの発明でなく、確立された数学を浮動小数点の漸化式に忠実に翻訳すること。

text:
/* superficially, d[i] and r[i] don't have to be stored for they can be
   recalculated backward ...
   unhappily it turns out that the recursion forward for d
   is quite strongly geometrically convergent--and is wildly
   unstable going backward.
   ... so the intermediate results must be kept. */
diag = (float *)malloc(...); r = (float *)malloc(...);

ai_analysis.technical_context: spline stores the triangularization intermediates d[i] and r[i] in malloc'd arrays. A comment explains a rejected optimization: you COULD avoid storing them by recomputing d backward via d[i-1]=h[i]^2/(a[i]-d[i]), saving memory. But 'unhappily it turns out' the forward recursion for d is strongly geometrically convergent, so running it backward is 'wildly unstable' -- floating-point rounding error explodes. So the intermediates must be kept. This is a comment documenting WHY a memory optimization was NOT taken, recording the forward-stable/backward-unstable asymmetry as a warning to future readers.
ai_analysis.human_story: There is an obvious way to save memory here -- recompute the values backward instead of storing them. The comment says they tried, and it was wildly unstable. So they kept the arrays, and wrote down why, so no one would 'optimize' it back into breakage.

appraiser_note: spline は三角化の中間結果 d[i]/r[i] を malloc 配列に保存する。コメントが却下した最適化を説明: d を後ろ向きに d[i-1]=h[i]^2/(a[i]-d[i]) で再計算すれば保存せずメモリ節約できる(superficially)。だが『unhappily it turns out』前進の d の漸化式は強く幾何級数的に収束するため逆向きは『wildly unstable(野放図に不安定)』——浮動小数点の丸め誤差が爆発。だから中間結果を保存せねばならない。メモリ最適化を『なぜ採らなかったか』を記録し、前進安定/後退不安定の非対称性を未来の読者への警告として刻むコメント。

ai_analysis.technical_context: spline's end behaviour is set by konst (the math k) via y"[0]=k*y"[1]: k=0 (natural, straight lines at ends), k=1 (parabolas), and k=1/2 'has been recommended as somehow pleasant' -- a strikingly subjective note after 140 lines of rigorous derivation. Curve beauty is not settled by a theorem; where theory ends, the human eye decides. -p enables periodic (closed-curve) splines, adding corner elements to the tridiagonal matrix with s/t/u/v correction terms, also fully derived in the comment. These are part of the Bell Labs graphics pipeline graph/plot/tplot, composed like the troff family.
ai_analysis.human_story: After 140 lines of exact mathematics, the choice of boundary condition comes down to: k=1/2 is 'somehow pleasant'. The rigor of the theorem and the vagueness of an aesthetic judgment, side by side in one comment.

appraiser_note: spline の端の振る舞いは konst(数学の k)で y"[0]=k*y"[1]: k=0(自然、端で直線)、k=1(放物線)、k=1/2 は『somehow pleasant(なんとなく感じが良い)として推奨』——140行の厳密な導出の後の、極めて主観的な一言。曲線の美しさは定理で決まらず、理論が尽きたところで人間の目が決める。-p は周期(閉曲線)スプラインで、三重対角行列の隅に s/t/u/v 補正項を加える(これもコメントで完全導出)。graph/plot/tplot の Bell Labs グラフィックパイプラインの一部で、troff 一族と同じく合成される。

ai_analysis.technical_context: cu (call Unix) is a modem dialer / remote-terminal program, the ancestor of telnet/ssh. While connected, everything you type flows to the line, so to give a LOCAL command cu intercepts any line that begins with '~' (tilde): ~. disconnect, ~<file send a file, ~! local shell, ~$ run local cmd piping output to the line, ~%put/~%take file transfer, ~~ a literal ~. wr() tests p==b && c=='~' to detect a local line. This beginning-of-line tilde-escape convention passed from cu to tip to OpenSSH: '~.' still force-disconnects an ssh session today. The command dies; the interface convention it invented lives 45+ years.
ai_analysis.human_story: Typing into a remote session, everything goes down the line -- so how do you tell cu 'this is for you'? Put a ~ at the start of the line. That tiny convention, ~. to hang up, is still how you kill a stuck ssh session in 2024.

appraiser_note: cu(call Unix)はモデムダイヤラ/リモート端末プログラムで telnet/ssh の祖。接続中は打った文字が全部回線へ流れるので、ローカルへの命令には行頭 '~'(チルダ)を横取り: ~.切断、~<file送信、~!ローカルシェル、~$ローカル実行→回線、~%put/take転送、~~でリテラル~。wr() が p==b && c=='~' でローカル行を判定。この行頭チルダエスケープは cu→tip→OpenSSH へ継承され、'~.' は今も ssh セッションを強制切断する。コマンドは消えても発明したインターフェース慣習は45年以上生きる。

ai_analysis.technical_context: cu has no file-transfer protocol -- only the terminal character stream. put/take build transfer on top of it via composition. ~%take sends shell commands INTO the remote ('echo ~>:; tee /dev/null < file; echo ~>') so the remote cats the file wrapped in ~> markers; cu's own rd() side recognizes ~> as an output-diversion start/end and writes the body to a local file. ~%put sends 'stty -echo; cat > file; stty echo' then streams the local file byte by byte, escaping kill/erase chars and retrying on write failure. No new protocol: just the shell, cat, tee, the terminal stream, and cu's own ~> escape -- the Unix compose-don't-invent ethos, like ptx delegating to sort.
ai_analysis.human_story: cu invents no transfer protocol. To pull a file it literally types shell commands to the remote that cat the file wrapped in ~> markers, then catches those markers on its own receive side. File transfer as a terminal magic trick, built from cat, tee, and an escape it already had.

appraiser_note: cu に専用のファイル転送プロトコルは無く、あるのは端末文字ストリームだけ。put/take はその上に合成で転送を載せる。~%take は相手にシェルコマンドを送り込み('echo ~>:; tee /dev/null < file; echo ~>')、相手が ~> で包んでファイルを吐き、cu 自身の rd() 側が ~> を出力横取りの開始/終了と認識しローカルファイルに書く。~%put は 'stty -echo; cat > file; stty echo' を送りローカルファイルを1バイトずつ流す(kill/erase 文字をエスケープ、書込失敗で再試行)。新プロトコル無し: シェル/cat/tee/端末ストリーム/自前の ~> エスケープの組合せ——ptx が sort に委譲したのと同じ Unix の合成哲学。

text:
if (*(p-1)!='<') { if (*(p-1)!='-') *p++='-'; *p++='<'; }	/* phone#: - pause, < End-Of-Number */
xalarm(5*t); t=write(dn,b,t);	/* write number to the ACU (dialer) */
...
xalarm(40);	/* was 5; sometimes missed carrier */
dh = open(dev,2);	/* wait for carrier, open the line */

ai_analysis.technical_context: conn() actually dials the phone. It opens the ACU (Automatic Calling Unit / modem dialer, /dev/cua0), writes the number string terminated by '<' (End-Of-Number) with '-' for dial pauses, then waits for carrier. Everything is wrapped in SIGALRM timeouts (xalarm/xsleep/sig14) because a modem can block forever on busy/no-carrier/dead lines; connmsg[] lists 'line busy','no carrier','acu access'. The most human comment in the program: 'xalarm(40); /* was 5; sometimes missed carrier */' -- the carrier-wait timeout was 5 seconds, but the remote modem sometimes answered later, so it was bumped to 40. A trace of fighting the flaky physical handshake, recorded in one line.
ai_analysis.human_story: Dialing a modem in 1979 was a physical gamble. The carrier-wait was 5 seconds until it 'sometimes missed carrier', so someone bumped it to 40 and wrote down why. The whole act wrapped in alarms because a phone line will block you forever.

appraiser_note: conn() が実際に電話をかける。ACU(自動発信装置/モデムのダイヤル部 /dev/cua0)を開き、'<'(番号終端)で終わる番号文字列を書く('-' はダイヤル一時停止)、そしてキャリアを待つ。全体が SIGALRM タイムアウト(xalarm/xsleep/sig14)で包まれる——モデムは話中/キャリアなし/回線切れで永遠にブロックしうるため(connmsg[] に 'line busy'/'no carrier'/'acu access')。最も人間味あるコメント: 'xalarm(40); /* was 5; sometimes missed carrier */'——キャリア待ちが5秒だったが相手モデムが遅れて応答することがあり40秒に延ばした。気まぐれな物理ハンドシェイクとの格闘の跡が1行に。

ai_analysis.technical_context: cu achieves full-duplex with two processes: main forks; the child runs rd() (read line, write your screen fd 1), the parent runs wr() (read keyboard fd 0, write line). One process per direction lets you type while the remote is sending -- the pattern every later interactive terminal (telnet, screen) inherits. When wr() ends (you typed ~.), the parent kills the child. mode() switches your terminal: mode(1)=RAW (chars pass one at a time, no local echo -- the remote echoes, hence the lag of slow links), mode(2)=no-echo (file send), mode(0)=cooked. It captures sg_kill/sg_erase first so the ~ escape line keeps local line editing. fork + ioctl weave the boundary between local and remote.
ai_analysis.human_story: Two processes, one each way: a child shows you what arrives, a parent sends what you type. That is why you can type over a remote that is still talking. The lag of remote echo, the RAW mode, all of it is here -- the shape every terminal program took after.

appraiser_note: cu は2プロセスで全二重を実現: main が fork し、子が rd()(回線を読み画面 fd 1 に出す)、親が wr()(キーボード fd 0 を読み回線に書く)。方向ごとに1プロセスで、相手が送信中でも打てる——後の対話端末(telnet/screen)がみな継ぐパターン。wr() 終了(~. を打った)で親が子を kill。mode() が端末を切替: mode(1)=RAW(1文字ずつ素通し・ローカルエコー無し=相手がエコー、遅い回線の遅延の正体)、mode(2)=no-echo(ファイル送信)、mode(0)=cooked。sg_kill/sg_erase を先に取得し ~ エスケープ行ではローカル行編集を効かせる。fork + ioctl がローカルとリモートの境界を織る。

ai_analysis.technical_context: icheck (inode check) is the direct ancestor of fsck. It walks every inode, follows each file's data blocks, and detects two corruptions: dup (a block claimed by two files = cross-link) and missing (a block in no file and not on the free list = lost). The core data structure is one bit per data block: bmap is a bit array, block d sets bit d%8 of byte d/8. Tens of thousands of blocks cost only 1/8 byte each. duped() tests-and-sets the bit. The most telling line is the malloc-failure path: 'Not enough core; duplicates unchecked' -- it gives up dup detection (dflg++) and salvage (sflg=0) but keeps running the checks that need no bitmap. A filesystem checker that cannot fully trust even its own runtime environment, degrading gracefully instead of dying.
ai_analysis.human_story: How do you remember whether you have seen each of tens of thousands of disk blocks? One bit apiece. And if there is not even enough memory for that, icheck says so and checks what it still can, rather than giving up.

appraiser_note: icheck(inode check)は fsck の直接の祖。全 inode を舐め各ファイルのデータブロックを辿り、dup(2つのファイルが同一ブロックを共有=クロスリンク)と missing(どのファイルにも自由リストにも属さない迷子)を検出する。心臓部は『1ブロック=1ビット』: bmap はビット配列で、ブロック d は d/8 バイト目の d%8 ビット目。何万ブロックでも1個あたり1/8バイト。duped() がテスト&セット。最も雄弁なのは malloc 失敗時の『Not enough core; duplicates unchecked』——dup 検出(dflg++)と救済(sflg=0)は諦めるが、ビットマップ不要の検査は続行する。検査ツールが自分の動く環境すら信用しきれず、落ちずに優雅に劣化する。

text:
if(i < NADDR-3) { ndirect++; chk(iaddr[i], "data (small)"); continue; }   /* direct */
...
  if(i == NADDR-3) { chk(ind1[j], "data (large)"); continue; }   /* single indirect */
  ...
    if(i == NADDR-2) { chk(ind2[k], "data (huge)"); continue; }  /* double indirect */
    ...
      if(ind3[l]) chk(ind3[l], "data (garg)");                   /* triple indirect */

ai_analysis.technical_context: A Unix inode holds NADDR=13 block addresses: the first 10 are direct, then one single, one double, and one triple indirect block (each indirect block holds NINDIR=128 addresses). pass1 descends this whole staircase with a 4-deep nested loop, calling chk() on every block. The author named the data classes at each depth: 'small' (direct), 'large' (single indirect), 'huge' (double), and -- at the bottom of triple indirection -- 'garg' (gargantuan). A corruption report says 'inode=42, class=data (garg)', telling you how deep in the file's addressing the bad block lived. l3tol() first expands the 3-byte on-disk addresses to long, a reminder of how tightly the on-disk inode packed its bits.
ai_analysis.human_story: The addressing staircase of a Unix file -- direct, single, double, triple indirect -- got named small, large, huge, and garg (gargantuan). A diagnostic tool that still found room for a joke about how enormous a file could get.

appraiser_note: Unix の inode は NADDR=13 個のブロックアドレスを持つ: 最初の10個が直接、続いて単/二重/三重間接が各1個(間接ブロックは NINDIR=128 アドレス)。pass1 はこの階段を4重ネストループで降り、各ブロックを chk() に渡す。作者は深さごとのデータ class を命名: 'small'(直接)、'large'(単間接)、'huge'(二重)、そして三重間接の果てが 'garg'(gargantuan=とてつもなく巨大)。破損報告は 'inode=42, class=data (garg)' のように、悪いブロックがファイルのどの深さに居たかを告げる。l3tol() が3バイトのディスク表現を long に展開してから辿るのも、ディスク上 inode のビット節約の証拠。乾いた診断ツールに『どこまでファイルが巨大になりうるか』のユーモアが残る。

ai_analysis.technical_context: icheck -s does not just check -- it rebuilds the free list (salvage). After pass1 has marked every block used by a file, any block whose bitmap bit is clear is free, and gets relinked via bfree(). But icheck does not chain them in numeric order. It first builds a permutation adr[] by stepping m (default 3) slots at a time -- i=(i+m)%n -- so consecutive free-list blocks are spaced apart on the platter. This is interleaving for rotational latency: while the program processes one block the disk keeps spinning, so the next wanted block must sit a little ahead (m sectors of rotation) to be under the head exactly when needed. s_n is the blocks per group, s_m the skip factor. The free-list order itself is tuned to the disk's RPM -- software meeting the physics of a spinning iron disk.
ai_analysis.human_story: Rebuilding the free list, icheck does not lay blocks down in order. It spaces them out by an interleave factor, because while you finish one block the disk has already spun past its neighbour. The order of free blocks tuned to the speed the platter turns.

appraiser_note: icheck -s は検査だけでなく自由リストを丸ごと作り直す(salvage)。pass1 が全ファイルの使用ブロックに印を付けた後、ビットの立っていないブロック=未使用を bfree() で繋ぎ直す。だが番号順には繋がない。まず m(既定3)ずつインデックスを進めて未使用スロットを拾う順列 adr[] を作る——i=(i+m)%n——ので、自由リストに並ぶブロックは円盤上で飛び飛びになる。これは回転遅延(rotational latency)対策のインターリーブ: 1ブロックを処理する間もディスクは回り続けるので、次に欲しいブロックは少し先(m セクタ回った位置)に置いておけば、丁度欲しい時にヘッドの下に来る。s_n=1グループのブロック数、s_m=飛ばし幅。自由リストの並び順そのものをディスクの回転速度に合わせてチューニングしている——ソフトウェアが回る鉄の円盤の物理と地続きだった時代の痕跡。

text:
#ifndef STANDALONE
	while (--argc) { ... check(*argv); }     /* normal: run as a command */
#else
	{ static char fname[0];
	  printf("File: "); gets(fname);         /* standalone: no OS, ask for target */
	  check(fname); }
#endif

ai_analysis.technical_context: icheck.c is laced with #ifdef STANDALONE. In standalone mode it skips malloc (bmap=NULL, no dup check), skips sync(), changes output formats, and main() does not parse argv -- it prints 'File: ' and gets() the target. Why would a filesystem checker need to run without an OS? Chicken and egg: if the root filesystem is corrupt, Unix will not boot, and if it will not boot you cannot run icheck -- the very tool meant to repair the disk cannot run because of the disk it would repair. So icheck had a build that runs on bare hardware from boot media, reading the disk as raw bytes via lseek/read/write. This is the origin of the fsck philosophy: verify (and if needed repair) the filesystem before it is mounted, before the system comes up. A program that checks the filesystem must not itself depend on the filesystem.
ai_analysis.human_story: The tool that repairs a broken disk lives on that disk -- so when the disk breaks badly enough, it cannot run. icheck's answer was a version that boots on bare metal and asks 'File:' with no operating system underneath it at all.

appraiser_note: icheck.c は全編 #ifdef STANDALONE で編まれている。スタンドアロン時は malloc を諦め(bmap=NULL、dup チェック無し)、sync() を呼ばず、出力形式を変え、main() は argv を解析せず『File: 』とプロンプトを出して gets() で対象を尋ねる。なぜファイルシステム検査ツールに OS なしモードが要るのか。鶏と卵だ: ルートファイルシステムが壊れると Unix は起動できず、起動できなければ icheck も実行できない——ディスクを直すはずのツールが、そのディスクのせいで動かせない。だから icheck はカーネルも FS も介さずブートメディアから素のハードウェア上で動く版を持ち、lseek/read/write でディスクを生のバイト列として舐めた。これは fsck の思想の原点: マウントされる前・システムが立ち上がる前に FS の健全性を確かめ必要なら直す。ファイルシステムを検査するプログラムは、ファイルシステムに依存してはならない。

text:
pname(i, lev) ino_t i; {
	if (i==ROOTINO) return;		/* hit the root: base case */
	if ((hp = lookup(i, 0)) == 0) { printf("???"); return; }	/* parent gone = broken */
	if (lev > 10) { printf("..."); return; }	/* too deep / cycle guard */
	pname(hp->h_pino, ++lev);	/* print parent first, then */
	printf("/%.14s", hp->h_name);	/* append my own name */
}

ai_analysis.technical_context: ncheck (name check) reconstructs a full pathname from an inode number. A Unix inode does not store its own name or parent -- names live only inside directories as name->inode entries, and the reverse lookup is indexed nowhere. pname() rebuilds a path bottom-up: to print inode i it first recurses to i's parent (h_pino) and only then appends i's own name, so the stack unwinds as / -> /usr -> /usr/src -> /usr/src/cmd, in correct order. The base case is ROOTINO. Two guards make it survive a damaged disk: lookup()==0 prints '???' for an orphan whose parent is missing, and lev>10 prints '...' to stop infinite recursion if the directory graph has a cycle (A's parent is B, B's parent is A). ncheck does not assume the tree it reads is actually a tree.
ai_analysis.human_story: A file does not know its own name. To print a path, ncheck climbs from a directory up to its parent, to its parent's parent, all the way to the root, then writes the names back down on the way out. Recursion turns 'walk up to the root' into 'print the path top to bottom'.

appraiser_note: ncheck(name check)は inode 番号からフルパスを逆構築する。Unix の inode は自分の名前も親も持たない——名前はディレクトリの中の『名前→inode』エントリにしか無く、その逆引きはどこにも索引化されていない。pname() はパスを下から組み上げる: inode i を印字するにはまず i の親(h_pino)へ再帰し、それから i 自身の名前を継ぐので、スタックが巻き戻るにつれ / → /usr → /usr/src → /usr/src/cmd と正しい順に出る。底は ROOTINO。2つの防壁で壊れたディスクに耐える: lookup()==0 は親を失った孤児に '???'、lev>10 はディレクトリ構造が循環(Aの親がB、Bの親がA)していたときの無限再帰を '...' で止める。ncheck は読んでいる木が本当に木である(循環しない)とは仮定しない。
