ai_analysis.technical_context: To rebuild paths, ncheck keeps only DIRECTORY inodes -- a directory appears in the middle of many files' paths, while a plain file is a leaf printed once. htab stores each directory's inode, its parent inode (h_pino), and its name. lookup() is open addressing with linear probing from i%HSIZE, wrapping at the end of the array -- no chaining, no malloc, all inside one fixed array. HSIZE is 2503, a prime, so i%HSIZE spreads inode numbers evenly and avoids clustering -- the same 'hash with primes' habit as spell's bloom filter. When the table fills it prints 'increase HSIZE', asking you to recompile: filesystem scale pinned by one constant.
ai_analysis.human_story: Only directories get remembered, because only directories sit in the middle of other files' paths. The table is a prime-sized array probed linearly, and if it overflows the program tells you to recompile with a bigger number.

appraiser_note: パスを組み直すため ncheck が覚えるのはディレクトリ inode だけ——ディレクトリは多くのファイルのパスの途中に現れるが、普通のファイルは1度印字すれば済む葉。htab は各ディレクトリの inode・親 inode(h_pino)・名前を持つ。lookup() は i%HSIZE 起点の線形探索によるオープンアドレス法で、配列末尾で先頭へ回り込む——チェイン無し・malloc 無し、固定長配列の中だけ。HSIZE は素数 2503 で、i%HSIZE の分布を均し偏りを避ける(spell のブルームフィルタと同じ『ハッシュには素数』の手癖)。表が溢れると 'increase HSIZE' と再コンパイルを促す: ファイルシステムの規模が定数ひとつで決め打ちだった時代。

ai_analysis.technical_context: ncheck scans the inode table three times. A directory's name lives in its PARENT's entry, but in inode-number order the parent and child may appear in either order, so you cannot write names until all directories are registered. pass1 enters every directory inode (name and parent still blank). pass2 reads each directory's entries; when an entry points at a known directory it sets that child's parent (h_pino = current dir) and copies its name -- building the directory tree backwards as child->parent pointers. pass3 prints every entry as inode-number + full path. Only directories go in the tree because plain files are leaves; pass3 hangs each leaf under its parent and prints. Separating the register phase from the write phase is the whole reason for multiple passes.
ai_analysis.human_story: You cannot name a directory until you have seen every directory, because its name is written in a parent you might not have reached yet. So ncheck registers them all first, then wires up parents and names, then prints. The name space rebuilt in three sweeps.

appraiser_note: ncheck は inode テーブルを3回舐める。ディレクトリの名前は親のエントリに書かれるが、inode 番号順では親と子のどちらが先に出るか決まっていないので、全ディレクトリを登録し終えるまで名前を書けない。pass1 は全ディレクトリ inode を登録(名前も親も空)。pass2 は各ディレクトリのエントリを読み、エントリが既知のディレクトリを指すならその子に親(h_pino=今いるディレクトリ)と名前を書き込む——子→親ポインタとして木を逆向きに組む。pass3 は全エントリを inode番号＋フルパスで印字。表に入れるのはディレクトリだけ(普通のファイルは葉)で、pass3 がその葉を親の木にぶら下げて印字する。登録フェーズと書込フェーズの分離が、複数パスの理由そのもの。

ai_analysis.technical_context: ncheck embodies the asymmetry of a Unix filesystem: directories map name->inode, but the reverse is indexed nowhere, so the only way to know a file's current path is to read every directory and match. ncheck does that reverse lookup on the raw disk, without mounting or the kernel. This has two descendants. find: walking the tree to print paths matching conditions is ncheck made interactive and conditional -- and ncheck -s, which lists setuid/setgid and device files, is the ancestor of 'find / -perm -4000', the classic security audit for privilege-escalation files. And fsck's lost+found: when fsck finds an inode that no directory references (it lost its name) but whose data is alive, it relinks it as lost+found/#<inode>, giving the nameless inode its own number as a name -- exactly the inode<->name domain ncheck opened up.
ai_analysis.human_story: A filesystem can map a name to a file but not a file back to its name -- that index simply does not exist. ncheck builds it by brute force, reading every directory. Out of that grew find, the setuid-file security audit, and fsck rescuing nameless inodes into lost+found.

appraiser_note: ncheck は Unix ファイルシステムの非対称性を体現する: ディレクトリは『名前→inode』を引けるが逆はどこにも索引化されておらず、ファイルの現在のパスを知る唯一の方法は全ディレクトリを読んで突き合わせること。ncheck はその逆引きをマウントもカーネルも介さず生ディスク上でやる。子孫は2つ。find: 木を歩いて条件に合うパスを出すのは ncheck を対話的・条件付きにしたもので、とりわけ ncheck -s が setuid/setgid とデバイスファイルを列挙する機能は『find / -perm -4000』=権限昇格に使える危険ファイルを洗い出すセキュリティ監査の祖先。そして fsck の lost+found: どのディレクトリからも参照されない(名前を失った)が中身は生きている inode を fsck は lost+found/#<inode番号> に繋ぎ直す——名前なき inode に番号そのものを仮の名前として与える、まさに ncheck が切り拓いた『inode と名前の対応』の領域。

ai_analysis.technical_context: c2 is the 'object code improver' -- the peephole optimizer invoked by cc -O. It reads the assembly the compiler emitted as text and rewrites it into faster/smaller assembly. The engine is a nested do-while: iterate() slides a small window (peephole) over the instruction stream applying local rewrites and sets nchange whenever it changes something; the loop repeats while anything changed. Why iterate? Because one optimization exposes another -- delete a redundant jump and its target label becomes unreferenced, so the label can go too, which makes the following code contiguous with the previous block, exposing new rewrites. c2 runs to a fixpoint: keep sweeping until nothing more changes. This is the canonical Unix peephole optimizer, descendant in spirit of GCC's and LLVM's peephole passes.
ai_analysis.human_story: The compiler emits dumb, literal code. c2 reads that code back and polishes it -- and because fixing one thing reveals the next thing to fix, it keeps sweeping over the instructions until a full pass changes nothing at all.

appraiser_note: c2 は『object code improver』——cc -O が呼び出すピープホール最適化器。コンパイラが吐いたアセンブリをテキストとして読み、より速く小さいアセンブリに書き直す。エンジンは入れ子の do-while: iterate() が命令列の上を小さな窓(peephole)で滑らせ局所変換を当て、何か変える度に nchange を立てる。変わる限りループは回る。なぜ反復するか——ひとつの最適化が次を生むから。冗長なジャンプを消すとその飛び先ラベルが無参照になりラベルも消せ、すると後続コードが前の基本ブロックと地続きになり新たな書き換えが見える。c2 は不動点(もう何も変わらない)まで掃き続ける。Unix におけるピープホール最適化の規範実装で、GCC/LLVM のpeephole パスの精神的祖先。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Before optimizing, c2 turns the text assembly into a doubly-linked list of struct node. input() reads each line, looks the mnemonic up in optab, and links nodes via forw/back. A branch's ref points directly at its target label node; refc counts how many places reference that label. This representation is what makes every optimization cheap: p->ref reaches the target in one hop, p->ref->refc tells you if a label is dead, and inserting/deleting an instruction is just pointer surgery. Turning assembly text into an operable graph is the move that makes the rest possible. Memory is grown by hand with sbrk(2000) and carved by alloc(), reset to firstr after each function -- arena allocation, no malloc.
ai_analysis.human_story: Assembly as flat text is hard to reason about. c2 first rebuilds it as a linked graph where a jump points straight at its destination and you can count who still uses a label. Only then do the optimizations become easy pointer moves.

appraiser_note: 最適化の前に c2 はテキストのアセンブリを struct node の双方向連結リストに組み直す。input() が1行ずつ読み、命令名を optab で引き、forw/back で繋ぐ。分岐の ref は飛び先ラベルノードを直接指し、refc はそのラベルが何箇所から参照されるかの数。この表現こそ全最適化を安くする: p->ref で飛び先に一足、p->ref->refc でラベルが死んでいるか即判定、命令の挿入削除はポインタの繋ぎ替えだけ。アセンブリを操作可能なグラフに変える——これが残りを可能にする一手。メモリは sbrk(2000) で自前に伸ばし alloc() で切り出し、関数ごとに firstr へ巻き戻す。malloc 不使用のアリーナ管理。

ai_analysis.technical_context: iterate() is a textbook of peephole transforms on jumps: jump-to-jump (nbrbr) retargets a jump whose destination is itself an unconditional jump straight to the final target; unreachable code right after an unconditional jump (iaftbr) is deleted; a jump to the very next label (njp1) is removed; and a conditional branch over an unconditional jump is collapsed by reversing the condition via revbr[] (nskip). The cleverest is xjump -- cross-jumping (tail merging): when two control paths converge, it walks both backwards instruction by instruction while equop() says they match, inserts a label at the divergence point, and replaces one copy with a JBR to it -- duplicated tails fused into one. A structural size optimization, done with nothing but matching instructions back to front. The same spirit drives rmove(), which tracks what is in each register and drops a MOV whose value is already there (redunm) and a TST whose condition codes are already set (nrtst).
ai_analysis.human_story: Jump to a jump? Go straight to the end. Code after an unconditional jump? Dead, delete it. Two paths ending in the same instructions? Fuse the shared tail and jump into it. c2 knew every one of these little patterns and applied them by the thousand.

appraiser_note: iterate() はジャンプに関するピープホール最適化の教科書: jump-to-jump(nbrbr)は飛び先がまた無条件ジャンプなら最終目的地へ直接繋ぎ替える。無条件ジャンプ直後の到達不能コード(iaftbr)は削除。すぐ次のラベルへ飛ぶだけのジャンプ(njp1)も削除。条件分岐が無条件ジャンプを飛び越す形は revbr[] で条件反転して畳む(nskip)。最も賢いのが xjump——クロスジャンプ(末尾併合): 2つの制御経路が合流するとき、両方を後ろ向きに1命令ずつ equop() が一致する限り辿り、分岐点にラベルを挿し片方をそこへの JBR に置き換える——重複した末尾が1つに融合される。命令を後ろから突き合わせるだけで実現する構造的なサイズ最適化。同じ精神がrmove() にも: 各レジスタの中身を追跡し、もう入っている値の MOV(redunm)や、もう立っている条件コードの TST(nrtst)を捨てる。

ai_analysis.technical_context: c2 -n prints a ledger of exactly what it did: jumps to jumps, instructions after jumps, jumps to .+1, redundant labels, cross-jumps, code motions, branches reversed, redundant moves, simplified addresses, loops inverted, redundant jumps, common seqs before jumps, skips over jumps, sob's added, redundant tst's, jump on bit, field operations -- exactly 17 kinds of optimization, each a counter. The catalog is the best definition of what peephole optimization is: not deep global analysis, just small mechanical equivalences applied to a fixpoint -- 3-address to 2-address (redun3), DEC+branch folded into the VAX sob instruction (addsob), a branch reversed to fit a short displacement (jumpsw). An optimizer that counts and reports its own work, the same 'count and tell' temperament as wc and icheck.
ai_analysis.human_story: Run it with -n and c2 hands you an honest invoice of its work: so many jumps to jumps, so many redundant moves, so many loops folded into one instruction. Even the optimizer files a statistical report on itself.

appraiser_note: c2 -n は自分が何をしたかの帳簿を吐く: jumps to jumps、inst. after jumps、jumps to .+1、redundant labels、cross-jumps、code motions、branches reversed、redundant moves、simplified addresses、loops inverted、redundant jumps、common seqs before jmp's、skips over jumps、sob's added、redundant tst's、jump on bit、field operations——ちょうど17種の最適化が各々カウンタとして並ぶ。このカタログはピープホール最適化の最良の定義になる: 難しい大域解析ではなく、小さな機械的等価変換を不動点まで当てるだけ——3アドレス→2アドレス(redun3)、DEC+分岐を VAX の sob 1命令に畳む(addsob)、短い変位に収める分岐反転(jumpsw)。自分の成果を数えて報告する最適化器、wc や icheck と同じ『数えて告げる』気質。

ai_analysis.technical_context: adb is the UNIX debugger, ancestor of sdb -> dbx -> gdb -> lldb. To control another program a debugger must reach inside an isolated process -- stop it, read its memory, change its registers. setup() shows how: adb forks; the child calls ptrace(SETTRC,0,0,0) -- 'set trace', i.e. it volunteers to be traced -- then doexec()s the target onto itself. Now the parent (adb) controls the child (target) through ptrace. The child asking to be traced is the strange-looking handshake that still underlies gdb's run today: fork + PTRACE_TRACEME + exec.
ai_analysis.human_story: How do you get inside another running program? adb forks a child, and the child says to the kernel 'please trace me', then becomes the program you want to debug. The same odd ritual runs every time gdb launches a program, 46 years later.

appraiser_note: adb は UNIX のデバッガで、sdb→dbx→gdb→lldb の祖。他プログラムを操るにはデバッガが隔離された別プロセスの内部に立ち入る——止め、メモリを読み、レジスタを書き換える——必要がある。setup() がその方法を示す: adb はfork し、子が ptrace(SETTRC,0,0,0)＝『set trace（私を追跡してください）』と自ら名乗り出てから、対象を自分にdoexec する。こうして親(adb)が子(対象)を ptrace で操る。子が自ら追跡を志願するこの一見奇妙な握手は、今も gdb の run の土台＝fork + PTRACE_TRACEME + exec として生きている。

ai_analysis.technical_context: How does a breakpoint work? No special hardware -- adb rewrites the target's own code. setbp() reads the instruction at the breakpoint address with ptrace(RIUSER), saves it, and overwrites it with BPT (03, the breakpoint-trap instruction) via ptrace(WIUSER). When the target reaches that spot it executes BPT, traps, and stops; the parent wakes from wait(). adb then restores the saved instruction with delbp(). Continuing is the hard part ('continuing from a breakpoint is hard'): restore the real instruction, single-step it once with ptrace(SINGLE) (execbkpt), then re-plant BPT. This exact trick -- overwrite, restore, single-step, re-plant -- is how every software breakpoint still works; gdb's break main rewrites the first byte to int3 (x86 breakpoint) the same way. adb breakpoints can even carry an adb command string and a count, the ancestor of conditional/command breakpoints.
ai_analysis.human_story: A breakpoint is a forgery: adb overwrites one instruction of your program with a trap, catches the trap, puts the real instruction back, steps once, and forges the trap again. gdb still does exactly this, swapping a byte for int3.

appraiser_note: ブレークポイントはどう動くか。専用ハードも使わず、adb は対象自身のコードを書き換える。setbp() はブレークポイント地点の命令を ptrace(RIUSER) で読んで保存し、BPT(03、ブレークポイント・トラップ命令)で ptrace(WIUSER) 上書きする。対象がそこに達すると BPT を実行してトラップし停止、親が wait から目覚める。adb は delbp() で保存命令を書き戻す。続行が難所('continuing from a breakpoint is hard'): 本物の命令に戻し、ptrace(SINGLE) で1命令だけ実行(execbkpt)、それから BPT を埋め直す。この『上書き→復元→1命令→再設定』は現代の全ソフトウェアブレークポイントの仕組みそのもので、gdb の break main も先頭1バイトを int3(x86のブレークポイント命令)に書き換える。adb のブレークポイントは adb コマンド文字列と回数も結べ、条件付き/コマンド付きブレークポイントの原型。

text:
switch(lastcom&STRIP) {
	case '/': itype=DSP; ptype=DSYM; goto trystar;  /* / examine data space */
	case '=': itype=NSP; ptype=0;    goto trypr;    /* = value of the expression */
	case '?': itype=ISP; ptype=ISYM; goto trystar;  /* ? examine instruction space */
	case '$': printtrace(nextchar()); break;        /* $c=stack  $r=registers */
	case ':': subpcs(nextchar());    break;         /* :r=run  :s=step  :c=cont */
	case '>': /* assign dot to a register/variable */
	case '!': shell(); break;                       /* shell escape */
}

ai_analysis.technical_context: adb's command language is maximally terse: addr,count command format. '0x1000,5?i' means 'from 0x1000, 5 items, show as instructions' -- adb disassembles five. At the center is '.' (dot), the current address held in command(), advanced after each command, so you just hit return to continue. Three sigils switch the target: '?' reads the a.out instruction space, '/' the data/live-process space, '=' the value of the address expression itself. '$' is diagnostics ($c stack backtrace via print.c, $r registers), ':' is process control (:r run, :s step, :c cont, via pcs.c subpcs). This addr,count?format with dot and the x/i format letters lives on almost unchanged in gdb's x/5i $pc. And it is all written in mac.h's Bourne-shell-style Algol68 macro dialect (IF/THEN/FI/REP/PER/WHILE/DO/OD) -- the same C-as-Algol68 idiom Steve Bourne used in sh.
ai_analysis.human_story: An address, a one-letter command, a one-letter format. adb says nothing it does not have to. That terseness, and the '.' you advance by hitting return, is still in your fingers when you type x/5i $pc into gdb.

appraiser_note: adb のコマンド言語は極限まで簡潔: addr,count コマンド 書式。'0x1000,5?i' は『0x1000 から5個、命令として表示』＝5命令を逆アセンブル。中心は '.'(ドット)＝現在アドレスで command() が保持しコマンドごとに進めるので、改行を叩くだけで続きが出る。3つの記号が覗く先を切替: '?' は a.out の命令空間、'/' はデータ/生プロセス空間、'=' はアドレス式の値。'$' は診断($c スタック・$r レジスタ、print.c)、':' はプロセス制御(:r 実行 :s ステップ :c 継続、pcs.c の subpcs)。この addr,count?format と dot、x/i の書式文字は gdb の x/5i $pc にほぼそのまま生きている。そして全編が mac.h の Bourne シェル流 Algol68 マクロ方言(IF/THEN/FI/REP/PER/WHILE/DO/OD)＝Steve Bourne が sh で使った『C を Algol68 に見せる』流儀で書かれている。

ai_analysis.technical_context: adb a.out core: arg1 symfil is the executable (with the symbol table), arg2 corfil is a core dump. adb's most beautiful design is that the SAME command language examines three targets -- the executable file, the live process, and the core dump -- transparently. The trick is the maps (txtmap/datmap): '?' reads instruction space and '/' data space, but whether the read lands in the a.out file, a ptrace'd live process, or a core file is absorbed by the map's file descriptor (set to fsym for '?', fcor for '/'). So adb is also a postmortem tool: after a crash adb a.out core lets you $c the stack, $r the registers, ? the faulting instruction -- the target is dead, examined with the same grammar it had alive. adb /vmunix /dev/mem even examines the live kernel (the EDDT standalone build is a built-in kernel debugger). sym.c maps main+0x10 names to addresses; expr.c evaluates address expressions -- binding number to name for program symbols as ncheck did for files.
ai_analysis.human_story: One grammar, four targets: the binary on disk, the process running now, the core of a process that crashed, and the live kernel itself. adb skewers them all with addr,count?format, so a dead program is read exactly as a living one was.

appraiser_note: adb a.out core: 第1引数 symfil は実行ファイル(シンボル表入り)、第2引数 corfil はコアダンプ。adb 最美の設計は、同じコマンド言語で3つの対象——実行ファイル・生プロセス・コアダンプ——を透過的に覗けること。仕掛けはマップ(txtmap/datmap): '?' は命令空間、'/' はデータ空間を読むが、読み先が a.out ファイルか ptrace 越しの生プロセスかコアファイルかはマップの fd(? なら fsym、/ なら fcor)が吸収する。だから adb は事後解析の道具でもある: クラッシュ後 adb a.out core で $c のスタック、$r のレジスタ、? の障害命令を、死んだ対象を生きていたときと同じ文法で調べられる。adb /vmunix /dev/mem なら動くカーネルもライブで覗ける(EDDT スタンドアロン版はカーネル組込デバッガ)。sym.c が main+0x10 のような名前とアドレスを相互変換し expr.c が式を評価——番号と名前を結ぶ仕事を、ncheck がファイルでやったように adb はプログラムのシンボルでやる。

text:
%token IBYTE IWORD IINT ILONG IDATA IGLOBAL ISET ITEXT ICOMM IALIGN
%left PLUS MINUS
%left IOR XOR AND
%left MUL DIV LSH RSH TILDE
%%
file: /* empty */
	| file linstruction NL = { lineno++; goto reset; }
	;
labels: /* empty */
	| labels NAME COLON = { ... $2->value = dotp->xvalue; ... }  /* define a label */

ai_analysis.technical_context: Most assemblers are ad-hoc line parsers. Bell-32V's as is a yacc grammar (as0.y). It declares assembly language itself in BNF: a file is a sequence of lines, a line is labels plus an instruction, an operand is an expression with operators given precedence by %left. The pseudo-ops (.byte/.word/.text/.data/.globl/.align/.set) are grammar tokens (IBYTE/IWORD/...). A beautiful recursion: yacc (Stephen Johnson's compiler-compiler) generates a parser; here it is used to generate the parser for assembly language. %left PLUS MINUS / %left MUL DIV gives operands a full expression evaluator from the grammar alone. The Bell Labs habit of expressing the problem as a grammar -- as in bc, eqn, pic -- reaches all the way down to the assembler.
ai_analysis.human_story: An assembler written as a grammar. yacc, the tool that turns a grammar into a parser, is used to give assembly language itself a grammar -- operator precedence and all -- so even the lowest-level tool in the toolchain is a little language.

appraiser_note: 多くのアセンブラは行を手で切るアドホックなパーサだが、Bell-32V の as は yacc 文法(as0.y)。アセンブリ言語そのものを BNF で宣言する: ファイルは行の並び、行はラベル群＋命令、オペランドは %left で優先順位を与えた式。擬似命令(.byte/.word/.text/.data/.globl/.align/.set)はすべて文法トークン(IBYTE/IWORD/…)。美しい再帰だ: yacc(Stephen Johnson のコンパイラ生成ツール)が構文解析器を生成する——それを使って今度はアセンブリ言語の構文解析器を生成する。%left PLUS MINUS / %left MUL DIV でオペランドに電卓並みの式評価器が文法ひとつで備わる。問題を文法として表現する Bell Labs の流儀(bc/eqn/pic と同じ)が、最下層のアセンブラにまで貫かれている。

ai_analysis.technical_context: The jbr/jeql/jneq/jgtr in as3.c are not real VAX instructions -- they are synthetic branches (jxxx) as provides. A real VAX conditional branch reaches its target with only a one-byte displacement, so it cannot jump far. as lets you write 'jeql target' and decides: if near, a short branch (beql); if far, reverse the condition and branch over a real jmp (bneq .+N; jmp target). This is the classic span-dependent instruction problem -- an instruction's length depends on the distance to its target, but the distance depends on the lengths of intervening instructions, which depend on their distances. jxxxfix solves it optimistically: assume all short, grow (jxxxbump) the ones that overflow a byte, set nchange, sweep again, until nothing more grows -- a fixpoint the comment calls a 'lazy topological sort'. The same fixpoint shape as c2's optimizer; the source even cheers 'win!' and laments 'immediate lose'.
ai_analysis.human_story: Write jeql and let the assembler worry about distance. If the target is near it stays a short branch; if far it grows into a reversed branch over a jump. But growing one branch shifts everything after it, so as keeps re-deciding until nothing more changes -- printing 'win!' each time a branch fits.

appraiser_note: as3.c の jbr/jeql/jneq/jgtr は VAX の実在命令ではなく、as が提供する合成分岐(jxxx)。VAX の本物の条件分岐は飛び先を1バイト変位でしか表せず遠くへ飛べない。as は『jeql target と書け』と言い、距離を見て決める: 近ければ短い分岐(beql)、遠ければ条件を反転して本物の jmp を跨ぐ(bneq .+N; jmp target)。これは古典的な span 依存命令問題——命令の長さが飛び先までの距離に依存するが、距離は間に挟まる命令の長さに依存し、それもまた距離に依存する。jxxxfix は楽観的に解く: 全部短いと仮定し、1バイトを溢れるものだけ jxxxbump で膨らまし、nchange を立てて再周、もう膨らまなくなるまで——コメントいわく『lazy topological sort』の不動点。c2 の最適化と同じ不動点の形。ソースは収まれば『win!』、溢れれば『immediate lose』と呟く。

text:
passno = 1; yyparse();        /* pass 1: build symbols, size each segment */
jxxxfix(); stabfix();          /* pass 1.5: fix branch lengths */
/* assign segment origins, final symbol values, write a.out header */
passno = 2; yyparse();        /* pass 2: emit machine code + relocation */
for (c=0; c<NLOC; c++) {       /* NLOC=4 location counters */
	usedot[c].xtype = XTEXT;       /* .text */
	usedot[c+NLOC].xtype = XDATA;  /* .data */
}

ai_analysis.technical_context: as reads the source twice because of forward references: you can use a label (jeql forward) before it is defined. Pass 1 (passno=1) collects all symbols and sizes each segment; jxxxfix then fixes branch lengths; segment origins and final symbol addresses are assigned; pass 2 (passno=2) emits the actual machine code and relocation. Symbols forward-referenced while undefined carry the XFORW flag, resolved later. as also keeps multiple location counters (NLOC=4): .text, .data, .bss end up in separate a.out segments, but the source may interleave .text and .data freely -- so as keeps a separate 'how far have I written' counter (usedot[]) per segment and gathers like with like regardless of source order, dotp pointing at the active one. Symbols are up to NCPS=8 chars, looked up in a hash table.
ai_analysis.human_story: Read it once to learn where every label lands, read it again to emit the code. And because a function can drop a data table mid-stream and return to code, the assembler keeps a separate ruler for text and for data, gathering each at the end.

appraiser_note: as がソースを2回読むのは前方参照のため: まだ定義されていないラベルを先に使える(jeql forward)。pass1(passno=1)で全シンボルを集め各セグメントの大きさを測り、jxxxfix が分岐長を確定、セグメント原点とシンボルの最終アドレスを割り当て、pass2(passno=2)で実際の機械語と再配置を吐く。未定義のまま前方参照されたシンボルには XFORW フラグが立ち後で解決。as は位置カウンタを複数持つ(NLOC=4): .text/.data/.bss は a.out では別セグメントに集まるが、ソースでは .text と .data を交互に書ける——so as はセグメントごとに『今どこまで書いたか』のカウンタ(usedot[])を別々に保ち、出現順と無関係に同種をまとめる(dotp が現在の位置を指す)。シンボルは NCPS=8 文字まで、ハッシュ表で引く。

ai_analysis.technical_context: What as finally emits is the a.out file. Its head is struct hdr -- magic 0410, text size, data size, bss size, symbol-table size, entry point, relocation sizes -- eight words. That header is the same layout adb's defs.h diagrams as 'Layout of a.out file'. as writes it; ld and adb read it. The whole toolchain meets on the a.out contract, and as is its issuer. The most telling part is #ifndef vax: writel() writes a long, but on a non-VAX host (comment: 'stupid fp-11' = PDP-11) it swaps the word halves. Why? Because this VAX assembler itself had to be compiled and run on a non-VAX machine -- generating a.out for the new VAX on the old PDP-11. Cross-assembly: building the new architecture's binaries on a machine with a different byte order. as was the keel of the ship carrying Unix to the VAX.
ai_analysis.human_story: as writes the a.out that ld links and adb dissects -- it issues the contract the whole toolchain meets on. And it could run on an old PDP-11 to build binaries for the new VAX, swapping byte order as it went: the assembler that carried Unix to a new machine.

appraiser_note: as が最後に吐くのは a.out。先頭は struct hdr——マジック 0410、テキスト/データ/bss/シンボル表サイズ、エントリポイント、再配置サイズ——の8ワード。このヘッダは adb の defs.h が『Layout of a.out file』として図示したレイアウトと同じ。as が書く側、ld と adb が読む側。ツールチェーン全体が a.out という契約で出会い、as はその発行元だ。最も味わい深いのが #ifndef vax: writel() は long を書くが、VAX でないホスト(コメントいわく 'stupid fp-11'=PDP-11)ではワードの上下を入れ替える。なぜか——この VAX アセンブラ自身が、まだ VAX 以外のマシンでコンパイル・実行される必要があったから。新しい VAX 用 a.out を古い PDP-11 上で生成する。バイトオーダーの違うマシンで目的のマシンのバイナリを組むクロスアセンブル——as は Unix を VAX へ運ぶ船の竜骨だった。

ai_analysis.technical_context: uucp (Unix-to-Unix copy) moves files between machines -- but in 1979 the link is a phone line and a modem. The g protocol (pk0.c/pk1.c) builds reliable transfer over that dirty medium with a sliding window. Waiting for an ACK after every packet would be ruinously slow over phone-line round trips, so the sender transmits up to a window (p_swindow) of packets ahead without waiting; each is counted as unacked (p_xcount), and an RR (receiver ready) for sequence N slides the window forward. Sequence numbers cycle mod 8 (next[8]) -- three bits to pair packets with acks. When p_xcount hits the window, the sender SLEEPs: flow control, so a fast sender never floods a slow receiver. Send ahead by the window, slide on ACK -- the same skeleton as TCP's sliding window, done over a phone line with 3-bit sequence numbers.
ai_analysis.human_story: Waiting for an ack after every packet would crawl over a phone line. So uucp sends a whole window of packets ahead, and only sleeps when the window fills -- the same trick TCP uses, running in 1979 over a modem.

appraiser_note: uucp(Unix-to-Unix copy)はマシン間でファイルを運ぶ——だが1979年、回線は電話線とモデムだ。g プロトコル(pk0.c/pk1.c)はその汚い媒体の上にスライディングウィンドウで信頼転送を築く。1パケットごとに ACK を待つと電話線の往復で遅すぎるので、送信側はウィンドウ(p_swindow)の分だけ確認を待たず先行送信する。各々を未確認(p_xcount)として数え、シーケンス N への RR(receiver ready)で窓が前へ滑る。シーケンス番号は mod 8 で巡回(next[8])——3ビットでパケットと ACK を対応づける。p_xcount が窓に達すると送信側は SLEEP: フロー制御で、速い送信側が遅い受信側を溢れさせない。窓の分だけ先行送信し ACK で滑らせる——TCP のスライディングウィンドウと同じ骨格を、電話線の上で3ビットのシーケンス番号で。

ai_analysis.technical_context: When the line opens, both ends handshake: INITA/INITB/INITC negotiate window size and packet size, and on agreement the state becomes LIVE -- the role TCP's three-way handshake plays, establishing the connection and agreeing parameters before data flows. Corruption is caught by a checksum: chksum() rotates the running sum left one bit (carrying the top bit back in) and adds each byte -- a rotate-add checksum of the same lineage as sum's. The receiver recomputes and compares (sum==CHECK); a mismatch means the line flipped a bit, so the packet is rejected (RJ) and retransmitted (RXMIT). The g protocol assumes corruption, loss, and delay as the normal case -- the same flaky phone-line world cu fought with 'sometimes missed carrier', tamed here at the protocol layer.
ai_analysis.human_story: Before sending data, the two ends agree on window and packet size; then every packet carries a rotate-and-add checksum. A flipped bit on the noisy line fails the check, and the packet is sent again. The protocol expects the line to be bad.

appraiser_note: 回線が開くと両端は握手する: INITA/INITB/INITC が窓サイズとパケット長を交渉し、合意できたら状態を LIVE にする——TCP の3ウェイハンドシェイクが接続を確立しパラメータを交渉するのと同じ役割。化けはチェックサムが捕らえる: chksum() は累算値を左1ビット回転(最上位ビットを下から戻す)してから各バイトを加える——sum と同じ系譜の回転加算チェックサム。受信側は計算し直して比較し(sum==CHECK)、不一致は回線がビットを化けさせた証拠なのでパケットを拒否(RJ)し再送(RXMIT)する。g プロトコルは化け・欠け・遅れを通常の事態として設計されている——cu が『sometimes missed carrier』と格闘した同じ気まぐれな電話線の世界を、ここではプロトコルの層で飼い慣らす。

ai_analysis.technical_context: uucp foo remote!path does not transfer on the spot. The uucp command's job is to write the request as a work file into the spool (it also kicks off uucico by default, but that runs async). The actual dialing and copying is a separate program, cico (copy-in/copy-out), run later -- often at night when calls are cheap. This is store-and-forward: the two machines need not be up at the same time. Your file sits in the local spool until cico next reaches the remote; if that machine relays further, it pools again and moves on at the next call. The bang path host1!host2!host3!user is exactly that hop-by-hop route written by hand. Stattext[] -- DIAL FAILED, LOGIN FAILED, WRONG TIME, SYSTEM LOCKED -- shows how earthy the calls were over the real phone network: if it does not connect, try again later.
ai_analysis.human_story: uucp does not send your file now; it drops it in a spool, and a separate program dials the other machine later -- maybe at 3am when calls are cheap. The two computers never have to be awake together. That is how files crossed the world before always-on networks.

appraiser_note: uucp foo remote!path はその場で転送しない。uucp コマンドの仕事は依頼をスプールに作業ファイルとして書くこと(既定では uucico も起動するが非同期に走る)。実際のダイヤルとコピーは別プログラム cico(copy-in/copy-out)が後で——しばしば通話料の安い深夜に——行う。これが store-and-forward: 両方のマシンが同時に起きている必要がない。ファイルはローカルのスプールに溜まり、cico が次に相手と繋がったときに運ばれる。相手がさらに中継するならそこでまた溜まり次の通話で先へ進む。バン・パス host1!host2!host3!user はまさにその一跳びずつの経路を手で書いたもの。Stattext[]——DIAL FAILED/LOGIN FAILED/WRONG TIME/SYSTEM LOCKED——が、現実の電話網の上の通話がいかに泥臭かったかを物語る: 繋がらなければ後で掛け直す。

ai_analysis.technical_context: uucp carried more than files: it was the network that connected the world before the Internet. Two giants grew on it. Email -- before user@host, mail was delivered along the bang path host1!host2!user, riding uucp calls hop by hop. And Usenet -- the vast distributed bulletin board was built on uucp's store-and-forward: articles copied machine to machine over nightly phone calls, rippling out to the world. Universities, companies, and labs connected not with expensive leased lines but with an ordinary phone line and uucp -- the poor man's network, open to anyone who already had a phone. cico's Role (MASTER = called, SLAVE = answered) shows it was peer-to-peer: no central server, no fixed IP, just machines that knew each other's phone numbers calling nightly. That loose web actually connected the world's Unix machines until TCP/IP spread in the 1990s.
ai_analysis.human_story: Before the Internet, the world's Unix machines were tied together by phone calls. Email and Usenet both rode uucp -- articles and messages copied from one machine to the next, night after night, until they had crossed the planet. A network anyone with a phone line could join.

appraiser_note: uucp が運んだのはファイルだけではない: インターネット以前に世界を繋いだネットワークそのものだった。二つの巨大なものが育った。電子メール——user@host 以前、メールはバン・パス host1!host2!user で uucp の通話を乗り継いで配達された。そして Usenet——巨大な分散掲示板網は uucp の store-and-forward の上に築かれ、記事は夜ごとの電話通話でマシンからマシンへ複製され世界へ広がった。大学も企業も研究所も、高価な専用線でなくただの電話線と uucp で繋がった——誰もが既に持つ電話だけで参加できる貧者のネットワーク。cico の Role(MASTER=掛けた側/SLAVE=受けた側)が示すようピアツーピアだった: 中央サーバも固定IPもなく、互いの電話番号を知るマシンが夜ごと掛け合う。その緩やかな網が、TCP/IP が普及する1990年代まで、世界中の Unix マシンを実際に繋いでいた。

ai_analysis.technical_context: sdb is the source-level debugger. adb spoke in addresses (0x1000,5?i); sdb speaks in the programmer's own source -- function names, variables, file:line. Its emblem is prframe(): a backtrace. Where adb gave a list of addresses, prframe walks each stack frame and prints it as function(args)   [file:line], e.g. main(1,2) [foo.c:42]. This is exactly the bt output you get from gdb today. What makes it possible is adrtofilep (address->source file) and adrtolineno (address->line) -- the reverse mapping from a raw machine address to a position in the source. sdb built a translation layer between the machine's addresses and the human's source.
ai_analysis.human_story: Crash, and adb hands you a column of numbers. sdb hands you 'foo.c:42, inside bar, called from main' -- the stack told as a story of function calls. That is the bt you still type into gdb.

appraiser_note: sdb はソースレベルデバッガ。adb はアドレスで語った(0x1000,5?i)が、sdb はプログラマ自身のソース——関数名・変数名・ファイル:行——で語る。その象徴が prframe()＝バックトレース。adb が番地の羅列を返したところを、prframe は各スタックフレームを遡り 関数名(引数) [ファイル:行] の形で表示する(例: main(1,2) [foo.c:42])。今 gdb で bt を打つと出るあれそのもの。可能にするのが adrtofilep(アドレス→ソースファイル)と adrtolineno(アドレス→行)＝生の機械番地をソース上の位置へ逆写像する仕掛け。sdb は機械の番地と人間のソースの間に翻訳の層を架けた。

text:
decode() {   /* decode a command line item by item */
	if (*q == '(') { scallf=1; cpname(proc,p); }   /* proc(...)  procedure call */
	if (*q == ':') { cpname(proc,p); }             /* proc:      function name */
	if (*q == '$') { cpname(var,p); }              /* var$       variable name */
	if (number(*p)) { integ = readint(&p); }       /* 42         line number */
}
/* docomm: t=trace  p=print  b=break  s=step  n=next  c=cont  d=delete */

ai_analysis.technical_context: adb's command was addr,count?format -- address, count, format. sdb's commands are the names of source things. decode() parses a function name (proc:), a variable name (var$), a procedure call (proc(...)), a decimal line number (42), and single-letter commands. So debugging uses the same vocabulary as reading the code: main:b breaks at main, 42b at line 42, x$/ prints variable x. docomm.c's command set (t=trace, p=print, b=break, s=step, n=next, c=continue, d=delete) maps almost one-to-one onto today's gdb (bt/print/break/step/next/continue/delete). The debugger command vocabulary was essentially finished at this moment: adb spoke the machine's language, sdb speaks the programmer's.
ai_analysis.human_story: Where adb made you compute 'this variable is so many bytes off the frame pointer', sdb lets you say 'show me x'. break at main, step a line, print a variable -- the words you still type into gdb were set here.

appraiser_note: adb のコマンドは addr,count?format——アドレス・個数・書式。sdb のコマンドはソースの構成要素の名前だ。decode() は関数名(proc:)、変数名(var$)、関数呼び出し(proc(...))、10進の行番号(42)、単一文字コマンドを切り分ける。だからデバッグはソースを読むのと同じ語彙でできる: main:b で main にブレーク、42b で42行目、x$/ で変数 x を表示。docomm.c のコマンド(t=トレース p=表示 b=ブレーク s=ステップ n=ネクスト c=継続 d=削除)は今日の gdb(bt/print/break/step/next/continue/delete)とほぼ一対一に対応する。デバッガの操作語彙はこの瞬間にほぼ完成した:adb が機械の言葉を話したなら、sdb はプログラマの言葉を話す。

ai_analysis.technical_context: sdb can answer 'show me x' because it knows the variable's type and where it lives -- from stab (symbol table debugging) entries the compiler plants in the symbol table for debugging. A C variable's home depends on its storage class: a global (N_GSYM) sits at a fixed address, a static (N_STSYM) likewise, a local is at an offset in the stack frame, and a register variable (N_RSYM) is not even in memory but in a register. formaddr() reads the stab's class and computes where the variable is now -- a register var from the current frame's saved-register area (getframe), a global from its fixed address. The stab also carries the type (int vs char* vs struct), so sdb interprets the raw bytes accordingly: where adb could only say 'these 4 bytes in hex', sdb says 'x is an int, value 42'. And the s command uses stab line info to run to the next SOURCE line, not the next machine instruction.
ai_analysis.human_story: How does sdb know x is an int in a register and not just four bytes somewhere? The compiler left notes -- stab entries -- saying each variable's type and where it lives. sdb reads the notes and shows you 'x = 42', and steps a source line at a time.

appraiser_note: sdb が『変数 x を見せて』に応えられるのは、変数の型と居場所を——コンパイラがデバッグのためシンボル表へ埋め込む stab(symbol table debugging)記号から——知っているから。C 変数の居場所は格納クラスで違う: グローバル(N_GSYM)は固定番地、静的(N_STSYM)も同様、ローカルはスタックフレーム上の相対位置、レジスタ変数(N_RSYM)はメモリですらなくレジスタの中。formaddr() は stab の格納クラスを見て変数が今どこにあるか計算する——レジスタ変数なら現在フレームのレジスタ退避域(getframe)から、グローバルなら固定番地から。stab は型(int か char* か struct か)も運ぶので、sdb は生バイト列を型に従って解釈する: adb が『この4バイトを16進で』としか言えなかったところを sdb は『x は int で値は42』と言える。s コマンドは stab の行情報で次のソース行まで実行する(機械語1命令でなくソース1行単位)。

ai_analysis.technical_context: sdb's design is two-layer. The lower layer is the mechanism inherited from adb: fork + ptrace(SETTRC) to seize the child, overwrite an instruction with BPT to set a breakpoint, read/write registers and memory via ptrace, wait for stops -- runpcs.c is nearly identical to adb's. The upper layer is the meaning sdb adds: reading stab entries to translate addresses into functions, files, lines, types. So the same BPT sets a breakpoint, but adb reports a failure as an address while sdb reports it as proc:line. Below it works in addresses; above it speaks in names. This split -- low-level process control vs high-level source correspondence -- is the universal debugger architecture sdb established, and it stands on a contract: the compiler must emit debug symbols (stab, later -g) or sdb cannot know types or line<->address maps. Compiler writes the notes, debugger reads them -- the contract dbx, then gdb and DWARF, refined.
ai_analysis.human_story: sdb is adb with a translator bolted on top. The same BPT breakpoint, the same ptrace -- but where adb says 'failed at 0x1000', sdb says 'failed at main:42'. The trick is that the compiler leaves notes in the binary, and the debugger reads them. That handshake is why gdb knows your source.

appraiser_note: sdb の設計は二層だ。下層は adb から受け継いだ機構: fork+ptrace(SETTRC) で子を捕まえ、命令を BPT で上書きしてブレークポイントを置き、ptrace でレジスタ/メモリを読み書きし、wait で停止を待つ——runpcs.c は adb のそれとほぼ同じ。上層が sdb の足した意味: stab 記号を読みアドレスを関数・ファイル・行・型へ翻訳する層。だから同じ BPT でブレークを置きながら、その失敗を adb は番地で、sdb は proc:line で告げる。下では番地で動き上では名前で語る。この分離(低レベルのプロセス制御 vs 高レベルのソース対応)が sdb の確立したデバッガの普遍アーキテクチャで、それは契約の上に立つ: コンパイラが stab(後の -g)デバッグ記号を吐かねば sdb は型も行↔番地対応も知れない。コンパイラが注釈を書きデバッガが読む——この契約を dbx、そして gdb と DWARF が精緻化した。

ai_analysis.technical_context: learn is a CAI (computer-aided instruction) system whose subject is Unix itself -- files, the ed editor, eqn, C. Its lessons live as scripts in /usr/lib/learn, by Brian Kernighan and Mike Lesk. dounit() runs one lesson and shows the heart of its method: it does not just display text. copy(0,scrin) shows the lesson, copy(1,stdin) lets the student actually do the task, and then a grading command embedded in the lesson script is run; wait(&didok) waits for it and status==0 means correct. Wrong, and it asks: try again, skip, or bye. This -- make the learner actually perform the task, then auto-grade by a checker's exit status -- is the oldest prototype of modern interactive coding lessons. Not read; do, and let the machine judge.
ai_analysis.human_story: learn does not show you how to use Unix and quiz you on it. It tells you to do the thing, lets you do it for real, then runs a hidden check and grades you by whether it exited cleanly. The interactive 'now you try' lesson, in 1979.

appraiser_note: learn は CAI(計算機支援教育)システムで、教える対象は Unix そのもの——ファイル操作・ed エディタ・eqn・C。レッスンは /usr/lib/learn に台本として置かれ、作者は Brian Kernighan と Mike Lesk。dounit() は1レッスンを実行し、その教え方の核心を見せる: 説明文を見せて終わりにしない。copy(0,scrin) で説明を表示、copy(1,stdin) で生徒に実際にやらせ、レッスン台本に仕込まれた採点コマンドを実行する。wait(&didok) でそれを待ち status==0 なら正解。間違えば try again/skip/bye を尋ねる。これ——学習者に実際に課題をやらせ、採点プログラムの終了ステータスで自動採点する——が現代のインタラクティブなコード学習の最も古い原型。読むのでなく、やる。そして機械が判定する。

ai_analysis.technical_context: learn's teaching is real because it does not simulate. To teach 'type ls', makpipe() forks a REAL interactive shell (/bin/sh -i) and pipes to it; the student's commands run for real against the real filesystem -- learn ed launches the real ed. The student works in a protected playpen (.../lib/play), a writable sandbox set up per lesson by start(), so rm and mv only touch the sandbox. Then: how to grade real work? maktee() splices a dedicated tee between learn and the screen, so it can keep a copy of everything the student's session printed; the lesson's checker inspects that transcript and the files made. makpipe (real shell) + maktee (watch the output) put learn in the teacher's seat: let the student work freely, but see everything. mysys() even sorts commands into EASY (direct fork+execv: mv/cp/rm/ls), MEDIUM (exec: */>/$), HARD (full shell: |;&) to avoid needless shell launches.
ai_analysis.human_story: learn teaches the real ed, the real shell -- inside a sandbox where your rm can do no harm. And it tees your whole session so a hidden checker can read over your shoulder and decide whether you got it right.

appraiser_note: learn の教育が本物なのは、模擬しないから。『ls と打って』を教えるのに、makpipe() は本物の対話シェル(/bin/sh -i)を fork してパイプで繋ぐ。生徒のコマンドは本物のファイルシステムに本当に実行される——learn で ed を学べば起動するのは本物の ed。生徒は保護された作業場(.../lib/play)＝レッスンごとに start() が整える書込み自由の砂場で操作するので、rm も mv も砂場の中だけ。では本物の操作をどう採点するか? maktee() が learn と画面の間に専用 tee を挟み、生徒のセッションが表示した一部始終の控えを取る。レッスンの採点コマンドはその控えや作ったファイルを調べる。makpipe(本物のシェル)＋maktee(出力の盗み見)が learn を教師の立ち位置に置く: 自由に操作させ、しかし全て把握する。mysys() はコマンドを EASY(直接fork+execv: mv/cp/rm/ls)/MEDIUM(exec: */>/$)/HARD(本物のシェル: |;&)に選り分け、無駄なシェル起動を避ける。

ai_analysis.technical_context: learn's lessons are not a single track. Each lesson script ends with a #next block listing the lessons that may follow, each with a difficulty number -- the lessons form a branching graph. selunit() chooses the next lesson by the student's speed (skill level): among the candidates it picks the one whose difficulty is closest to speed (abs(diff[m]-speed) minimal). A fast student (high speed) gets harder lessons, a struggling one easier; after each lesson whatnow() raises speed on success and lowers it on error, and the next selunit() branches on the new speed. already() skips finished lessons; a dead end backs up one level (nsave, once). This is adaptive learning -- the same 'watch the learner and adjust difficulty' as quiz (widen on right) and arithmetic (drill the missed numbers), now applied to the path through a whole curriculum. Not one fixed order for everyone; a path chosen per student by the machine.
ai_analysis.human_story: learn's lessons branch like a tree, each marked with a difficulty. It steers you to the next lesson whose difficulty best matches how you have been doing -- harder if you breeze through, easier if you stumble. A curriculum that finds each student's own path.

appraiser_note: learn のレッスンは一本道ではない。各台本の末尾の #next 節に、次に来られるレッスン候補が難度つきで並ぶ——レッスンは枝分かれするグラフを成す。selunit() は次のレッスンを生徒の speed(習熟度)で選ぶ: 候補のうち難度が speed に最も近いもの(abs(diff[m]-speed) 最小)を選ぶ。すらすら正解する生徒(speed 高)には難しいレッスンを、つまずく生徒には易しいものを。1レッスンごとに whatnow() が正解で speed を上げ不正解で下げ、次の selunit() が新しい speed で枝を選ぶ。already() で既習を飛ばし、行き止まりなら一段戻る(nsave、一度だけ)。これは適応学習——quiz(正解で範囲拡大)/arithmetic(間違えた数を集中ドリル)と同じ『学習者を見て難度を調整する』をカリキュラム全体の経路選択に及ぼしたもの。全員一律の固定順序でなく、一人ひとりの道を機械が選ぶ。

ai_analysis.technical_context: learn's C is tiny -- just an engine. The teaching content (what to teach, how to grade, where to branch) all lives in course scripts under /usr/lib/learn. main() merely loops: choose a course (selsub), pick a lesson (selunit), run and grade (dounit), adjust speed (whatnow). Like lex separating its ncform skeleton from the tables, learn separates engine from content: a script is text + tasks + a grading command + a #next branch table -- a 'program of instruction' the engine interprets. Add scripts and you teach a new course. Most learn of all is its reflexivity: to teach Unix it uses Unix -- a real shell inside, tee for the transcript, fork/exec/pipe/wait to drive lessons. The very tools the student is learning are the tools learn uses to teach them. The README asks admins to keep logs so lessons can be improved: the courseware grows as it is used. Kernighan and Lesk taught the system with the system.
ai_analysis.human_story: learn is barely there: 657 lines that just run scripts. The teaching is content, not code. And it teaches Unix using Unix -- piping into a real shell, teeing your output, forking processes. The tool teaches its own use by using itself.

appraiser_note: learn の C は驚くほど小さく、エンジンでしかない。教育の中身(何をどう教え採点し分岐するか)はすべて /usr/lib/learn のコース台本にある。main() はループするだけ: コースを選び(selsub)、レッスンを選び(selunit)、実行・採点し(dounit)、できに応じ speed を調整する(whatnow)。lex が ncform 雛形と表を分けたのと同じ、エンジンとコンテンツの分離: 台本は説明文＋課題＋採点コマンド＋#next 分岐表からなる『教育のプログラム』で、エンジンはそれを解釈するインタプリタ。台本を書き足せば新コースを教えられる。最も learn らしいのは反射性: Unix を教えるのに Unix を使う——本物のシェルを内に飼い、tee で控えを取り、fork/exec/pipe/wait でレッスンを駆動する。生徒が学んでいる道具をlearn 自身が教えるために使う。README はログを取りレッスンを改善するよう求める: 教材が使われながら育つ。Kernighan と Lesk は、システムをシステムで教えた。

ai_analysis.technical_context: prof is the execution profiler: after a run it tells you what fraction of time each function spent. You cannot learn that by reading the source -- you must measure. prof's method is statistical sampling: while the program runs, a profiling clock interrupts it periodically and records which address (PC) was executing; over thousands of samples, hot addresses get more hits. But samples accumulate by address, while humans want functions. This loop is the finish: prof knows each function's address range from the a.out symbol table, and distributes each histogram bucket's time to the function(s) whose range overlaps it (overlap), pro-rated when a bucket straddles two functions. A hill of addresses becomes time-per-function: 'strcmp 37%, malloc 22%'. Measure, attribute, show -- that is a profiler.
ai_analysis.human_story: How do you find where a program spends its time? Wake it up thousands of times and write down where it is each time. The addresses that come up most are the slow spots -- and this loop turns that pile of addresses back into function names.

appraiser_note: prof は実行プロファイラ: 走らせた後、各関数が実行時間の何%を使ったかを教える。それはソースを読んでも分からない——測るしかない。prof の方法は統計的サンプリング: 実行中、プロファイリングクロックが一定間隔で割り込み、その瞬間に実行中の番地(PC)を記録する。何千サンプルも取れば、よく実行される番地ほど多く当たる。だがサンプルは番地で集まり、人間が欲しいのは関数だ。このループが仕上げ: prof は a.out のシンボル表から各関数の番地範囲を知り、ヒストグラムの各バケツの時間を、それと重なる関数に按分する(overlap、バケツが二つの関数にまたがれば比例配分)。番地の山が関数ごとの時間に変わる——『strcmp 37%、malloc 22%』。測って、振り分けて、見せる——それがプロファイラ。

ai_analysis.technical_context: prof reads mon.out, written by the profiled program at runtime once it calls monitor(). The runtime gathers two kinds of data. First, the PC-sample histogram: a profiling clock interrupts periodically and bumps the bucket (a UNIT=short) for the current address; most of mon.out is this big array. A high bucket means that address range was executing often -- this is statistical (sample occasionally, infer where time goes), so it is cheap: the program runs near full speed, just one increment per interrupt. Second, the call-count table (cbuf, struct cnt = address + cncall): counting code (mcount) planted at each function's entry bumps a counter on every call -- exact counting. Time (sampled, approximate) plus calls (counted, exact): combining the two is prof's trick. The mon.out header (lowpc/highpc/ncount) gives the profiled range and the boundary between the two datasets.
ai_analysis.human_story: Two rulers. One samples: a clock pokes the program now and then and tallies where it is -- approximate but cheap. The other counts: a tick at every function's door, exact. prof reads both out of mon.out and plays them against each other.

appraiser_note: prof は mon.out を読む——プロファイル対象が実行中、monitor() を呼んで書き残したファイルだ。ランタイムは二種類のデータを集める。ひとつめが PC サンプルのヒストグラム: プロファイリングクロックが一定間隔で割り込み、現在の番地のバケツ(UNIT=short)を1つ増やす。mon.out の大半はこの巨大配列で、値の大きいバケツ＝そのアドレス範囲が頻繁に実行されていた。これは統計的(ときどき覗いて時間の所在を推し量る)なので速い: プログラムはほぼ通常速度で走り割り込みごとに1つ数えるだけ。ふたつめが呼び出し回数の表(cbuf、struct cnt＝アドレス＋cncall):各関数の入口に仕込まれた計数コード(mcount)が呼ばれるたびにカウンタを増やす——正確な計数。時間(サンプリング、近似)＋回数(計数、正確)。この二つを組み合わせるのが prof の妙。mon.out のヘッダ(lowpc/highpc/ncount)がプロファイル範囲と二データの境界を告げる。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Samples and counts accumulate by address; to show them, addresses must become names. prof reads the a.out symbol table, keeps the N_TEXT (function) symbols with their value and name, and sorts by address -- the same symbol table adb, sdb and ld read, used here to bind time to a location. The output is an optimization map: per function, %time (and cumsecs) from sampling, #call from counting, and ms/call = time / calls -- the two measurements divided. Sorted by time, the worst offender sits on top. ms/call distinguishes a light function called 100k times (called too much) from one called twice but heavy each time (bad algorithm). This is Kernighan's discipline 'Measure. Don't tune for speed until you've measured': do not guess where it is slow -- profile, and fix only the few functions at the top. Where c2 polished code mechanically, prof tells you WHICH code to polish.
ai_analysis.human_story: prof binds time to names using the same a.out symbol table the debugger and linker read, then prints %time, #call and ms/call sorted worst-first. ms/call tells the function called too often from the one that is simply slow. Don't guess what is slow -- measure.

appraiser_note: サンプルも回数も番地で集まる。見せるには番地を名前に変えねばならない。prof は a.out のシンボル表を読み、N_TEXT(関数)シンボルを value と name つきで拾い、番地順にソートする——adb/sdb/ld が読むのと同じシンボル表を、ここでは時間を場所に紐づけるのに使う。出力は最適化の地図: 各関数について %time(と cumsecs)はサンプリング由来、#call は計数由来、ms/call＝時間÷回数は両者の割り算。時間順にソートされ、一番重い犯人が上に来る。ms/call は、10万回呼ばれて時間を食う軽い関数(呼びすぎ)と、数回だが1回が重い関数(アルゴリズムが悪い)を見分けさせる。これはKernighan の規律『Measure. Don't tune for speed until you've measured(測れ、計測するまで速度に手を入れるな)』。どこが遅いか推測せず、プロファイルして上位の数関数だけ直す。c2 が機械的に磨いたのに対し、prof はどこを磨くべきかを教える。

ai_analysis.technical_context: With -v (#ifdef plot) prof also draws the profile: each function a bar proportional to its time, labeled by name, via plot's graphics (openpl/line/label) -- table and graph, two ways to show where the weight is. The method prof established -- statistical PC sampling plus symbol-table attribution to functions -- is still the basic shape of profiling. Its direct successor gprof adds the call graph (who called whom, how time propagates to callers); cc -pg plants the monitor()/mcount instrumentation in prof's direct line; and modern perf and samplers are the same at root: wake the program periodically, tally the PC, attribute by symbol. Faster sample rates, full call stacks, flame graphs -- but the 1979 skeleton, 'sample to measure, name by symbol', is unchanged. In the toolchain prof's role is distinct: c2/as/ld make the program, adb/sdb examine it, prof measures how it ran -- closing the make/fix/measure loop that feeds back into optimization.
ai_analysis.human_story: Every profiler since works prof's way: poke the program, note where it is, name it by symbol. gprof added the call graph, perf made it fast and drew flame graphs, but the 1979 idea -- sample to measure, symbols to name -- never changed.

appraiser_note: -v(#ifdef plot)をつければ prof はプロファイルを図にも描く: 各関数を時間に比例した棒として並べ名前を添える(plot の openpl/line/label)——表とグラフ、二つの見せ方。prof が確立した方式——統計的 PC サンプリング＋シンボル表による関数への按分——はプロファイリングの基本形として今に続く。直接の後継 gprof は呼び出しグラフ(誰が誰を呼び時間が呼び出し元へどう伝播するか)を足し、cc -pg は monitor()/mcount の計測を prof の直系として埋め込み、現代の perf やサンプラーも根は同じ: プログラムを定期的に叩き起こし PC を数えシンボルで関数に振り分ける。サンプル周期が速くなりコールスタック全体を取れ可視化が華やかになっても、1979年の骨格『サンプリングで測りシンボルで名づける』は変わらない。ツールチェーンの中で prof の役回りは独特: c2/as/ld が作り、adb/sdb が調べ、prof がどう動いたかを測る——作って直して測るループを閉じ、最適化へ還す。

ai_analysis.technical_context: plot is device-independent graphics, by Mike Lesk. In 1979 there were Tektronix vector terminals, DASI dot terminals, Versatec raster plotters -- each with different commands and resolution. Rather than write per-device code in every program, plot defines plot(5): a compact binary command stream -- m (move), l (line), c (circle), a (arc), t (text), p (point), s (space), f (linemod) -- with 2-byte little-endian coordinates read by getsi(). driver.c's fplt() reads it one char at a time and calls a drawing primitive. The intent ('a circle at 100,100 radius 50') is the same on any device. Like troff's intermediate output and as's a.out, plot(5) separates WHAT to draw from HOW: a program emits plot(5), a device filter renders it -- graphics made a stream of bytes that flows through a pipe.
ai_analysis.human_story: Every plotter and terminal spoke a different language. plot invents one in between -- m, l, c for move, line, circle -- so a program says 'draw a circle' once, as bytes in a pipe, and any device can render it.

appraiser_note: plot は Mike Lesk によるデバイス非依存グラフィックス。1979年、Tektronix のベクタ端末・DASI のドット端末・Versatec のラスタプロッタが乱立し、命令も解像度も違った。プログラムごとに機械別のコードを書く代わりに、plot は plot(5)＝コンパクトなバイナリのコマンド列を定義する: m(move)/l(line)/c(circle)/a(arc)/t(text)/p(point)/s(space)/f(linemod)、座標は getsi() が2バイトのリトルエンディアンで読む。driver.c の fplt() がそれを1文字ずつ読み描画プリミティブを呼ぶ。『中心(100,100)半径50の円』という意図はどの機械でも同じ。troff の中間出力や as の a.out と同じく、plot(5) は『何を描くか』を『どう描くか』から分ける: プログラムが plot(5) を吐きデバイスフィルタが描く——図形さえパイプを流れるバイト列になる。

text:
# Makefile: same driver.o, relinked per device
tek:   driver.o ; cc -o tek  driver.o -l4014 -lm   # Tektronix 4014
t300:  driver.o ; cc -o t300 driver.o -l300  -lm   # DASI 300
t450:  driver.o ; cc -o t450 driver.o -l450  -lm   # DASI 450
vplot: chrtab.o vplot.o ; cc -o vplot vplot.o chrtab.o   # Versatec raster
# plot.sh dispatches by $TERM: -T4014->tek  -Tver->vplot  -T450->t450

ai_analysis.technical_context: With plot(5) as the common language, you just need a way to render it per device -- and plot's trick is in the linking. driver.c CALLS move/line/circle/label but does not define them; the bodies live in per-device libraries (libplot): -l4014 defines Tektronix's line/circle, -l450 DASI's. So the SAME driver.o, relinked against a different library, becomes tek (draws on Tektronix) or t450 (draws on DASI). Versatec differs enough (raster) to be its own program, but the primitives called are the same names. plot.sh dispatches: it reads $TERM or -T and exec's tek for -T4014, vplot for -Tver. Swapping device implementations behind an abstract interface is what we now call plugins, device drivers, polymorphism -- plot did it by the humblest means, link-time library substitution. One call line(x0,y0,x1,y1) becomes a Tektronix vector command or a Versatec bit, depending only on what was linked.
ai_analysis.human_story: How do you support five plotters with one program? Write the interpreter once, and link it against a different drawing library for each device. The same line() becomes Tektronix vectors or Versatec dots, decided at link time. Polymorphism via the linker.

appraiser_note: plot(5) という共通言語があれば、あとは機械ごとに描き方を用意すればいい——plot の妙はリンクにある。driver.c は move/line/circle/label を呼ぶが実体を持たず、実体はデバイスごとのライブラリ(libplot)側にある: -l4014 は Tektronix のline/circle を、-l450 は DASI のそれを定義する。だから同じ driver.o を別ライブラリにリンクし直すだけで、Tektronix に描く tek、DASI に描く t450 が生まれる。Versatec はラスタで描き方が大きく違うので独立プログラムだが、呼ばれるプリミティブ名は同じ。plot.sh が振り分け役で、$TERM や -T を見て -T4014 なら tek、-Tver なら vplot を exec する。抽象インターフェースの背後でデバイス実装を差し替えるのは、今でいうプラグイン/デバイスドライバ/ポリモーフィズム——plot はそれを最も素朴な手段=リンク時のライブラリ差し替えで実現した。同じ line(x0,y0,x1,y1) が、リンク次第でTektronix のベクタ命令にも Versatec のビットにもなる。

ai_analysis.technical_context: vplot (the Versatec raster implementation) turns the abstract 'line' into actual dots. A raster plotter can only set dots on a 200-dot/inch grid; which dots best approximate a slanted line is rasterization. vplot's line() is a Bresenham-style integer algorithm -- no floating point. It steps one dot at a time along the longer axis (x if dx>=dy), accumulating an error term (res1/res2), and when it crosses the threshold steps the shorter axis: res1 += dy (add the slope's numerator), and if (res1>res2) { ...; y0 += yinc; } (when it builds up, bump y). No multiply, no divide -- just adds and compares draw the dots nearest the ideal line, fast on a tiny 1979 machine. Line style (solid/dashed) is the linmod bitmask: if((x0+slope*y0)&linmod) decides whether to set a dot, so DOTTED=014 / SHORTDASHED=034 patterns emerge. Integer adds draw the line; a bitmask cuts the dashes.
ai_analysis.human_story: A diagonal line on a dot grid: which dots? vplot answers with adds and compares only -- Bresenham's trick -- stepping the long axis and nudging the short one as error builds. Dashes fall out of ANDing the coordinate with a bit pattern.

appraiser_note: vplot(Versatec のラスタ実装)は抽象的な『直線』を実際のドット列に変える。ラスタプロッタは 200 dot/inch の格子に点を打つことしかできず、斜め線をどの点で近似するかがラスタ化だ。vplot の line() は Bresenham 系の整数アルゴリズム——浮動小数点なし。長い軸(dx>=dy なら x)に沿って1ドットずつ進み、誤差項(res1/res2)を累積し、閾値を超えたら短い軸を進める: res1 += dy(傾きの分子を足す)、if(res1>res2){...; y0+=yinc;}(溜まったら y を上げる)。掛け算も割り算もなく足し算と比較だけで理想の線に最も近いドット列を描く——1979年の小さな計算機で速い。線種(実線/破線)は linmod のビットマスク: if((x0+slope*y0)&linmod) で点を打つか決め、DOTTED=014/SHORTDASHED=034 のパターンになる。整数の足し算で線を引き、ビットマスクで破線を刻む。

ai_analysis.technical_context: The Versatec is 11 inches wide at 200 dot/inch; the whole bitmap is huge -- ~1000 blocks (about 500KB) in /usr/tmp/raster, far more than 1979 core. vplot handles this image-bigger-than-memory by block paging: the bitmap lives in a temp file and only some blocks (up to NB=88) are held in memory. point(x,y) first computes which block the dot belongs to (bno); if that differs from the block in memory, getblk() reads it in (writing back an old block if needed), then sets the right bit in the right byte. This is the same problem early Unix kept solving -- icheck managing every block with one bit, ed spilling a file out of core -- handling data too big for memory with secondary storage and block transfer, by hand, before virtual memory was capable. When drawing finishes, the giant paged bitmap is streamed to the Versatec: vector intent -> integer rasterization -> paged bitmap -> physical ink.
ai_analysis.human_story: The full plotter bitmap was half a megabyte -- it could not fit in 1979 memory. So vplot keeps it in a temp file and pages blocks in and out by hand, setting one bit per dot, then streams the whole thing to the plotter. An image too big to hold, drawn a block at a time.

appraiser_note: Versatec は 11インチ幅・200 dot/inch で、ビットマップ全体は巨大——/usr/tmp/raster に約1000ブロック(約500KB)、1979年のメモリには到底収まらない。vplot はこの『メモリより大きい絵』をブロックページングで扱う: ビットマップを一時ファイルに置き、メモリには一部のブロック(最大 NB=88)だけ持つ。point(x,y) はまず点がどのブロックに属するか(bno)を計算し、メモリ内のブロックと違えば getblk() で読み込み(必要なら古いブロックを書き戻し)、正しいバイトの正しいビットを立てる。これは初期 Unix が繰り返し解いた問題——icheck がビットマップで全ブロックを1ビット管理し、ed がファイルをメモリ外に退避したのと同じ、メモリに収まらないデータを二次記憶とブロック転送で手作業で扱う、仮想記憶が貧弱だった時代の技。描画が終わると巨大なページングビットマップを Versatec へ流し込む: ベクタの意図→整数ラスタ化→ページングビットマップ→物理的なインク。

ai_analysis.technical_context: sa is process accounting -- it interprets /usr/adm/acct, the file the kernel appends to every time a process exits. Each struct acct (sys/acct.h) records who (ac_uid), what command (ac_comm, first 8 chars), and how long (elapsed ac_etime, user CPU ac_utime, system CPU ac_stime), plus flags. This is the prototype of an OS-level audit log: the application does not log itself; the OS records every process that runs, so the trail is complete. doacct cleans the raw records -- empty name to '?', control/non-ASCII to '?' -- and marks with '*' a process that forked but died before exec (its name is still the parent's). Then it tallies per user (user[uid]) and per command (tab[] via enter()). Why keep such a ledger? In 1979 computers were expensive and shared by time-sharing; who used how much resource was the basis for billing, health checks, and abuse detection.
ai_analysis.human_story: Every process that ever ran left a line in a ledger the kernel kept. sa reads that ledger -- who ran what, for how long -- and adds it up. The OS, not the program, did the bookkeeping, so nothing escaped the record.

appraiser_note: sa はプロセス会計——カーネルがプロセス終了ごとに追記する /usr/adm/acct を解釈する。各 struct acct(sys/acct.h)が、誰が(ac_uid)・何のコマンドを(ac_comm 先頭8文字)・どれだけ(経過 ac_etime/ユーザCPU ac_utime/システムCPU ac_stime)使ったか、＋フラグを記録する。これは OS レベル監査ログの原型: アプリが自分でログを書くのでなく OS が走った全プロセスを記録するので台帳は完全。doacct は生レコードを掃除し(空名→'?'、制御/非ASCII→'?')、fork したが exec せず死んだプロセス(名前は親のまま)に '*' を付ける。そしてユーザ別(user[uid])とコマンド別(tab[]、enter())に集計する。なぜそんな台帳が要るか: 1979年コンピュータは高価でタイムシェアリングで共有され、誰がどれだけ資源を使ったかが課金・健康診断・不正検知の根拠だった。

ai_analysis.technical_context: An accounting record is written once per process that runs -- tens or hundreds of thousands a day on a busy system -- so each record must be tiny. The kernel packs each time (clock ticks) into 16 bits as comp_t, a pseudo-float, and expand() decodes it: take the low 13 bits as the mantissa (t & 017777, octal = 0x1FFF), the high 3 bits as the exponent (t >> 13), and shift the mantissa left 3 bits per exponent unit -- so value = mantissa * 8^exponent (each <<3 multiplies by 8). Exponent 0 keeps small values exact; a larger exponent shifts big values up, coarsely. No floating-point hardware -- integer shifts alone span a huge range of times in 16 bits at 13-bit precision. Shrinking one record by two bytes matters across hundreds of thousands -- the same motive behind modern log compression and time-series databases bit-packing timestamps and values.
ai_analysis.human_story: One accounting record per process, hundreds of thousands a day -- so each must be small. Times are squeezed into 16 bits as a tiny pseudo-float (13-bit mantissa, 3-bit exponent), expanded by shifting. The eternal logging problem -- write a lot, write each one small -- answered in 1979.

appraiser_note: 会計レコードは走ったプロセスの数だけ書かれる——混雑したシステムなら1日に何万〜何十万件——ので1件は極小でなければならない。カーネルは各時間(クロックティック)を 16ビットの comp_t(疑似浮動小数)に詰め、expand() が復号する: 下位13ビットを仮数(t & 017777、8進=0x1FFF)、上位3ビットを指数(t >> 13)とし、指数のぶん仮数を3ビットずつ左シフトする——つまり 値＝仮数 × 8^指数(各 <<3 が×8)。指数0なら小さな値は精密に、指数が大きければ大きな値を粗く左シフト。浮動小数点ハードウェアなしで、整数シフトだけで13ビット精度を保ち桁違いに広い時間範囲を16ビットに収める。1件を2バイト縮めることが何十万件では効く——現代のログ圧縮や時系列DBがタイムスタンプ/値をビットパッキングするのと同じ動機。

ai_analysis.technical_context: To fold tens of thousands of records by command, sa needs a table keyed by command name. enter() hashes the name with a polynomial (i = i*7 + char) and resolves collisions by open-addressing linear probe -- the same Bell Labs hash seen in ncheck and as. All records of one command land in one tab[] row, accumulating count and time. sa also rolls totals forward: init() reads prior totals from /usr/adm/savacct (per command) and /usr/adm/usracct (per user) and adds them in; run sa -s nightly (cron) and it merges the day's raw acct into the totals and truncates the raw log -- the rollup pattern of metrics aggregation, so the raw log never bloats but the totals survive. strip() reduces noise: rare commands (count <= thres) are folded interactively into one **junk** line. Output sorts by count (ncmp), time (tcmp), or average (bcmp) -- the same data shown from whatever angle you need.
ai_analysis.human_story: sa buckets records by command name in a hash table, carries yesterday's totals forward so the raw log can be wiped without losing history, and folds rare commands into a junk line. Roll up, trim noise, sort by what you care about -- exactly what a metrics tool still does.

appraiser_note: 何万件ものレコードをコマンド別に畳むには、コマンド名で引ける表が要る。enter() は名前を多項式ハッシュ(i = i*7 + 文字)し、オープンアドレス法の線形探索で衝突を解く——ncheck や as で見た Bell Labs 定番のハッシュ。同じコマンドの全レコードが同じ tab[] 行に集まり回数と時間が積み上がる。sa は総計も繰り越す: init() が前回までの累計を /usr/adm/savacct(コマンド別)と /usr/adm/usracct(ユーザ別)から読み足し込む。sa -s を日次(cron)で走らせると生 acct を累計にマージし生ログを切り詰める——メトリクス集計のロールアップパターンで、生ログは肥大しないが総計は残る。strip() はノイズ削減: 珍しいコマンド(count <= thres)を対話的に1行の **junk** にまとめる。出力は回数順(ncmp)/時間順(tcmp)/平均順(bcmp)——同じデータを知りたい切り口で見せる。

ai_analysis.technical_context: sa's function name says it all: printmoney. It maps each uid to a username via getpw(/etc/passwd) and prints how many commands the user ran and how much CPU time they used -- the basis for billing. In the time-sharing era compute was a product: a computing center charged users or departments by CPU time consumed, and printmoney's 'user smith: 12.5 CPU minutes' became a line on the invoice. This idea has returned with force: cloud pay-as-you-go -- AWS charging by the CPU-second, the GB -- is exactly sa's 'measure usage and charge', done at planetary scale. Beyond billing, sa is audit: which command eats the most time (a performance lead), who launched abnormally many processes (abuse/runaway). An OS recording every process and a tool summarizing it to find anomalies is the oldest form of observability and security audit. Where prof measured inside one program, sa measures the whole running system.
ai_analysis.human_story: The function is literally called printmoney: it turns CPU minutes per user into a bill. That is time-sharing accounting -- and it is exactly what your cloud invoice does today, CPU-seconds times a rate. The same ledger also tells you who is hogging the machine.

appraiser_note: sa の関数名が目的を露わにする: printmoney。getpw(/etc/passwd)で uid をユーザ名に変え、各ユーザが実行したコマンド数と使ったCPU時間を出す——課金の基礎だ。タイムシェアリング時代、計算資源は売り物で、計算センターはユーザや部門に使ったCPU時間で課金し、printmoney の『ユーザ smith：CPU 12.5分』がそのまま請求書の一行になった。この発想は強烈に回帰している: クラウドの従量課金——AWS が秒単位でCPUを、GB単位でストレージを課金するのは、sa の『使用量を測り課金する』を地球規模でやっているにほかならない。課金だけでなく sa は監査でもある: どのコマンドが最も時間を食うか(性能問題の手がかり)、誰が異常に多くのプロセスを起動したか(不正/暴走)。OS が全プロセスを記録しツールが集計して異常を見つける——現代のオブザーバビリティとセキュリティ監査の最も古い形。prof がプログラム1本の中を測ったなら、sa は走り続けるシステム全体を測る。

ai_analysis.technical_context: pcat interprets the Graphic Systems C/A/T phototypesetter's command stream -- the device-DEPENDENT output troff emitted to drive that one physical machine -- and re-creates on a plot(5) surface what the typesetter would have printed (a simulator; main opens with openpl/space). The C/A/T's output is not a human language: it is the machine's physical motions coded as bytes. A byte with the high bit set is an escape (horizontal carriage motion); it accumulates in esc and advances xx, with rx carrying the remainder for integer precision. A 0140-pattern byte is leading (vertical paper feed, the word from hot-metal spacing); it accumulates in leadtot and, when it exceeds the page length pl, flushes the page and starts another. This is the opposite of plot's abstract m/l (move/line): not 'draw from here to there' but 'feed the head 7 units, feed the paper 3' -- the output of typesetting software fused with the drive signals of one machine, just before device independence was invented.
ai_analysis.human_story: The C/A/T's language was its own motion: a byte to nudge the head sideways, a byte to feed the paper down. troff spoke it to one specific machine. pcat reads that motion back and replays what the typesetter would have set.

appraiser_note: pcat は Graphic Systems C/A/T 写植機のコマンド列——troff がその1台の物理機械を駆動するために吐いたデバイス依存出力——を解釈し、写植機が印画したであろう結果を plot(5) 面に再現する(シミュレータ。main は openpl/space で始まる)。C/A/T の出力は人間の言語ではなく、機械の物理的動作をコード化したバイト列だ。最上位ビットが立つバイトは escape(横方向の送り)で esc に積み上がり xx を進める(rx が端数を持ち越し整数精度を保つ)。0140 系のバイトは leading(紙の縦送り、活版の込め物に由来する語)で leadtot に累積し、ページ長 pl を超えるとページを吐いて次を始める。これは plot の抽象的な m/l(move/line)の対極だ: 『ここからここへ線を引け』でなく『ヘッドを7単位送れ、紙を3単位送れ』——組版ソフトの出力が1台の機械の駆動信号と一体だった、デバイス非依存が発明される直前の世界。

text:
case 0101: railmag &= ~01; continue;   /* lower rail */
case 0102: railmag |= 01;  continue;   /* upper rail */
case 0103: railmag |= 02;  continue;   /* upper mag */
case 0104: railmag &= ~02; continue;   /* lower mag */
...
i = (i & 077) | mcase;                  /* char: low 6 bits + case half */
if(railmag != 03) j = asctab[i];        /* normal font */
else              j = spectab[i];       /* special font when railmag==3 */

ai_analysis.technical_context: C/A/T characters came from a spinning photographic font disk: a transparent film engraved with glyphs spun fast, and a flash exposed photo paper the instant the wanted glyph passed the light. But a disk holds only so many glyphs, so the C/A/T solved it physically: the disk had two rails, each with two magnification lenses -- four font positions. pcat's railmag holds that physical state in two bits: lower/upper rail toggles bit 01, upper/lower mag toggles bit 02. troff's 'switch to italic' became the machine command 'raise the rail'. railmag==03 selects the special font (spectab, math symbols), else the normal font (asctab); mcase (lower/upper case) picks the top or bottom half of the 64-char set. This is hardware constraint becoming software model: early troff could use only four fonts at once because the disk physically had four positions. A typeface was not abstract data -- it was a fixed place on a spinning wheel.
ai_analysis.human_story: Why could early troff use only four fonts at once? Because the C/A/T's spinning font disk had exactly four positions -- two rails times two lenses. 'Switch to italic' meant physically moving the wheel. A font was a slot on a spinning disk.

appraiser_note: C/A/T の文字は回転する写真フォント盤から来た: 文字を刻んだ透明フィルムの円盤が高速回転し、目的の文字が光源を通った一瞬に閃光が印画紙に焼く。だが円盤に刻める文字数には限りがあり、C/A/T は物理的に解いた: 盤に2本の rail(溝)があり各々に2段の magnification(倍率)レンズ——計4つのフォント位置。pcat の railmag が1変数2ビットでその物理状態を映す: lower/upper rail が 01 ビット、upper/lower mag が 02 ビット。troff の『イタリックに切替』は機械命令『rail を上げよ』になった。railmag==03 は特殊フォント(spectab、数式記号)、それ以外は通常フォント(asctab)、mcase(大文字/小文字)が64文字集合の上半分/下半分を選ぶ。ハードウェア制約がソフトウェアのモデルになった例: 初期 troff が同時に4フォントまでだったのは、盤に物理的に4位置しかなかったから。書体は抽象データでなく、回る円盤上の決まった場所だった。

text:
int rtab[] = {6,7,8,9,10,11,12,14,16,18,20,22,24,28,26,18};  /* the only point sizes the machine had */
if((i & 0360) == 0120){                /* size-change code */
	for(j=0; i!=(stab[j]&017); j++);     /* map code to size table */
	size = stab[j]; psize = rtab[j];
	if(!(osize&DBL) && (size&DBL)) i = -55;   /* adjust escape for double size */
}
case 'i': j = 432;   /* 1 inch = 432 units */   case 'P': j = 72; /*pica*/   case 'p': j = 6; /*point*/

ai_analysis.technical_context: The C/A/T offered only the type sizes it physically had. rtab[] lists them: 6,7,8,9,10,11,12,14,16,18,20,22,24,28,26,18 points -- no arbitrary size. A .ps 13 in troff has no 13-point on the C/A/T; the size-change code (0120-family) maps through stab[] to a real rtab[] size. The DBL bit means double size, and switching to it nudges the escape by +-55 to fix the baseline shift when the lens changes -- a physical fudge. And the C/A/T's coordinate unit is 1/432 inch: case 'i' = 432 units, a pica 72, a point 6 (432/72). That INCH=432 recurs through the troff family source -- troff, tbl, pic all computed lengths in the C/A/T's 1/432 inch. The very numeric sense of the typesetting software was built around one machine's resolution. Sizes by physical lens, resolution by one device -- the opposite of today's infinite scaling.
ai_analysis.human_story: Ask the C/A/T for 13-point and there is none -- only the 16 sizes its lenses could make. And its world was measured in 1/432 of an inch, a number that runs all through troff's source. The software's sense of size and distance was one machine's body.

appraiser_note: C/A/T で使える文字サイズは機械が物理的に持つものだけ。rtab[] がその一覧: 6,7,8,9,10,11,12,14,16,18,20,22,24,28,26,18 ポイント——任意の大きさには出来ない。troff の .ps 13(13ポイント)は C/A/T に無く、サイズ変更コード(0120 系)が stab[] を引いて rtab[] の実在サイズに丸める。DBL ビットは倍角で、切替時に送りを ±55 補正する——レンズが変わると基準位置がずれる物理的辻褄合わせ。そして C/A/T の座標単位は 1/432 インチ: case 'i' が 432単位、パイカ 72、ポイント 6(432/72)。この INCH=432 は troff 一族のソースに繰り返し現れ、troff/tbl/pic は長さを C/A/T の 1/432 インチで計算した。組版ソフトの数値感覚そのものが1台の機械の解像度で作られていた。サイズは物理レンズで、解像度は1機種で——デジタル時代の無段階拡大の対極。

text:
openpl(); space(0,0,4095,3071);          /* render onto a plot(5) surface */
...
if (j==0){ trace(railmag==03 ? drawtab[i] : moretab[i]); continue; }  /* not in table: draw it */
else if(j){ oput(j); alpha++; }          /* in table: place it (oput -> label()) */
# define SCL(x) (psize*x/10)             /* scale strokes by point size */
trace(p){ while(*p) switch(*p){ case 'l': line(PT(p[1],p[2]),PT(p[3],p[4])); p+=5; ... } }

ai_analysis.technical_context: pcat does not drive a real C/A/T; main opens a plot(5) surface (openpl; space) and re-creates what the typesetter would have printed -- a simulator (the source calls itself a C version of tcatsim). Real C/A/T output was expensive and slow to develop on photo paper; pcat lets you see how troff would set a page right now. Characters come out two ways: those mapped in asctab/spectab are placed by oput() via plot's label() (asctab/spectab map C/A/T codes to ASCII glyphs); characters not in the table (j==0) are drawn from stroke lists in drawtab/moretab via trace()'s line() calls, scaled to the current point size by SCL. pcat is the end of the troff pipeline -- eqn/tbl/pic prepare, troff sets to C/A/T codes, pcat makes it visible. But typesetting was still bound to one machine; the later arc -- ditroff's device independence, then PostScript -- freed it. pcat is the moment just before that freedom, when troff was still tied to the Graphic Systems C/A/T's spinning disk and 1/432 inch.
ai_analysis.human_story: No real typesetter, no wet photo paper: pcat opens a plot surface and shows you what the C/A/T would have set. Known glyphs are placed as labels, exotic ones drawn stroke by stroke. The last stage of troff -- and the last era when typesetting belonged to one machine.

appraiser_note: pcat は本物の C/A/T を駆動しない。main は plot(5) 面を開き(openpl; space)、写植機が印画したであろう結果を再現する——シミュレータ(ソース自身が tcatsim の C 版と名乗る)。本物の C/A/T 出力は高価で印画紙の現像が遅く、pcat があれば troff がページをどう組むか即座に見られる。文字は二通りで出る: asctab/spectab に対応がある文字は oput() が plot の label() で置く(asctab/spectab は C/A/T コードを ASCII 文字へ写す表)。表に無い文字(j==0)は drawtab/moretab のストローク列から trace() の line() で描き、SCL で現在の級数にスケールする。pcat は troff パイプラインの末端——eqn/tbl/pic が整え、troff が C/A/T コードに組み、pcat が見える形にする。だが組版はまだ1台の機械に縛られていた。その後の弧——ditroff の device-independence、そして PostScript——が解放した。pcat はその自由の直前、troff がまだ Graphic Systems C/A/T の回る円盤と 1/432 インチに結びついていた瞬間だ。

ai_analysis.technical_context: calendar is a reminder service in ~70 lines (calendar.c + calendar.sh): each user keeps a plain ~/calendar file of lines like 'Jan 15  dentist', and cron (typically on weekdays) mails them the lines matching today and tomorrow. The gem is main's switch with NO break. C falls through to the next case when break is omitted -- usually a bug, here deliberate design. On Friday (case 5) it prints today, then Saturday, falls through to case 6 for Sunday, falls through to default for Monday -- so Friday's reminder covers Sat+Sun+Mon. Why? calendar runs only on weekdays, so Friday must look ahead over the weekend. Rather than write 'if Friday look 3 days else if Saturday 2 else 1', calendar stacks the cases and omits break: the physical order of the cases IS the look-ahead count. The ABSENCE of break is the logic -- the kind of intentional fallthrough later languages (Go, Rust, Swift) made you opt into.
ai_analysis.human_story: Run on Friday, calendar quietly reminds you of Saturday, Sunday AND Monday -- because no one runs it on the weekend. It does this not with extra logic but by leaving out the break, letting the cases fall into each other. Code that works by what it does not write.

appraiser_note: calendar は約70行(calendar.c + calendar.sh)のリマインダサービス: 各ユーザが ~/calendar に 'Jan 15  歯医者' のような平文を置き、cron が(典型的には平日に)今日と明日に当たる行をメールで届ける。白眉は main の switch に break が無いこと。C は break を省くと次の case へ流れ落ちる——普通はバグだが、ここでは意図的な設計。金曜(case 5)は今日を出し土曜を足し、case 6 へ流れ落ちて日曜、default へ流れ落ちて月曜——つまり金曜のリマインダは土+日+月を覆う。なぜか: calendar は平日しか走らないので金曜に週末を先読みする必要がある。『if 金曜なら3日先 else if 土曜なら2日 else 1日』と書く代わりに、case を積み重ね break を省く: case の物理的な並び順が先読み日数になる。break の不在がロジック——後の言語(Go/Rust/Swift)が opt-in にした意図的フォールスルー。

ai_analysis.technical_context: The C program /usr/lib/calendar does one thing: emit a regular expression that matches today's (or tomorrow's) date. tprint() turns a date into an egrep pattern that absorbs how people actually write dates: Jan 15, jan 15, January 15, 1/15, 1/5 all match. Month names use a case-class first letter ([Jj]an), %s[^ ]* catches the 'uary' of January, %d/ allows the numeric form, 0*%d absorbs day zero-padding (1/05 and 1/5), and the surrounds (^|[ (,;]) ... ([^0123456789]|$) keep word boundaries so 15 does not match inside 150 or 1500. calendar writes no date-matching logic of its own; it builds 'the regex for today' and hands the actual matching to egrep -- the same compose-and-delegate seen when ptx hands sorting to sort or bc hands arithmetic to dc. A program generating input (a pattern) for another tool to run: Unix metaprogramming.
ai_analysis.human_story: calendar never parses a date. It writes a regular expression that means 'today' -- matching Jan 15 or January 15 or 1/15 -- and lets egrep do the matching. The program's job is to describe what to find, not to find it.

appraiser_note: C プログラム /usr/lib/calendar の仕事はただ一つ: 今日(や明日)の日付に当たる正規表現を吐くこと。tprint() は日付を、人が予定を書くときの自然な揺れを吸収する egrep パターンに変える: Jan 15/jan 15/January 15/1/15/1/5 すべてに当たる。月名は先頭を大小文字クラス([Jj]an)、%s[^ ]* で January の uary を拾い、%d/ で数字形式、0*%d で日のゼロ詰め(1/05 も 1/5 も)、前後の (^|[ (,;]) … ([^0123456789]|$) で語境界を守り 15 が 150/1500 に誤マッチしないようにする。calendar は日付照合の専用ロジックを書かず、『今日の正規表現』を組み立てて照合は egrep に委ねる——ptx がソートを sort に、bc が計算を dc に委ねたのと同じ合成と委譲。プログラムが別ツールのための入力(パターン)を生成する Unix のメタプログラミング。

text:
/usr/lib/calendar >$tmp      # generate the egrep pattern for today/tomorrow
case $# in
0)  egrep -f $tmp calendar;; # no args: match the local ./calendar file
*)  ... per-user (see next) ...
esac

ai_analysis.technical_context: calendar.sh assembles the whole thing. It runs /usr/lib/calendar (the C program) to emit today/tomorrow's egrep pattern to a temp file, then egrep -f matches that pattern against the user's calendar file -- plain text. Matching lines, the day's events, print out. This is the Unix philosophy distilled: a reminder feature with no dedicated data structure or engine. The schedule is not a record but a line a user typed in an editor; the matching is not a parser but egrep applied to a generated pattern; the scheduling is not a daemon but cron starting calendar daily; the delivery is not a notification system but mail. calendar's own code is ~70 lines; the rest is egrep, cron, mail, sed, pipes, and a text file -- existing tools composed. Small tools, each doing one thing, joined by pipes -- rarely shown this purely.
ai_analysis.human_story: No database, no engine. Your schedule is a text file, the matching is grep, the clock is cron, the delivery is mail. calendar is barely 70 lines of glue. You can read your reminders, fix them, and check them by hand -- the opposite of a giant calendar app.

appraiser_note: calendar.sh が全体を組み上げる。/usr/lib/calendar(C プログラム)を走らせ今日/明日の egrep パターンを一時ファイルに吐き、egrep -f でそれをユーザの calendar ファイル(平文)に照合する。マッチ行=その日の予定が出る。Unix 哲学の蒸留だ: 専用のデータ構造もエンジンも持たないリマインダ。予定はレコードでなくユーザがエディタで書いた行、照合はパーサでなく生成パターンへの egrep 適用、スケジュールはデーモンでなく cron の日次起動、配達は通知システムでなく mail。calendar 自身のコードは約70行で、残りは egrep/cron/mail/sed/パイプ/テキストファイルという既存の道具の組み合わせ。小さな道具が一つのことに徹しパイプで繋がる——これほど純粋に現れた例は少ない。

text:
*)  sed 's/\([^:]*\):.*:\(.*\):[^:]*$/y=\2 z=\1/' /etc/passwd \
    | while read x; do
        eval $x                       # y=home  z=username
        if test -r $y/calendar; then
            egrep -f $tmp $y/calendar 2>/dev/null | mail $z   # mail the user
        fi
    done
esac

ai_analysis.technical_context: Called with an argument (typically by cron as root), calendar becomes the whole system's reminder courier. It carves /etc/passwd with sed to extract each user's home (y) and name (z), turning each passwd line into a shell assignment 'y=... z=...' that eval runs -- sed generating code that the shell evaluates, the same metaprogramming as the regex generation, but here truly executable. For each user with a readable ~/calendar it matches today's pattern and mails the hits. What these ~70 lines defined is the concept of a reminder service: a user records dated notes, the system checks them daily, and actively delivers the day's items. Your phone's 'today's agenda', a calendar app's pre-meeting alert -- all are the extension of the type calendar set in 1979. The implementation went from text+grep+mail to databases and push notifications, but the skeleton -- a dated note announced when its day arrives -- was first drawn here.
ai_analysis.human_story: Run for everyone, calendar walks /etc/passwd, finds each person's calendar file, and mails them their day. That is the reminder service in embryo: record a dated note, and have the machine tell you when the day comes. Your phone still does exactly this.

appraiser_note: 引数つきで(典型的には cron から root 権限で)呼ばれると calendar はシステム全員のリマインダ配達人になる。/etc/passwd を sed で削り各ユーザのホーム(y)とユーザ名(z)を取り出す——各 passwd 行を 'y=... z=...' というシェル代入文に変形し eval で実行する。sed が生成したコードをシェルが評価する、正規表現生成と同じメタプログラミングだが、こちらは真に実行可能。読める ~/calendar を持つ各ユーザに今日のパターンで照合しマッチを mail する。この約70行が定義したのはリマインダサービスという概念そのものだ: ユーザが日付つきの覚え書きを記録し、システムが毎日確認し、その日の項目を能動的に届ける。あなたのスマホの『今日の予定』、カレンダーアプリの会議前通知——すべて 1979年に calendar が確立した型の延長。実装はテキスト+grep+mail からDB+プッシュ通知へ変わったが、骨格——日付に紐づく覚え書きを、その日が来たら知らせる——はここで最初に描かれた。

ai_analysis.technical_context: dmesg sucks up system messages -- the kernel's diagnostics (device probes at boot, disk errors, the cry of panic). But where can a kernel write a message? A normal program printfs to the screen or a file, but the kernel IS the OS: 'screen' and 'file' are abstractions it provides, not ones it can rely on -- mid-boot or mid-error the filesystem may be unmounted, the terminal driver not yet up. The kernel's answer is a fixed-size ring buffer in its own memory: _msgbuf, MSGBUFS bytes, with _msgbufp as the write head that wraps to the front when full, overwriting the oldest. It just writes to memory -- no file, no device, a record that cannot fail. Showing it is left to someone else, later: dmesg.
