This 'kernel writes a fixed ring, another program drains it' design lives on 47 years later -- Linux's kernel ring buffer (/dev/kmsg, where printk writes) is the direct descendant, and even the command name dmesg is unchanged.
ai_analysis.human_story: Where does the operating system itself write an error, when the screen and the filesystem are the very things that might be broken? Into a fixed ring of memory it always owns. dmesg is the program that comes along later and reads that ring out.

appraiser_note: dmesg はシステムメッセージを吸い上げる——カーネルの診断(起動時のデバイス認識、ディスクエラー、panic の悲鳴)。だがカーネルはどこにメッセージを書けるのか。普通のプログラムは printf で画面やファイルに書くが、カーネルは OS そのもの: 『画面』も『ファイル』もカーネルが提供する抽象で、自身が頼れるものではない——起動の途中やエラーの最中、ファイルシステムは未マウントかも、端末ドライバはまだ動いていないかも。カーネルの答えは自分のメモリ内の固定リングバッファ: _msgbuf(MSGBUFS バイト)、_msgbufp が書込ヘッドで一杯なら先頭へ巻き戻り最古を上書きする。ただメモリに書くだけ——ファイルもデバイスも要らない、決して失敗しない記録。見せるのは後で別の誰か(dmesg)に任せる。この『カーネルが固定リングに書き別プログラムが吸い上げる』設計は47年後も生きている——Linux のカーネルリングバッファ(/dev/kmsg、printk の書込先)は直系の子孫で、dmesg というコマンド名すら不変。

text:
nlist(argc>2? argv[2]:"/unix", nl);       /* resolve symbol addresses from the kernel a.out */
mem = open("/dev/mem", 0);                /* open physical memory itself */
lseek(mem, (long)nl[0].n_value, 0);        /* seek to _msgbuf's address */
read(mem, msgbuf, MSGBUFS);                /* read the live kernel memory */
if (msgbufp < (char *)nl[0].n_value || msgbufp >= (char *)nl[0].n_value+MSGBUFS)
	done("Namelist mismatch\n");            /* out of range = /unix doesn't match the running kernel */

ai_analysis.technical_context: To peek into kernel memory you need two tools: knowing an address and reading it. nlist() supplies the address: given /unix -- the running kernel's own executable -- it reads that a.out's symbol table and resolves where _msgbuf and _msgbufp sit in memory, the same trick adb and prof used to bind names to addresses. The kernel is just an a.out; its symbol table says '_msgbuf lives at 0x...'. /dev/mem supplies the reading: a special device that exposes physical memory as a file, so lseek to the address and read the bytes there -- the live running kernel's memory, from an outside program. A wise sanity check follows: if the read msgbufp points outside _msgbuf's range, the supplied /unix disagrees with the running kernel (wrong or stale kernel file) and dmesg stops with 'Namelist mismatch'. The map (/unix) and the territory (/dev/mem) must agree. This nlist + /dev/mem idiom is what ps, pstat and adb all used to inspect the live kernel.
ai_analysis.human_story: dmesg reads the kernel's own RAM. It asks the kernel's symbol table 'where is _msgbuf?', then opens /dev/mem -- physical memory as a file -- and reads that exact address. If the map and the memory disagree, it refuses: namelist mismatch.

appraiser_note: カーネルメモリを覗くには二つの道具が要る——番地を知ることと、その番地を読むこと。nlist() が番地を供給する: /unix(動作中のカーネルそのものの実行ファイル)の a.out シンボル表を読み、_msgbuf と _msgbufp がメモリのどの番地にあるかを引き当てる——adb や prof が名前と番地を結んだのと同じ仕掛け。カーネルもただの a.out で、そのシンボル表が『_msgbuf は 0x... にある』と教える。/dev/mem が読み取りを供給する: 物理メモリをファイルに見せる特殊デバイスで、番地へ lseek してバイトを read——動作中のカーネルの生メモリを外のプログラムから。賢明な安全確認: 読んだ msgbufp が _msgbuf の範囲外なら、指定 /unix が動作カーネルと食い違う(別/古いカーネルファイル)ので 'Namelist mismatch' で止まる。地図(/unix)と現地(/dev/mem)が一致して初めて読める。この nlist + /dev/mem は ps/pstat/adb が動作カーネルを覗くのに使った常套手段。

ai_analysis.technical_context: A ring is circular but output is a line; dmesg must unroll the ring into one correctly-ordered stream. The key: _msgbufp (the write head) is also the oldest message's position -- once the ring is full, the slot the head is about to write holds the not-yet-overwritten oldest data. So dmesg reads from there, goes all the way around, and stops when it returns to the write head -- oldest to newest. The one-lap engine is if (mp == &msgbuf[MSGBUFS]) mp = msgbuf: when the pointer reaches the array end, wrap to the front. Those few lines are the heart of ring-buffer handling -- a linear array treated as a logical circle. mp++ steps, wraps at the end, until it catches msgbufp; the \0 of unused space is skipped by if(*mp). The same circular geometry as uucp's sliding window or vplot's block cycling.
ai_analysis.human_story: The buffer is a circle; the screen is a line. dmesg starts at the write head (which is also the oldest entry), walks once around -- wrapping from the end back to the front -- and stops when it meets the head again. A circle unrolled into the right order.

appraiser_note: リングは円環だが出力は一直線。dmesg は円環を正しい順序の一本の流れに展開する。鍵は _msgbufp(書込ヘッド)が最古メッセージの位置でもあること——リングが一杯になると、ヘッドが次に書く場所にはまだ上書きされていない最古データがある。だから dmesg はそこから読み始め、ぐるりと一周し、書込ヘッドに戻るまで読む——古いものから新しいものへ。一周を支えるのが if (mp == &msgbuf[MSGBUFS]) mp = msgbuf: ポインタが配列末尾に達したら先頭へ巻き戻す。この数行がリングバッファ操作の核心——線形配列を論理的な円環として扱う。mp++ で進め端で巻き戻し msgbufp に追いつくまで。未使用部分の \0 は if(*mp) で飛ばす。uucp のスライディングウィンドウや vplot のブロック巡回と同じ円環の幾何学。

ai_analysis.technical_context: dmesg's most refined part is incremental output. With - (sflg) it reads the previous run's ring contents from /usr/adm/msgbuf and compares them to the current ring. Why? To run dmesg periodically (cron) and accumulate kernel messages into a log file without re-logging the same lines. So it compares saved (omesg) and current byte by byte, finds the divergence point -- the start of messages new since last time -- and prints only from there, prefixing '...' to mean 'everything before was already reported'; then done() writes the current ring back to /usr/adm/msgbuf as the next baseline. This is log accumulation: a fixed ring loses old messages if left alone, but running dmesg - regularly skims the new ones before they are overwritten and persists them. Volatile ring to durable log -- the same 'only what is new since last read' idea as rsync's delta or log shippers, in 1979 via byte comparison. This two-stage 'kernel writes a volatile ring, a periodic drainer persists it' grows into syslog, and journald is its heir.
ai_analysis.human_story: Run dmesg - from cron and it only logs what is new: it remembers last time's ring, finds where the new messages begin, prints from there, and saves the new state. A volatile ring skimmed into a durable log before it overwrites itself -- the seed of syslog.

appraiser_note: dmesg の最も洗練された部分が増分出力。-(sflg) で前回のリング内容を /usr/adm/msgbuf から読み今回と比較する。なぜか: dmesg を定期的に(cron)走らせカーネルメッセージをログファイルに溜めたいが、毎回リング全体を出すと同じ行が重複する。そこで保存した前回(omesg)と現在を1文字ずつ突き合わせ、食い違った地点——前回以降に新しく書かれたメッセージの始まり——を見つけ、そこから先だけ出力し、先頭に '...' を付けて『ここより前は報告済み』と示す。そして done() が今回のリングを /usr/adm/msgbuf に書き戻し次回の基準にする。これはログの累積: 固定リングは放っておけば古いものを失うが、dmesg - を定期実行すれば上書き前に新着を拾い永続化できる。揮発するリングから永続するログへ——rsync の差分や log shipper の『前回読んだ位置から先だけ』と同じ発想を1979年に文字比較で。この『カーネルが揮発リングに書き定期的に吸い上げ永続ログにする』二段構えが syslog へ発展し、journald はその裔。

ai_analysis.technical_context: ac answers one question: how long was each user logged in. Where sa totals CPU time (process accounting), ac totals connect time (login accounting) -- the two faces of time-sharing billing. Its source is /usr/adm/wtmp, the login ledger: every login and logout appends a struct utmp (ut_name, ut_line=tty, ut_time) -- written by login as users come in and by init as terminals open and close. Like sa's /usr/adm/acct, it is a ledger piling up in /usr/adm. ac reads it record by record, validating ut_name (alnum + NUL-padded) and skipping garbage left by crashes. A refined touch: after reading the file, ac appends one synthetic record of its own -- empty name, line '~' (logout), time now -- so any session still open at the end of wtmp is closed off as 'logged out now'. The books must balance, so it pencils a closing entry.
ai_analysis.human_story: ac reads the login ledger -- wtmp, where every login and logout is stamped. To balance the books it even writes a fake 'logged out now' at the end, closing any session that was still open when the log ran out.

appraiser_note: ac は一つの問いに答える: 各ユーザがどれだけログインしていたか。sa が CPU 時間(プロセス会計)を集計するのに対し、ac は接続時間(ログイン会計)を集計する——タイムシェアリング課金の表と裏。元は /usr/adm/wtmp、ログインの台帳: ログイン/ログアウトのたびに struct utmp(ut_name/ut_line=tty/ut_time)が追記される——login がユーザを通すたび、init が端末を開け閉めするたびに書く。sa の /usr/adm/acct と同じ、/usr/adm に積もる会計台帳の一つ。ac は1件ずつ読み、ut_name(英数字+NUL詰め)を検証しクラッシュ由来のゴミを skip する。洗練された一手: ファイルを読み終えた後、ac は自分で擬似レコード(名前空・行 '~'(ログアウト)・時刻 now)を追加し、wtmp 末尾でまだ開いていたセッションを『今ログアウトした』ことにして締める。帳尻を合わせるため、締めの一筆を入れる。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Connect time needs login matched to logout: a user logs into tty03 at 14:00 and out at 16:00 -- pair those two records and you get 2 hours. ac's key is pairing per terminal. It keeps tbuf[] indexed by terminal (tty), each entry holding who is logged in (userp) and since when (ttime), taken from ut_line 'ttyNN' via (ut_line[3]-'0')*10+(ut_line[4]-'0'). On a login record it stamps that terminal with user+start; on the next record for the same terminal, update() adds now-start to that user's total. It is like matching open and close brackets: login '(' and logout ')'. But wtmp interleaves events from many terminals, so the terminal number is the bracket type -- one open bracket held per terminal -- letting ac stitch the tangled stream back into individual sessions. A logout (line '~', empty name) clears the terminal's userp: bracket closed.
ai_analysis.human_story: Login is an open bracket, logout a close. But many terminals' events are tangled together in wtmp, so ac uses the terminal number as the bracket type, keeping one open bracket per terminal, and pairs each login to its logout to measure the session.

appraiser_note: 接続時間はログインとログアウトの対応づけが要る: ユーザが tty03 に14時にログインし16時にログアウト——この2件を対にして2時間が出る。ac の鍵は端末ごとの対応づけ。tbuf[] を端末(tty)で索引し、各エントリが『今誰がログイン中か(userp)』『いつから(ttime)』を持つ。端末番号は ut_line 'ttyNN' から (ut_line[3]-'0')*10+(ut_line[4]-'0') で取り出す。ログインレコードでその端末にユーザ+開始時刻を刻み、同じ端末の次のレコードで update() が now-start をそのユーザの合計に足す。これは開き括弧と閉じ括弧の対応づけに似る: ログイン '('、ログアウト ')'。だが wtmp は複数端末のイベントが入り混じって流れるので、端末番号を括弧の種類として使い、端末ごとに1つの開き括弧を保持して、もつれた流れを個々のセッションに縫い合わせる。ログアウト(行 '~'、名前空)で端末の userp を空に——括弧が閉じた。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Connect time is just now - start, but that assumes the clock advances correctly and monotonically -- which breaks when someone runs date to reset it, jumping or rewinding time and wrecking the subtraction. ac guards against the unreliable clock twice. First, the | and } records: when the clock is changed, wtmp records the time just before (|) and just after (}); ac adds their difference (the jump amount) to every terminal's session start ttime, so each start follows the shift and now-start stays correct -- absorbing the discontinuity by moving the reference point. Second, the sanity guard: if a record's time goes backwards or jumps more than 1.5 days it resets the day calc, and update()'s if (t1>0 && t1 < 1.5*day) simply discards any computed session longer than 1.5 days or negative -- no one is logged in over a day straight, let alone for negative time. Like uucp expecting line noise and icheck expecting disk corruption, ac is built expecting the clock to lie, and keeps the books right anyway.
ai_analysis.human_story: Someone runs date and the clock jumps -- now every session length is wrong. ac watches for the before/after pair wtmp records around a clock change and shifts all the session starts to match, and it throws away any session that comes out negative or longer than a day and a half.

appraiser_note: 接続時間は now - start の引き算だが、それは時刻が正しく単調に進む前提に立つ——誰かが date で時計を直すと時刻が飛んだり巻き戻ったりして引き算が壊れる。ac は狂う時計に二重に備える。一つめが | と } のレコード: 時計が変更されると wtmp に変更直前の時刻(|)と直後の時刻(})が記録され、ac はその差(ジャンプ量)を全端末のセッション開始ttime に足す。こうして各開始時刻がずれに追随し now-start が正しさを保つ——基準点を動かして不連続を吸収する。二つめが妥当性の番人: レコードの時刻が逆行 or 1.5日超飛躍したら日付計算をリセットし、update() の if (t1>0 && t1 < 1.5*day) が1.5日超や負の計算結果を捨てる——誰も24時間以上ぶっ続けで(まして負の時間)繋がない。uucp が回線ノイズを、icheck がディスク破損を前提にしたように、ac は時計が嘘をつく前提で作られ、それでも帳尻を合わせる。

ai_analysis.technical_context: ac prints each user's connect time in hours and a grand total: -p per user, -d per day (newday/midnight compute the midnight boundary with timezone and DST), people args filter via among(). Seconds in utime become hours via /3600. Here ac and sa pair up. Time-sharing billing had two axes: CPU time -- how long a process actually used the machine (sa from /usr/adm/acct) -- and connect time -- how long a user was logged in (ac from /usr/adm/wtmp). Even with little CPU, holding a terminal ties up a line and memory, a real cost; so computing centers measured and billed both. sa counts work done, ac counts time present -- two ledgers, two programs, two lines on one user's bill. The connect-time idea is everywhere now: a cloud VM is billed for uptime regardless of CPU (connect time itself), SaaS sells monthly access, web analytics measure session duration, and last(1) reads the same wtmp to show who was logged in when. Not just how much you used, but how long you were attached -- ac is the prototype of that metering.
ai_analysis.human_story: sa measures the work you did; ac measures the time you were there. Two ledgers, two bills. And the 'time you were attached' is exactly what your cloud VM is billed for by uptime, what web analytics call session duration, what last still reads from wtmp.

appraiser_note: ac は各ユーザの接続時間(時間単位)と合計を出す: -p でユーザ別、-d で日別(newday/midnight がタイムゾーン・DST考慮で真夜中境界を計算)、people 引数で among() による絞り込み。秒の utime を /3600. で時間に直す。ここで ac と sa が対をなす。タイムシェアリング課金には二軸あった: CPU 時間——プロセスが実際に計算機を使った時間(sa が /usr/adm/acct から)——と接続時間——ユーザがログインしていた時間(ac が /usr/adm/wtmp から)。CPU をほとんど使わなくても端末を占有すれば回線とメモリを押さえ、立派なコスト。だから計算センターは両方を測って課金した。sa が働いた分を、ac が居た分を数える——二つの台帳、二つのプログラム、一人の請求書の別々の行。接続時間の発想は今や至る所に: クラウドVMは CPU 使用率に関わらず起動時間(=接続時間そのもの)で課金され、SaaS は月額でアクセス期間を売り、Web 解析はセッション時間を測り、last(1) は同じ wtmp を読んで誰がいつログインしていたかを見せる。何を使ったかだけでなく、どれだけ繋がっていたか——ac はその計測の祖型。

ai_analysis.technical_context: iostat measures I/O performance -- transfers per minute, ms per transfer, ms per seek, and the CPU's user/nice/system/idle split. But CPU usage and disk activity change moment to moment inside the kernel; how does an outside program know them? Same idiom as dmesg: read the kernel's own counters through /dev/kmem. _dk_busy/_dk_time (disk histogram), _dk_numb (transfers), _dk_wds (words), _tk_nin/_tk_nout (tty chars), _io_info (buffer cache) are plain global variables the kernel keeps updating, exposed through no API. iostat resolves their addresses with nlist(/unix) -- the running kernel's symbol table -- then lseeks and reads them from /dev/kmem, the kernel's virtual memory as a file (dmesg used /dev/mem, physical). This nlist + /dev/kmem combo is how ps, pstat, vmstat and adb all peeked into the live kernel: no dedicated monitoring interface, just a transparent kernel whose every counter is readable from outside -- until /proc and sysctl gave it a tidy window later.
ai_analysis.human_story: How do you watch a running kernel from outside? You ask its symbol table where each counter lives and read that address straight out of /dev/kmem. The kernel kept no monitoring API -- it just let you read its memory.

appraiser_note: iostat は I/O 性能を測る——毎分転送数・1転送あたりミリ秒・シーク ミリ秒、CPU の user/nice/system/idle 内訳。だが CPU 使用率もディスク稼働もカーネルの中で刻一刻変わる。外のプログラムがどう知るか? dmesg と同じ手: カーネルのカウンタを /dev/kmem 越しに読む。_dk_busy/_dk_time(ディスクhistogram)、_dk_numb(転送回数)、_dk_wds(ワード数)、_tk_nin/_tk_nout(端末文字数)、_io_info(バッファキャッシュ)はカーネルが更新し続けるただのグローバル変数で、API 公開はされない。iostat は nlist(/unix)＝動作カーネルのシンボル表で番地を解決し、/dev/kmem(カーネル仮想メモリをファイルに見せたもの。dmesg は物理の /dev/mem)を lseek+read する。この nlist + /dev/kmem は ps/pstat/vmstat/adb が動作カーネルを覗くのに使った手。専用の監視IFはなく、全カウンタが外から読める透明なカーネル——後に /proc や sysctl が整理した窓口を作るまで。

ai_analysis.technical_context: iostat's cleverest move is how etime[32] packs information into the bin index. Each clock tick the kernel increments exactly one bin, and that bin number encodes two things at once. Read the 0-31 index as five bits: the low three bits are which disks are spinning (bit0 RF, bit1 RK, bit2 RP); the high part (in groups of 8) is what the CPU is doing (0-7 user, 8-15 nice, 16-23 system, 24-31 idle). So 'CPU in system mode while RP spins' increments bin 16+4=20. One bin = one CPU-state x disk-busy combination. The encoding pays off because reads are bit operations: how busy was disk dn? sum bins where if(i & (1<<dn)); how much time in user? sum bins 0-7. A single 32-element array folds the joint distribution of 4 CPU states x 8 disk combinations, and any cross-section is a bitmask away. Same spirit as prof's PC sampling -- sample the state once per tick and count it -- but iostat folds two axes into one bin, capturing even the correlation (was the CPU in system busy while disks spun -- I/O wait or compute?) in just 32 counters.
ai_analysis.human_story: Thirty-two numbers hold everything: each clock tick the kernel bumps one bin whose number says both what the CPU was doing and which disks were turning. Want disk RP's busy time? Add the bins with that bit set. Two dimensions folded into one index, read back with a bitmask.

appraiser_note: iostat の最も巧妙な点は etime[32] のビン番号への情報の詰め方だ。クロック1tickごとにカーネルはちょうど1ビンを増やし、そのビン番号に二つの情報が同時に符号化される。0-31 を5ビットと見ると、下位3ビットがどのディスクが回っているか(bit0 RF/bit1 RK/bit2 RP)、上位(8単位)が CPU が何をしているか(0-7 user/8-15 nice/16-23 system/24-31 idle)。だから『CPU が system モードで RP が回る』なら 16+4=20 を増やす。一つのビン=CPU状態×ディスク稼働の組合せ。読み出しがビット演算なのが効く: ディスク dn の稼働は if(i & (1<<dn)) のビンを合計、user の割合はビン0-7を合計。32要素の配列に CPU状態4種×ディスク稼働2³=8通りの同時分布が畳まれ、任意の断面がビットマスク一つで切り出せる。prof の PC サンプリングと同思想——tickごとに状態を1回サンプリングして数える——だが iostat は二軸を一つのビンに畳み、相関(CPU が system で忙しい間ディスクは回っていたか＝I/O待ちか計算か)まで32カウンタで捉える。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Kernel counters are totals since boot. Reading _dk_numb once gives only the lifetime count; you want the rate -- how fast it is going now. iostat's answer is the universal one: sample twice and subtract. It computes current minus previous (s.etime[i] -= s1.etime[i]), which is the activity between the two reads, saves the current value in s1, sleeps the requested seconds, and loops. iostat 5 prints the I/O rate over each 5-second window. The first read is the since-boot total, so it is the second read onward that gives the true recent rate. This 'sample a cumulative counter twice and diff for the interval rate' is the basic pattern of system monitoring -- vmstat 1, iostat 5, sar, mpstat, and top/htop reading /proc/stat twice all do exactly this. The kernel never computes rates (expensive, and who needs which?); it just honestly increments totals, and the viewer derives the rate by subtraction. Record and interpret kept separate, like sa and dmesg.
ai_analysis.human_story: The kernel only counts totals. To see a rate, iostat reads the counter, waits, reads again, and subtracts -- the gap is what happened in between. Every monitor since, from vmstat to top, is this same read-wait-read-subtract loop.

appraiser_note: カーネルのカウンタは起動からの累積値だ。_dk_numb を一度読んでも総数しか分からない。欲しいのは率——今どれくらいの速さで進んでいるか。iostat の答えは普遍的: 二度測って差を取る。現在値−前回値(s.etime[i] -= s1.etime[i])が二回の読みの間の活動で、現在値を s1 に保存し、指定秒 sleep してループする。iostat 5 なら5秒窓ごとに I/O 率を出す。最初の読みは起動からの累積なので、二回目以降が本当の直近の率になる。この『累積カウンタを二度サンプリングして区間の率を差分で出す』はシステム監視の基本パターン——vmstat 1/iostat 5/sar/mpstat、/proc/stat を二度読む top/htop もまさにこれ。カーネルは率を計算しない(高価で誰が要るか不明)。ただ累積を正直に増やし、見る側が引き算で率を導く。記録と解釈の分離——sa や dmesg と同じ。

ai_analysis.technical_context: Raw tick counts mean nothing to a human; iostat's last job is to translate them into physical quantities -- milliseconds, transfers per minute. The key is xf[], a table of disk physical constants: RF takes 16.0us per word, RK 11.1us, RP06 2.48us, the hardware transfer speeds baked into code. From the histogram iostat gets total busy time f1, subtracts the pure transfer time (words x seconds-per-word) to leave seek time f5 -- the time the head spent moving rather than reading. So a disk's busy time splits into transfer (mspt) and seek (msps). Long seek time means scattered, random access; transfer-dominated means big sequential I/O. With -b, biostats() prints buffer-cache stats (reads, read-aheads, cache hits, writes) so you see the cache's effectiveness too. Turning raw counts into meaningful metrics via hardware physics is the essence of performance monitoring -- today's iostat %util, await, r/s and w/s, and top's CPU breakdown are all the descendants of this 1979 reading of 32 bins and disk constants.
ai_analysis.human_story: Busy is not enough: was the disk reading data or just moving its head? iostat subtracts the pure transfer time (words times the disk's microseconds-per-word) from the total busy time, and what is left is seek. Counts become milliseconds, the way every performance tool since reports them.

appraiser_note: 生のtick数は人間に意味をなさない。iostat の最後の仕事はそれを物理量——ミリ秒・毎分転送数——に翻訳することだ。鍵は xf[]、ディスクの物理定数表: RF はワードあたり16.0µ秒、RK 11.1µ秒、RP06 2.48µ秒、ハードの転送速度がコードに焼き込まれている。histogram から得た総稼働時間 f1 から、純転送時間(ワード数×ワードあたり秒)を引いて残りがシーク時間 f5——ヘッドが読み書きでなく移動に費やした時間。だからディスクの稼働時間が転送(mspt)とシーク(msps)に分かれる。シーク時間が長ければ散らばったランダムアクセス、転送支配なら大きな連続I/O。-b では biostats() がバッファキャッシュ統計(読み/先読み/キャッシュヒット/書き)を出しキャッシュの効きも分かる。生の数をハードの物理で意味ある指標に変えるのが性能監視の本質——今日の iostat %util/await/r/s/w/s、top の CPU 内訳はすべて、この1979年の32ビンとディスク定数の読み取りの子孫だ。
